title: Verify-and-hunt remediation ROUND 2 (2026-06-02): 25 calibrated findings (14 keep + 11 defense-in-depth)
description: Round-2 epic: the convergence re-hunt surfaced 26 new findings; ground-truth calibration kept 14 actionable + 11 defense-in-depth (1 dropped = already-deleted probe scratch). Dupes merged: idx10->1 (keyword-UDF), idx20->19 (precedence tier). Each child is self-contained.

title: Stop gating UDF detection on sqlparser keyword table — non-reserved Oracle names (purge/merge/refresh/...) fail-open SELECT to Safe
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs:386 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** A custom tool defined as PackageCall("billing.purge()") (or any UDF whose name collides with a non-reserved sqlparser keyword) loads at the ReadOnly privilege floor / classifies Safe instead of Guarded, so an agent can invoke a side-effecting (row-deleting) routine inside a SELECT with no step-up gate — a fail-open in the guard's safety spine that defeats R15.
**Evidence:** classifier.rs:385-388 (verbatim, current committed code):
  if let Token::Word(name) = toks[i - 1] {
      if name.keyword != Keyword::NoKeyword {
          continue; // a keyword like VALUES( / IN( etc.
      }
SELECT path classifier.rs:553-585: calls.is_empty() → all_proven vacuously true; UnknownOracle.statement_purity returns Unknown (purity.rs:99-101) so stmt_side_effecting=false; stmt_pure=true → DangerLevel::Safe / OperatingLevel::ReadOnly.
purity.rs:68-69: permits_safe() == matches!(self, Purity::ProvenReadOnly); purity.rs:78-80 UnknownOracle.routine_purity → Purity::Unknown (so a DETECTED keyword-named UDF would correctly be Guarded — the keyword skip is the sole cause).
Production caller: custom_tools.rs:302  ToolBody::PackageCall(c) => format!("SELECT {c} FROM dual"); comment lines 300-301 assert the intended invariant "fail-closed to Guarded — never silently Safe".
Empirical 
**Repro:** Trigger X→Y: input X = `SELECT billing.purge() FROM dual` (a UDF named after the non-reserved Oracle keyword PURGE), classified by Classifier::default() (the live UnknownOracle, since the real engine oracle binding is deferred per classifier.rs:566-571). Output Y = DangerLevel::Safe / OperatingLevel::ReadOnly, udf_calls empty. Expected: Guarded/ReadWrite (the control `billing.purge_old_rows()` correctly yields Guarded). Mechanism: tokenizer tags the `purge` Word token with a non-NoKeyword value (PURGE is in sqlparser 0.62 ALL_KEYWORDS), so the `if name.keyword != Keyword::NoKeyword { continue; }` at line 386 drops it from the candidate set before is_builtin_function is ever consulted; calls 
**Suggested fix:** Replace the blanket `if name.keyword != Keyword::NoKeyword { continue; }` at classifier.rs:386 with an explicit small skip-set of the genuinely clause-introducing words that can legally precede `(` but are never UDF calls (e.g. VALUES, IN, CASE — match the lowercased name.value against a tiny literal set), and let the existing `if !is_builtin_function(&fname)` filter handle real built-ins (COUNT/MAX/etc.). Every other ident( / schema.ident( becomes a UDF candidate routed through the oracle consult (defaulting to Guarded under UnknownOracle). Add a regression test asserting `SELECT billing.purge() FROM dual` plus the other keyword-collision names classify as Guarded/ReadWrite, mirroring select_calling_udf_is_guarded_not_safe.

### crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs:386 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** A side-effecting Oracle routine (e.g. PRAGMA AUTONOMOUS_TRANSACTION DML) whose name collides with any sqlparser keyword — billing.purge(), app.merge(), app.delete(), app.comment(), app.refresh(), etc. — is classified Safe/ReadOnly when called in a SELECT, so the guard never forces a step-up and the DML executes undetected; the agent/attacker controls the routine name, so this is a trivially-reachable bypass of the classifier's headline fail-closed contract.
**Evidence:** classifier.rs:386 (HEAD == disk, md5 da9448e971b074909c698d3f1ccb49d6 both): `if name.keyword != Keyword::NoKeyword { continue; }`. Then classify_statement (lines 553-584): `let calls = user_defined_calls(sql); ... let stmt_pure = (calls.is_empty() || all_proven) && !stmt_side_effecting; if stmt_pure { danger: Safe, ReadOnly }`. sqlparser-0.62.0/src/keywords.rs lines 117/238/323/407/504/591/635/822/844/1133/1134: ANALYZE, COMMENT, DELETE, EXPORT, IMPORT, LOAD, MERGE, PURGE, REFRESH, VALUE, VALUES are all Keyword variants. LIVE repro via real public API Classifier::default().classify on HEAD (temp integration test, since removed): `SELECT billing.purge() FROM dual` -> danger=Safe level=ReadOnly objects=[]; `app.comment()`/`app.merge(x)`/`app.refresh()`/`app.delete()` -> Safe; vs non-colliding `billing.purge_old_rows()` -> Guarded/ReadWrite objects=["purge_old_rows"] and `app.recalc(id)` -
**Repro:** Trigger: Classifier::default().classify("SELECT billing.purge() FROM dual") returns DangerLevel::Safe / OperatingLevel::ReadOnly with objects_affected=[], whereas the spec-required output is Guarded/ReadWrite. Mechanism: at line 386 the schema-qualified UDF `purge` is tokenized with name.keyword=PURGE != NoKeyword, so `continue` drops it before it is pushed to `calls`; calls.is_empty()==true makes all_proven vacuously true and routine_purity() is never invoked (a bound engine oracle cannot recover); query_base_objects yields only [dual], and statement_purity under the default UnknownOracle returns Unknown (not ProvenSideEffecting), so stmt_side_effecting==false; therefore stmt_pure==true => 
**Suggested fix:** In user_defined_calls() replace the blanket `if name.keyword != Keyword::NoKeyword { continue; }` skip with: (1) never skip a schema-qualified `schema.name(` form (SQL constructs like VALUES/IN/CAST/CASE are never schema-qualified, so a qualified keyword-named identifier is unambiguously a routine call); (2) for a bare `name(`, skip only the small allow-list of genuine SQL-construct keywords that can legally precede `(` and are never UDFs — VALUES, VALUE, IN, CAST, CASE, EXISTS, ALL, ANY, SOME, ARRAY — and treat every other keyword-colliding identifier (PURGE, MERGE, DELETE, COMMENT, ANALYZE, REFRESH, ...) as a candidate UDF that falls through to is_builtin_function(). This stays fail-closed (over-detection only adds Guarded). Add keyword-named variants (billing.purge(), app.comment(), app.merge()) to select_calling_udf_is_guarded_not_safe and adversarial_corpus.rs asserting Guarded. NOT

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit fcb67103bb), re-verified on main. Root cause: classifier.rs user_defined_calls() had a blanket `if name.keyword != Keyword::NoKeyword { continue; }` that fail-OPENed every keyword-colliding name (purge/merge/delete/comment/analyze/refresh/...) before the purity consult, so `SELECT billing.purge() FROM dual` -> Safe/ReadOnly. Fix: (1) a schema-qualified `schema.name(` is NEVER skipped (it is unambiguously a routine call — SQL constructs are never schema-qualified), closing the headline billing.purge() attack; (2) a bare `name(` is skipped only when its keyword is a structural/clause keyword (AS/IN/VALUES/OVER/OR/JOIN/...) that

title: Merge `=` into the relational precedence tier so `<=`/`>=`/`!=` aren't mis-split on the embedded `=`
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/expr.rs:290 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** When a function call or tainted local sits on the LHS of a `<=`, `>=`, or `!=` comparison (e.g. `IF compute_total(x) <= limit`, `IF v_count >= p_limit`), the LHS is corrupted into an Expr::Raw node and silently dropped, producing Calls-edge false negatives in the dependency graph and intra-proc taint false negatives in the SAST engine. It under-reports (misses edges/flows); it does not fabricate a safe verdict on a dangerous sink.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-ir/src/expr.rs:287-295 precedence table puts `=` in its own tier BEFORE the relational tier:
    let precedence: &[&[&str]] = &[
        &["OR"], &["AND"], &["="],
        &["<>", "!=", "<=", ">=", "<", ">"], &["||"], ...];
recognise_top_level_binary iterates tiers in order (expr.rs:297) and returns on first match; find_top_level_op scans left-to-right and matches the bare `=` byte (single-char op, alpha word-boundary guard at expr.rs:375-387 does not apply). Downstream blindness confirmed: collect_calls (calls.rs:207-225) only descends Call/Binary/Unary, Raw hits `_ => {}`; collect_expr_flow (flow_intra.rs:183-281) drops Raw at `_ => {}`.

Empirical probe (compiled against the crate, `cargo test -p plsql-ir`, output via --nocapture):
        a <= b  =>  Binary { op: "=", lhs: Raw { text: "a <", reason: UnrecognizedShape }, rhs: Name(b) }
   x >= p_user  =>  Binary { op: "="
**Repro:** Trigger X -> wrong output Y: lower_expression("a <= b") yields Binary{op:"=", lhs: Raw{text:"a <"}, rhs: Name(b)} instead of Binary{op:"<=", lhs: Name(a), rhs: Name(b)}. Concretely, extract_call_sites on body `BEGIN IF compute_total(x) <= 10 THEN log_it('y'); END IF; END;` returns ["log_it"] (compute_total dropped); the identical body with `<` returns ["compute_total","log_it"]. Affects exactly `<=`, `>=`, `!=`; `<>`, `<`, `>`, `=` are unaffected.
**Suggested fix:** Replace the two tiers `&["="]` and `&["<>", "!=", "<=", ">=", "<", ">"]` (expr.rs:290-291) with a single tier `&["<>", "!=", "<=", ">=", "=", "<", ">"]`. Multi-char ops stay ahead of single-char ones, so scanning `a <= b` reaches the `<` byte first and the tier tries `<>`, `!=`, then `<=` (match) — splitting correctly into lhs="a"/rhs="b". `=`, `<`, `>` continue to work. Add regression tests asserting lower_expression of "a <= b"/"a >= b"/"a != b" produce Binary{op:"<="/">="/"!="} with both operands lowered (not Raw), plus an extract_call_sites test that `IF f(x) <= 10 THEN` yields a call site for f.

### crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:803 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** lower_expression_text mislabels every top-level <=, >=, != comparison as op "=" with a corrupted LHS slice (e.g. "a <"); today the only callers are the fuzz target and the crate's own tests (the MCP production path uses lower_source, which never reaches this function), so no shipping analysis output is corrupted yet, but the pub API silently violates its documented shape-correct contract and is a latent trap for any future consumer that reads Binary.op/lhs_text.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:800-808 tiers array has `&["="]` (line 803) BEFORE `&["<>", "!=", "<=", ">=", "<", ">"]` (line 804). split_top_level_bin (lines 866-922) is a position-driven left-to-right byte scan that tests each op of the current tier at every depth-0 byte. Empirical run of the REAL function via a throwaway integration test (since removed):
        a <= b => Binary { op: "=", lhs_text: "a <", rhs_text: "b", ... }
        a >= b => Binary { op: "=", lhs_text: "a >", rhs_text: "b", ... }
        a != b => Binary { op: "=", lhs_text: "a !", rhs_text: "b", ... }
        a <> b => Binary { op: "<>", lhs_text: "a", rhs_text: "b", ... }  (correct, no '=' byte)
         a < b => Binary { op: "<", lhs_text: "a", rhs_text: "b", ... }   (correct)
         a = b => Binary { op: "=", lhs_text: "a", rhs_text: "b", ... }   (correct)
  x <= y AND z => Binary { op: "AND", lhs
**Repro:** Trigger: lower_expression_text("a <= b", FileId::new(1), 0) returns AstExpr::Binary { op: "=", lhs_text: "a <", rhs_text: "b" } instead of op:"<=", lhs:"a". Mechanism: the " OR "/" AND " tiers return None on a bare comparison, then the &["="] tier (line 803) scans left-to-right and matches the '=' byte at index 3 of "a <= b" before the relational tier (line 804) is ever reached. Same bug for ">=" and "!=" (both contain '='); "<>", "<", ">", "=" are unaffected. Nesting under a lower-precedence operator (x <= y AND z) splits on AND at this level so the corruption only surfaces if "x <= y" is later re-lowered. NOT "every such expression in production": currently no production code path calls th
**Suggested fix:** Split the relational tier so the 2-char ops precede bare '='/'<'/'>': replace lines 803-804 `&["="], &["<>", "!=", "<=", ">=", "<", ">"],` with `&["<>", "!=", "<=", ">="], &["=", "<", ">"],`. This makes the 2-char forms match whole before the single-char tier can break them. Apply the identical reordering to the sibling at crates/plsql-ir/src/expr.rs:287-295 (where it is worse, since operands ARE recursively lowered there per expr.rs:300). Add regression tests asserting lower_expression_text("a <= b") yields Binary{op:"<=", lhs:"a", rhs:"b"} (and >=, !=) in both crates.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit a9ab5913d6), re-verified on main. Merged the `=` tier into the relational tier at BOTH sites. expr.rs:287-295 and lower/mod.rs:800-808 now use a single tier &["<>", "!=", "<=", ">=", "=", "<", ">"] (multi-char ops first). Root cause: a separate higher &["="] tier matched the embedded `=` byte in <=/>=/!= before the relational tier, corrupting the LHS into Raw/a-corrupted-slice and op into `=`, causing Calls-edge and intra-proc taint false negatives. Within-tier left-to-right matching reaches the `<`/`>`/`!` byte first and matches the 2-char op whole.

title: Make G2 roundtrip_check (and G8 residue_check) fail closed on unreadable collected files instead of vacuous-passing over 0 verified inputs
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-accretion/src/gate.rs:419 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=medium
**Impact:** If the G2 round-trip gate is pointed at a corpus where every collected source file is unreadable (non-UTF-8 legacy PL/SQL, or a file removed/chmod'd between listing and read), it reports "lossless round-trip 100%" and exits 0 — a vacuous PASS over zero verified files, directly violating the stage's own fail-closed invariant. Under the default repo-controlled ASCII corpus it never triggers, so impact is latent.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-accretion/src/gate.rs:392-427 — the empty-files guard (line 392 `if files.is_empty()`) only fails-closed for files-NOT-FOUND; the loop body at line 404 `let Ok(src) = std::fs::read_to_string(f) else { continue; };` silently skips unreadable files, `checked` (line 417) counts only successful reads, and there is NO post-loop `if checked == 0` re-check before the unconditional `Ok(format!("lossless round-trip 100% over {checked} inputs ({} corpus + {} prior MinFixtures)", collect_sources(corpus_dir).len(), ...))` at line 419-427 (the parenthetical re-counts files FOUND, not files VERIFIED).
Docstring contract it violates, lines 393-394: "A round-trip stage with zero inputs cannot make a real claim — fail-closed (never a vacuous pass)."
Same continue-on-read-error pattern confirmed in residue_check at line 592-594.
Crate builds clean: `cargo build -p plsql-accretion --bin usr-ga
**Repro:** TRIGGER (confirmed live): mkdir a corpus dir, write one file with a gate ext and non-UTF-8 bytes: `printf '\xff\xfe\x00\x80 SELECT 1 FROM dual;' > corpus/legacy.sql`; run `usr-gate-rs roundtrip <corpus> <nonexistent-fixtures>`. Observed: stdout `lossless round-trip 100% over 0 inputs (1 corpus + 0 prior MinFixtures)`, exit 0 — vacuous PASS. TEMPERING (why defense-in-depth not keep): the default corpus `corpus/synthetic/l1` and the entire committed `corpus/` tree are ASCII/UTF-8 (verified with file(1)), so under normal operation collected files always read successfully and checked==files.len(); the bug only manifests under a non-default USR_GATE_CORPUS pointed at non-UTF-8 files or a TOCTOU r
**Suggested fix:** In roundtrip_check, treat an unreadable collected file as a hard gate failure (it is a found input the stage cannot verify): replace `let Ok(src) = std::fs::read_to_string(f) else { continue; };` with `let src = std::fs::read_to_string(f).map_err(|e| GateError::Io(format!("round-trip input {} could not be read ({e}) — cannot prove lossless over a file that exists; fail-closed", f.display())))?;`. As defense-in-depth also add a post-loop `if checked == 0 { return Err(GateError::Io("0 round-trip inputs verified — vacuous claim refused".into())); }` and make the evidence line report the verified `checked` count consistently (carry separate verified-corpus/verified-fixture counters rather than re-calling collect_sources(...).len()). Apply the identical read-failure-fails-closed treatment to residue_check at line 592 so an unreadable fixture fails the privacy stage rather than being silently 

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 7450e1f63a), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix in crates/plsql-accretion/src/gate.rs. roundtrip_check: replaced the silent `let Ok(src) = std::fs::read_to_string(f) else { continue; }` with a hard GateError::Io failure naming the unreadable file (a found input the stage cannot verify must not vacuous-pass); added separate verified corpus/fixture counters so the evidence line reports the VERIFIED split instead of re-calling collect_sources().len() (fixes the self-inconsistent 'over 0 inputs (1 corpus + ...)' line); added a post-loop `if checked == 0` vacuous-claim guard. residue_check: an unreadable fixture now fails closed a

title: Pass case-preserving connect string to wallet_param in ADB descriptor branch (oci.rs:168)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-db/src/oci.rs:168 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** None today: the case-folding bug is in a public API (validate_adb_connect_string) that has no production caller, so no operator-visible failure occurs. If a future caller wired AdbConnectInfo.wallet_location into wallet discovery, a descriptor connect-string containing an uppercase path segment (e.g. wallet_location=/home/Oracle/wallet) would resolve to /home/oracle/wallet and mTLS auto-login would fail on case-sensitive Linux while the tcps:// form of the same wallet still worked.
**Evidence:** crates/oraclemcp-db/src/oci.rs:156 `let lower = t.to_ascii_lowercase();`
oci.rs:168 (descriptor branch) `wallet_location: wallet_param(&lower),`  <- case-folded input
oci.rs:178 (tcps:// branch)   `wallet_location: wallet_param(t),`        <- case-preserving input
oci.rs:208-214 wallet_param: `let idx = s.to_ascii_lowercase().find(needle)? + needle.len(); let rest = &s[idx..]; ...` -> lowercases ONLY for find(), but slices the VALUE out of the passed-in string, so &lower yields a folded path. The asymmetry + folding are exactly as claimed.
Workspace grep (rg 'validate_adb_connect_string|AdbConnectInfo|wallet_param' crates/): only hits are lib.rs:50-51 (re-export) and oci.rs:138/151/166/168/176/178/193/208 (definitions) plus oci.rs:321,328,334,341,345,349 — all six call sites sit inside `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` which begins at oci.rs:273.
Cross-crate grep (rg 'AdbConnectInfo|validate_adb'
**Repro:** Disproof of live impact: there is no production code path from AdbConnectInfo.wallet_location to wallet discovery. The defect would only manifest if validate_adb_connect_string were called and its wallet_location consumed; today the sole callers are unit tests (oci.rs lines 321-349, all under #[cfg(test)] mod tests at line 273), and connection.rs:81-99 sources the real wallet dir from opts.wallet_location instead. Latent trigger (would-be repro if wired up): validate_adb_connect_string("(description=(address=(protocol=tcps)(host=h)(port=1522))(connect_data=(service_name=svc))(wallet_location=/home/Oracle/wallet))").unwrap().wallet_location == Some("/home/oracle/wallet") (folded) instead of "
**Suggested fix:** Change oci.rs:168 from `wallet_location: wallet_param(&lower),` to `wallet_location: wallet_param(t),`, matching the sibling tcps:// branch at line 178. wallet_param already lowercases internally (line 210) only to locate the needle, so case-insensitive matching of `wallet_location=` is preserved while the extracted path value keeps its original case. Add a regression test (the existing descriptor test at oci.rs:328 omits wallet_location and asserts nothing on it): validate a descriptor containing wallet_location=/home/Oracle/wallet and assert i.wallet_location.as_deref() == Some("/home/Oracle/wallet"). Defense-in-depth: fix it before any caller wires AdbConnectInfo.wallet_location into wallet discovery.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 1c93a3299e), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix in crates/oraclemcp-db/src/oci.rs descriptor branch: changed wallet_location: wallet_param(&lower) to wallet_param(t) so the extracted path keeps its original case (case-sensitive Linux mTLS auto-login), matching the sibling tcps:// branch. wallet_param still lowercases internally only to locate the wallet_location= needle, so case-insensitive matching of the key is preserved. Regression test descriptor_wallet_location_preserves_case validates a descriptor with wallet_location=/home/Oracle/Wallet and asserts the value round-trips with mixed case; it FAILED before the fix (got fo

title: Make ADB descriptor TLS check and wallet_location parse whitespace/quote-tolerant in oraclemcp-db oci.rs
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-db/src/oci.rs:160 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** An operator who supplies a hand-written or tooling-generated full Oracle Net ADB descriptor with conventional spacing — (DESCRIPTION = (ADDRESS = (PROTOCOL = TCPS)...)) — has their legitimate mTLS connection profile rejected at config-load with "ADB descriptor must use PROTOCOL=TCPS (TLS)", and a quoted wallet_location path containing a space is silently truncated to the first space. Both are fail-closed availability/robustness defects (never a plaintext-accept security hole); currently latent because the validator has no live caller.
**Evidence:** oci.rs:158-164 (verbatim):
  if lower.starts_with("(description") || lower.contains("(address") {
      let uses_tls = lower.contains("protocol=tcps");
      if !uses_tls { return Err(OciError::InvalidAdbConnectString("ADB descriptor must use PROTOCOL=TCPS (TLS)".to_owned())); }
Only preprocessing: t = s.trim() (152), lower = t.to_ascii_lowercase() (156) — internal whitespace preserved.
oci.rs:212 (verbatim): let end = rest.find(['&', ')', '?', ' ']).unwrap_or(rest.len());  // space is a terminator

Standalone repro (rustc -O, exact logic copied from oci.rs):
  spaced descriptor: is_desc=true uses_tls=false -> REJECTED as non-TLS
  compact descriptor: is_desc=true uses_tls=true -> accepted
  wallet_param quoted-with-space -> "/opt/My"
Fail-closed proof:
  plaintext "(DESCRIPTION = (ADDRESS = (PROTOCOL = TCP)...))": is_desc=true uses_tls=false -> REJECTED (correct)
  plaintext "(descripti
**Repro:** Trigger 1 (TLS over-rejection): validate_adb_connect_string("(DESCRIPTION = (ADDRESS = (PROTOCOL = TCPS)(HOST=adb)(PORT=1522)))") returns Err(InvalidAdbConnectString("ADB descriptor must use PROTOCOL=TCPS (TLS)")) — proven above: is_desc=true, uses_tls=false. The same string with no spaces is accepted, proving spacing is the sole cause.
Trigger 2 (wallet truncation): wallet_param on "...?wallet_location=\"/opt/My Wallet/dir\"" returns "/opt/My" instead of "/opt/My Wallet/dir" — the ' ' terminator at line 212 fires before trim_matches strips the quotes.
Disproof of any security angle: a plaintext descriptor (spaced "PROTOCOL = TCP" or compact "protocol=tcp") still yields uses_tls=false and is
**Suggested fix:** Make the TLS check whitespace-insensitive: compute let compact: String = lower.chars().filter(|c| !c.is_whitespace()).collect(); then test compact.contains("protocol=tcps") (and use compact.contains("protocol=tcp") to emit the precise plaintext-rejection message). For wallet_param (line 212), make it quote-aware: if the char after wallet_location= is a quote, read to the matching closing quote (allowing interior spaces); otherwise terminate on ['&', ')', '?'] and whitespace. Add tests: spaced (PROTOCOL = TCPS) accepted as TLS, spaced (PROTOCOL = TCP) rejected as plaintext, and a quoted wallet_location path with a space round-tripping intact.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 1c93a3299e), re-verified on main. Two root-cause fixes in crates/oraclemcp-db/src/oci.rs. (1) TLS check made whitespace-insensitive: build a whitespace-stripped 'compact' copy of 'lower' and test compact.contains("protocol=tcps") so conventional Oracle Net spacing (PROTOCOL = TCPS) is accepted as TLS while spaced/compact plaintext (PROTOCOL = TCP) is still rejected fail-closed. (2) wallet_param made quote-aware: if the value after wallet_location= begins with a quote, read to the matching closing quote (interior spaces preserved); otherwise terminate on ['&',')','?'] or whitespace as before. Regression tests spaced_descriptor_

title: Coalesce Oracle doubled-quote escape ('') in extract_execute_immediate walker
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs:203 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** An operator inspecting the `dynamic_sql_evidence` MCP tool output for a statement containing an Oracle doubled-quote literal (e.g. EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'UPDATE t SET note = ''done'' WHERE id = 5') sees the literal's inner text ("done") wrongly listed as a candidate_object and the single statement split into 3 fragments. No rule, fact, dependency edge, or confidence/opacity verdict is affected — it is purely cosmetic noise in an already-loose informational field. Not an injection fail-open.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs:203-230 — `for c in body.chars()` with `if c == '\'' { if in_string { fragments.push(...); in_string=false } else { ...; in_string=true } continue }`: every quote unconditionally toggles in_string, no peek for doubled ''.
Standalone trace of the exact walker on `'UPDATE t SET note = ''done'' WHERE id = 5'` -> `fragments = ["UPDATE t SET note = ", "done", " WHERE id = 5"]`, `has <expr> placeholder = false` (confirming `''` parity stays even so no <expr> leaks).
`rg '\.candidate_objects' crates/` -> only dynamic_sql.rs:384 and :475, BOTH inside #[cfg(test)] — zero production reads.
Confidence scorer dynamic_sql_confidence.rs:60 keys only on `f.contains("<expr>")`, never on fragment count, so the 3-way split does not change the verdict.
Fact emitter plsql-ir/src/fact_emit.rs:129 mints `FactPayload::DynamicSqlEvidence { site }` — carries ONLY site; fra
**Repro:** Trigger (proven): input body `'UPDATE t SET note = ''done'' WHERE id = 5'` -> Y = fragments ["UPDATE t SET note = ", "done", " WHERE id = 5"] and candidate_objects includes spurious {object:"done"}. Disproof of severity/escalation: the security verdict path is independent — SEC001 (plsql-sast/src/rules.rs) reads only `site` from the fact and re-derives taint/string-shape from ctx.flow (IR flow analysis), and candidate_objects/fragments are never persisted to the fact store (fact_emit.rs:129). Because a correctly-doubled '' is an even number of quote toggles, in_string parity is preserved, no <expr> placeholder is emitted, and opacity_reason stays LiteralOnly with High confidence — so confide
**Suggested fix:** Replace the plain `for c in body.chars()` with a peekable iterator and, in the `if in_string` arm of the `c == '\''` branch, check `if chars.peek() == Some(&'\'') { chars.next(); current_lit.push('\''); continue; }` before closing the fragment — so a doubled '' is treated as one embedded literal quote rather than a close/reopen. This yields the single correct fragment "UPDATE t SET note = 'done' WHERE id = 5" and drops the spurious `done` candidate. Add a regression test asserting one fragment, OpacityReason::LiteralOnly, and no candidate_object equal to the escaped literal content. Low priority: cosmetic fidelity of an informational field with no downstream consumer.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 9c85b97d3f), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix in crates/plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs. Two coordinated changes: (1) the char walker in extract_execute_immediate now uses a peekable iterator and, in the in_string arm of the c=='\'' branch, peeks for a doubled '' — consuming the second quote and pushing one literal ' into current_lit instead of closing/reopening the string. This keeps an Oracle doubled-quote literal (e.g. 'UPDATE t SET note = ''done'' WHERE id = 5') as ONE fragment with OpacityReason::LiteralOnly. (2) Discovered while implementing: even with the walker fixed, candidate_objects_from_fragments still tokenize

title: disambiguate_overloads Pass-2 ordinal can collide with a unique sibling's literal suffix (latent; fn currently unwired)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-bindgen/src/overload.rs:106 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** None today: disambiguate_overloads has no production caller, so its buggy collision output never reaches the emitter. The emitter writes `pub fn {}` straight from RoutineBinding.name (emit.rs:148) and never invokes the disambiguation pass. The bug is a latent correctness gap in a public API seam exercised only by tests; it would become a real compile-breaking defect only if/when the emitter is wired to consume this function's output.
**Evidence:** Confirmed the logic bug against the REAL crate function. Injected test into overload.rs (then reverted; git status clean, 341 lines):
  TMP_NAMES = ["proc_x_1", "proc_x_2", "proc_x_2"]
  TMP_UNIQUE = 2 of 3
  assertion failed: expected 3 unique, got ["proc_x_1", "proc_x_2", "proc_x_2"]
  test overload::tests::TMP_ordinal_collides_with_literal_sibling_suffix ... FAILED

Root cause at overload.rs:99-110 — counts map built only from original candidates (lines 99-102); line 106 `format!("{name}_{}", i + 1)` never checks collision with a unique sibling. Standalone trace: groups=[["x"],["x"],["x_2"]], shared_prefix_len=0, suffixes=["x","x","x_2"], candidates=["proc_x","proc_x","proc_x_2"], counts={proc_x:2,proc_x_2:1} -> OUT=["proc_x_1","proc_x_2","proc_x_2"].

NO PRODUCTION CALLER — `rg disambiguate_overloads crates/` returns only: the definition (overload.rs:59), the re-export `pub use overl
**Repro:** TRIGGER (logic bug, real): disambiguate_overloads(&[rb("proc",["x"]), rb("proc",["x"]), rb("proc",["x_2"])]) returns ["proc_x_1","proc_x_2","proc_x_2"] (index 1 and 2 collide) — verified by direct execution of the actual function. DISPROOF of the claimed compile-failure impact: the output of disambiguate_overloads is never consumed by emit_wrappers/emit_routine, which generate `pub fn {}` from routine.name (emit.rs:148) without ever calling the disambiguation pass. Whole-workspace `rg` shows the only non-test reference is the lib.rs:34 re-export. Therefore the duplicate name cannot appear in any generated binding file today; no rustc E0428 is reachable through the current pipeline.
**Suggested fix:** Make Pass-2 enforce global uniqueness with a `seen: HashSet<String>` instead of consulting only the original candidate multiset: for each candidate, take `{base}_{i+1}` when its original count > 1 else the base, then `while !seen.insert(name) { escalate }`. Add a regression test asserting `[proc(x), proc(x), proc(x_2)]` yields 3 pairwise-distinct names. Separately (more important than the suffixing nuance): when the emitter is wired to actually consume this output, route emit_routine's `pub fn {}` name through the disambiguated Vec — otherwise the function remains dead and overloaded packages still emit duplicate `pub fn` names regardless of this fix.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 9ac2e4992c), re-verified on main. Defense-in-depth latent bug in disambiguate_overloads (overload.rs Pass-2). Root cause: Pass-2 consulted only the original per-group candidate multiset (counts) and never checked whether an emitted name collided with a unique sibling's literal suffix, so [proc(x),proc(x),proc(x_2)] produced ["proc_x_1","proc_x_2","proc_x_2"] (index 1 and 2 collide). Fixed as part of the shared root-cause rewrite (see ajm2.21): every assignment is now reserved through a deterministic global claim() probe, yielding ["proc_x_1","proc_x_2","proc_x_2_2"] (3 unique). Note: the bead correctly observes the function ha

title: Strip Oracle comments before FOR/UPDATE token scan in has_for_update_lock
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-mcp/src/query.rs:406 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** An agent without enable_writes can acquire an uncommitted Oracle row lock (FOR UPDATE) through the read-only `query` tool by inserting an Oracle comment between FOR and UPDATE, evading the documented PLSQL-MCP-SEC-2 row-lock gate; impact is bounded to a transient, non-mutating lock that releases on connection rollback/close.
**Evidence:** query.rs:406-421 has_for_update_lock() splits on non-identifier chars and requires FOR and UPDATE to be ADJACENT tokens: line 416 `if tok == "FOR" && tokens.clone().next() == Some("UPDATE")`. Standalone reproduction of the exact loop (rustc -O /tmp/repro_for_update.rs):
  plain FOR UPDATE             has_for_update_lock = true
  block-comment-with-word      has_for_update_lock = false   <-- bypass (SELECT * FROM t FOR/* x */UPDATE)
  block-comment-spaced         has_for_update_lock = false   <-- bypass (FOR /* x */ UPDATE)
  line-comment                 has_for_update_lock = false   <-- bypass (FOR -- c\nUPDATE)
  empty-block-comment          has_for_update_lock = true    (FOR/**/UPDATE caught: no word token)
  newline                      has_for_update_lock = true
run_query (query.rs:80) gates solely on is_read_only_sql then passes sql verbatim to conn.query_rows (line 85); dispatch.rs
**Repro:** Trigger: call run_query with sql = "SELECT dummy FROM dual FOR/* x */UPDATE" and no enable_writes token. is_read_only_sql returns true (has_for_update_lock returns false because the comment word `x` becomes an intervening token, breaking FOR/UPDATE adjacency), so run_query forwards the verbatim text to Oracle. Oracle treats /* */ and -- comments as whitespace-equivalent token separators, so it parses as a valid FOR UPDATE clause and opens a transaction holding a TX row lock — a write-bearing operation the module says must route through enable_writes. The word-less form FOR/**/UPDATE is still caught (empty comment yields no token), confirming the bypass is specifically the non-empty-comment c
**Suggested fix:** Add a literal-naive comment-stripping pass (collapse `/* ... */` and `-- ... \n` to a single space) at the top of has_for_update_lock before tokenizing, matching Oracle's lexer where comments are whitespace-equivalent. is_read_only_sql already strips leading block comments (lines 369-375), so the module is comment-aware elsewhere; this just extends that awareness to the row-lock scan. Add regression cases to read_only_predicate_rejects_for_update_with_non_space_whitespace covering `FOR/* x */UPDATE`, `FOR /* x */ UPDATE`, and `FOR -- c\nUPDATE`. The claim's suggestion to mirror the fix in plsql-cicd/src/inspector.rs is unnecessary — no FOR-UPDATE predicate exists there.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 089402ae49), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix: has_for_update_lock now scans a comment-stripped copy of the SQL (new strip_sql_comments helper) so an Oracle comment spliced between FOR and UPDATE (FOR/* x */UPDATE, FOR /* x */ UPDATE, FOR -- c\nUPDATE) no longer breaks the FOR/UPDATE token adjacency and evades the PLSQL-MCP-SEC-2 row-lock gate. This mirrors Oracle's lexer where comments are whitespace-equivalent, and matches the comment-awareness is_read_only_sql already applies to the leading token. The stripper is string-literal aware (single-quote with '' doubled-escape handling, double-quote identifiers) so a /* or -- s

title: Wire doctor artifact-read gate to schema_compatibility (dead forward-compat tier + inaccurate/contradictory schema-version docs)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-engine/src/main.rs:440 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** None observable today. The doctor gate fails closed (rejects, never mis-parses) on a version mismatch, the AnalysisRun artifact is a transient same-build analyze->doctor handoff (architecture doc pins it at ">= 1.1.0", no cross-version migration in the tree), and the documented one-command remedy is to regenerate. The only concrete effect is that a hypothetical future same-major higher-minor artifact would be rejected by doctor even though is_readable_by() says it is readable — i.e. the additive-minor tolerance the crate documents and unit-tests is enforced nowhere in production.
**Evidence:** matches_schema is exact-equality (crates/plsql-output/src/lib.rs:86-88): `self.schema_id == schema.id && self.schema_version == schema.version`. Doctor gate (crates/plsql-engine/src/main.rs:440): `if !envelope.matches_schema(ANALYSIS_RUN_SCHEMA) { return Err(CliError::IncompatibleSchema ... }`. The permissive helpers (crates/plsql-engine/src/lib.rs:472-503: schema_compatibility / compatibility_with / is_readable_by, returning ForwardCompatible for same-major higher-minor) have ZERO production callers — `rg 'schema_compatibility|is_readable_by|compatibility_with' crates/` returns only their definitions, the lib.rs:1571-1633 test module, and a doc-comment mention in plsql-store/src/protocol.rs:36. main.rs:434/440 is the ONLY production site deserializing RobotJsonEnvelope<AnalysisRun> from file/stdin; every other matches_schema hit is inside #[cfg(test)]. Test `manifest_helpers_route_throu
**Repro:** Disproof of the claim's headline repro: a genuine 1.0.0 AnalysisRun JSON cannot reach the IncompatibleSchema gate. The 1.1.0 additive CompletenessReport fields (crates/plsql-core/src/lib.rs:741-752: diagnostics_total, objects_unrecognized, objects_with_extracted_semantics, extracted_semantics_ratio, posture) are NOT #[serde(default)], so a 1.0.0 JSON lacking them fails at serde_json::from_str (main.rs:434, CliError::ParseArtifact) before line 440 is ever evaluated. The remaining real (but latent) trigger: construct an envelope with schema_id "plsql.engine.analysis_run" and schema_version {1,2,0} carrying all current required fields; is_readable_by(1.1.0) returns true (ForwardCompatible) yet 
**Suggested fix:** Treat as a low-priority consistency cleanup, not a correctness fix. (1) Either route the doctor gate (main.rs:440) through schema_compatibility(envelope.schema_version, ANALYSIS_RUN_SCHEMA.version) != Incompatible so the documented additive-minor tolerance is actually enforced, OR consciously delete the dead is_readable_by/schema_compatibility/compatibility_with helpers + ForwardCompatible variant and commit to the strict regenerate-on-mismatch policy. Pick one — do not keep a documented+tested policy that production ignores. (2) Correct the inaccurate doc comment at lib.rs:198-200 (no production consumer calls is_readable_by/schema_compatibility; only doctor reads the serialized envelope, via exact-match matches_schema). (3) Reconcile docs/components/lineage.md:280-284 — items 2 ("tolerate additive minor bumps") and 3 ("validate via matches_schema()") are mutually contradictory because 

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

Wired the doctor artifact-read gate (the ONLY production site reading a serialized RobotJsonEnvelope<AnalysisRun>) through schema_compatibility so the documented additive-minor forward-compat tolerance is actually enforced instead of being a dead tier. Picked option (1) of the suggested fix (route the gate, don't delete the helpers). Extracted a small testable helper check_artifact_schema(schema_id, schema_version) in crates/plsql-engine/src/main.rs (replacing the inline matches_schema exact-equality block at the former main.rs:440): it accepts same-id artifacts whose schema_compatibility verd

title: Slice trimmed string in resolve_sql so upper-derived offsets index the same buffer
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/sql_resolve.rs:204 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** None today: resolve_sql has zero non-test callers in the workspace, and the live lineage pipeline uses dml_edges (which uppercases without trim_start, so its offsets are aligned). The bug only manifests if a future internal caller or an external consumer of the publishable plsql-ir crate calls resolve_sql with leading-whitespace SQL, in which case it silently returns corrupted table/alias/schema lineage with no panic or error.
**Evidence:** sql_resolve.rs:42 `let upper = raw.trim_start().to_ascii_uppercase();` then the tokenizer scans `upper`'s byte offsets (lines 175-203) but slices the UNtrimmed `raw`: line 204 `let token = &raw[tok_start..i];`, line 224 `let cand = raw[a_start..i].to_string();`, line 247 `alias = raw[real_start..i].to_string();`.

Faithful repro of the slicing logic (rustc -O, ASCII input "    SELECT id FROM Employees emp"):
  table (token_upper) = "ROM EMPLO"
  alias = "ees"
i.e. off by the 4-byte leading-whitespace shift instead of "EMPLOYEES"/"emp".

Reachability: `rg resolve_sql crates/` shows the ONLY non-definition references are lib.rs:63 (`pub use sql_resolve::resolve_sql;`) and calls inside `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` blocks (sql_columns.rs:370, sql_fact_emit.rs:146, column_edges.rs:136, plus sql_resolve.rs's own tests). No external-crate or production caller exists. Production lineage path: plsql-
**Repro:** Triggering input (if reached): resolve_sql("    SELECT id FROM Employees emp") -> table "ROM EMPLO", alias "ees" (proven by standalone rustc run of the verbatim slice logic). Disproof of live impact: no production caller exists (grep above), the live dml_edges path uppercases the same untrimmed string it slices so it is aligned, and all in-tree callers pass pre-trimmed literals — hence the failing branch is unreachable in the current build.
**Suggested fix:** Bind the trimmed string once and thread it through instead of `raw`: in resolve_sql change line 42 to `let trimmed = raw.trim_start(); let upper = trimmed.to_ascii_uppercase();` and pass `trimmed` everywhere `raw` is currently passed to collect_from_and_joins/tables_after_keyword, so the offsets computed against `upper` index into `trimmed` (identical byte length for ASCII). Then the slices at lines 204/224/247 read the correct region. Add a regression test `resolve_sql("    SELECT id FROM Employees emp")` asserting table=="EMPLOYEES" and alias=="emp".

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit a9ab5913d6), re-verified on main. Fixed resolve_sql (sql_resolve.rs:41-83): bound `let trimmed = raw.trim_start()` once, derived `upper` from trimmed, and threaded `trimmed` everywhere `raw` was previously passed to collect_from_and_joins/tables_after_keyword. Root cause: upper was trim_start'd but slices indexed the untrimmed raw, so leading whitespace shifted every table/alias offset (table -> 'ROM EMPLO', alias -> 'ees'). ASCII whitespace is single-byte so trimmed/upper share offsets. (Labelled defense-in-depth: no non-test caller today, but fixed fully per instructions for the safe local fix.)

title: Stamp produced AnalysisArtifactManifest with ANALYSIS_RUN_SCHEMA.version instead of hardcoded 1.0.0
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-engine/src/lib.rs:705 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** A future or external consumer that gates on the embedded manifest's schema_version (via the documented is_readable_by/compatibility_with API) would classify a real 1.1.0-field-bearing artifact as Compatible-for-1.0.0 instead of ForwardCompatible, silently trusting fields it may not understand. No in-tree consumer hits this today — every current reader validates the envelope (correctly 1.1.0), so there is zero live failure path; it is stale provenance that worsens as the schema advances.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-engine/src/lib.rs:704-708 producer hardcodes the stamp:
    run.artifacts = AnalysisArtifactManifest {
        schema_version: plsql_output::SchemaVersion::new(1, 0, 0),
        artifact_digests: Vec::new(),
        redaction_policy: req.redaction_policy.clone(),
    };
Canonical constant lib.rs:208 `version: SchemaVersion::new(1, 1, 0)` with 1.1.0 additive-fields changelog at 203-207; analysis_run_envelope (lib.rs:307-308) wraps the SAME run with ANALYSIS_RUN_SCHEMA (1.1.0). schema_compatibility (lib.rs:477-484): equal minor => Compatible, produced.minor>consumer.minor => ForwardCompatible — so 1.0.0-stamp+1.0.0-consumer = Compatible, the inverted signal. 1.1.0 fields confirmed on CompletenessReport (plsql-core/src/lib.rs:741 diagnostics_total, 745 objects_unrecognized, 750 extracted_semantics_ratio, 752 posture, 659/666 unmeasured marker). Workspace grep `rg '\.artifacts'`
**Repro:** Triggering scenario (latent, not live): let run = analyze_project(req)?; then run.artifacts.compatibility_with(SchemaVersion::new(1,0,0)) returns SchemaCompatibility::Compatible because the stamp is 1.0.0 and consumer is 1.0.0 (equal minor). With the correct stamp ANALYSIS_RUN_SCHEMA.version (1.1.0) the same call would return ForwardCompatible. Proof it is not live today: rg '\.artifacts' across crates/ yields a single non-declaration hit — the producer itself (lib.rs:704); no production code reads the embedded manifest version, and main.rs:440 (the doctor) gates on envelope.matches_schema(ANALYSIS_RUN_SCHEMA), i.e. the envelope, which carries the correct 1.1.0. So the wrong stamp never reac
**Suggested fix:** Replace the literal at lib.rs:705 `schema_version: plsql_output::SchemaVersion::new(1, 0, 0)` with `schema_version: ANALYSIS_RUN_SCHEMA.version` so the producer and the single-source-of-truth constant cannot diverge, and add a regression test asserting `analyze_project(AnalysisRequest::default()).unwrap().artifacts.schema_version == ANALYSIS_RUN_SCHEMA.version` to guard against future drift when the schema advances.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit d2a5bc8d63), re-verified on main. Replaced the hardcoded literal at crates/plsql-engine/src/lib.rs:705 (was `schema_version: plsql_output::SchemaVersion::new(1, 0, 0)`) with `schema_version: ANALYSIS_RUN_SCHEMA.version` so the producer (analyze_project) and the single-source-of-truth canonical constant (currently 1.1.0) cannot diverge as the schema advances. Prevents a future/external consumer that gates on the embedded manifest version (compatibility_with / is_readable_by) from mis-classifying a real 1.1.0-field-bearing artifact as Compatible-for-1.0.0 instead of ForwardCompatible.

title: Fix asymmetric BEGIN/END depth in advance_to_decl_end (and recover_to_statement_boundary): count IF/LOOP/CASE and match END IF/LOOP/CASE as single terminators
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:1174 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** In the text-scanner fallback (reached when the ANTLR parse yields zero declarations or panics), any routine whose body contains an IF/LOOP/CASE block has its declaration span truncated at the first inner END IF;/END LOOP;/END CASE;, so every later statement — procedure calls, INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE writes, and EXECUTE IMMEDIATE dynamic-SQL sinks — is silently dropped from the dependency graph and injection/taint analysis: a fail-open false negative for a SQL-guard product, biting precisely when parsing has degraded.
**Evidence:** mod.rs:1189-1200 — depth is incremented ONLY on whole-word BEGIN (depth += 1; i += 5) but decremented on EVERY whole-word END (if depth>0 {depth-=1}; i += 3), with no IF/LOOP/CASE opener arms and no END IF/LOOP/CASE special-casing. matches_keyword_ignore_case (1101-1121) matches `END` whenever the following byte is a non-identifier char (a space in `END IF`), so `END IF`/`END LOOP`/`END CASE` each trigger a spurious decrement while their openers were never counted. The sibling lower_statement_body is correct: it increments depth on BEGIN/IF/LOOP/CASE (547-566) and matches END IF/LOOP/CASE as single terminators via end_keyword_len (597-614). recover.rs:35 recover_to_statement_boundary is structurally identical and shares the flaw. Consumption confirmed: lower_source sets body_statements: Vec::new() (mod.rs:50); engine lib.rs:936-951 looks up parse-tree bodies by span and on miss slices so
**Repro:** Faithful standalone port of advance_to_decl_end (skip_opaque_span is a no-op for these literal-free inputs, verified at recover.rs:156-182): "CREATE PROCEDURE p IS BEGIN IF x THEN NULL; END IF; END;" len=56 -> end=51 (TRUNC at "END IF;"); END LOOP variant len=61->56; END CASE len=65->60; control-flow-free "CREATE PROCEDURE p IS BEGIN NULL; END;" len=38->38 (FULL, correct); and the security-relevant case "CREATE PROCEDURE a IS BEGIN IF x THEN NULL; END IF; INSERT INTO audit VALUES(1); END;" len=85 -> end=51, slice="CREATE PROCEDURE a IS BEGIN IF x THEN NULL; END IF;" — the trailing INSERT-write of AUDIT lies entirely outside the span and is never fed to the DML-edge/taint extractor. Offsets m
**Suggested fix:** Make advance_to_decl_end (mod.rs:1174) and recover_to_statement_boundary (recover.rs:35) symmetric with the already-correct lower_statement_body: before the bare-END arm, detect END followed by IF/LOOP/CASE (mirror end_keyword_len), decrement depth once and advance i past the whole `END <kw>`; treat a bare END as the only thing that closes a BEGIN block; add IF/LOOP/CASE opener arms that increment depth (mirroring mod.rs:552-566). Best: extract the shared BEGIN/IF/LOOP/CASE-opener + bare-END/END-IF/LOOP/CASE-terminator + opaque-span bookkeeping into one helper used by all three scanners so they cannot drift again. Add a regression test asserting span.end == src.len() for `CREATE PROCEDURE a IS BEGIN IF x THEN NULL; END IF; INSERT INTO audit VALUES(1); END;` so the trailing INSERT/EXECUTE IMMEDIATE is analyzed.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit a9ab5913d6), re-verified on main. Fixed asymmetric BEGIN/END depth in advance_to_decl_end (lower/mod.rs) and recover_to_statement_boundary (recover.rs). Per the bead's 'Best' suggestion, extracted a single shared helper block_structure_step (+ end_block_len) in recover.rs that recognises BEGIN/IF/LOOP/CASE openers and matches END/END IF/END LOOP/END CASE as single terminators, then routed both scanners through it (lower_statement_body already had the correct mirrored logic via end_keyword_len). Root cause: openers IF/LOOP/CASE were never counted but a bare END matched the END of END IF/LOOP/CASE, decrementing depth spuriously

title: Harden extract_quoted / extract_first_quoted to honor Oracle doubled-'' escape (reuse string_literal_end)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs:640 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** None observable today: the truncated sql_literal field is write-only dead data — no analysis pass, security rule, or output ever reads it — but it would silently become a correctness bug the moment any future code consumes ExecuteImmediate.sql_literal as semantic SQL text.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs:640-657 extract_quoted returns at the first lone quote (line 649: `if nc == '\'' { return Some(buf); }`) with no doubled-'' handling. The same module's string_literal_end (stmt.rs:299-313) DOES handle the escape (line 304-305: `if j+1<len && chars[j+1]=='\'' { j += 2; }`). Sibling extract_first_quoted has the identical flaw (crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:726-741, returns line 733). classify() routes EXECUTE IMMEDIATE through extract_quoted at stmt.rs:494. Reproduction (throwaway test, run output): `RESULT: [ExecuteImmediate { sql_literal: "SELECT ", has_bind_variables: false }]`. Impact trace: `rg 'sql_literal' crates/ | grep -v test` returns only field decl (stmt.rs:77), two construction sites (stmt.rs:497, plsql-engine/src/lib.rs:626) and zero reads. `rg 'ExecuteImmediate|sql_literal'` across the production consumers (flow_intra.rs, dml_edges.rs
**Repro:** Function-level defect reproduces: lower_statement_body("EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'SELECT ''x'' FROM dual';") yields sql_literal == "SELECT " (verified by running a throwaway test against the current code, output quoted above). Disproof of impact: the field is never read in any non-test code path. Construction-only (stmt.rs:497, lib.rs:626); no destructuring read; the six production consumers of the lowered Statement list (flow_intra/dml_edges/calls/canonical/flow_query/fact_emit) never reference ExecuteImmediate or sql_literal; SEC001 injection detection operates on the DynamicSqlEvidence `site` string and taint facts, not the literal text; no serde serialization of the IR Statement reaches MCP/CLI
**Suggested fix:** In extract_quoted (stmt.rs:640) and extract_first_quoted (plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:726), on encountering an inner `'` peek the next char: if it is also `'`, consume it, push a single `'` to the buffer, and continue; otherwise the literal ends — mirroring string_literal_end. Add a unit test asserting EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'SELECT ''x'' FROM dual' lowers to sql_literal == "SELECT 'x' FROM dual". Given the field is currently write-only (no consumer reads sql_literal), this is a cheap defense-in-depth hardening and can be batched; if deferred, add a code comment noting the known divergence from string_literal_end so a future consumer of sql_literal isn't surprised by truncation.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit a9ab5913d6), re-verified on main. Hardened extract_quoted (stmt.rs:640) and extract_first_quoted (lower/mod.rs:726) to honor Oracle doubled-'' escapes by peeking the next char on an inner quote (consume + push single ' + continue) like string_literal_end. Root cause: both returned at the first lone quote, truncating sql_literal/sql_text at the first inner escaped quote ('SELECT ''x'' FROM dual' -> 'SELECT '). Switched both from char_indices to chars().peekable(). (Labelled defense-in-depth: the field is currently write-only, fixed fully per instructions.)

title: Make overload disambiguation uniqueness global (or downgrade its contract) in plsql-bindgen
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-bindgen/src/overload.rs:99 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** None today: disambiguate_overloads has no production caller, so no generated module can currently contain the duplicate pub fns. If a future phase wires its returned names into the emitter, two distinct overload buckets (or an ordinal/suffix name vs an unrelated singleton) can mint identical Rust identifiers, producing a generated module with duplicate `pub fn`s that fails to compile.
**Evidence:** overload.rs:70 `for (base_name, indices) in buckets {` ... line 99 `let mut counts: BTreeMap<&str, usize> = BTreeMap::new();` is rebuilt fresh inside the per-bucket loop; singletons emit verbatim at line 73 `out[only] = routines[only].name.clone();`. Docstring lines 24-26 and Pass-2 comment lines 95-96 claim "every emitted pub fn name is unique" (global), but state is per-bucket only.

Reproduced via faithful re-trace of the exact algorithm (snake_case + shared_prefix_length + Pass1/Pass2 transcribed verbatim; transcription validated against the repo's own test expectations — "transcription matches all in-repo expected outputs"):
  cross-bucket get/get_x: ["get_x_a", "get_x_b", "get_x_a", "get_x_b"]  (2 unique of 4)  *** DUPLICATE ***
  ordinal vs singleton:  ["touch_1", "touch_2", "touch_1"]  (2 unique of 3)  *** DUPLICATE ***
  suffix vs singleton:   ["hire_p_emp_id", "hire_p_emp_name"
**Repro:** Triggering input (legal PL/SQL, would break IF names were consumed by the emitter): routines [("get",["x_a"]),("get",["x_b"]),("get_x",["a"]),("get_x",["b"])] → disambiguate_overloads returns ["get_x_a","get_x_b","get_x_a","get_x_b"], two duplicate identifiers across the `get` and `get_x` buckets. Also ("touch",[]),("touch",[]) alongside singleton ("touch_1",["p_id"]) → ["touch_1","touch_2","touch_1"]. Impossibility-of-active-failure proof: no non-test code calls disambiguate_overloads (only `pub use` re-export at lib.rs:34); the emitter writes r.name verbatim and the binary never invokes the disambiguator, so no current build can produce these duplicates. Latent in an unused public API.
**Suggested fix:** Hoist a single `taken: BTreeSet<String>` above the `for (base_name, indices) in buckets` loop (line 70), seed it with singleton names as they are emitted verbatim (line 73), and on each disambiguated/singleton assignment probe a deterministic global ordinal (`_2`,`_3`,...) until the name is free, recording every emitted name. Iterate buckets in their existing BTreeMap order to preserve determinism. Add regression tests for the cross-bucket `get`/`get_x` case and the ordinal/suffix-vs-singleton cases. Alternatively, if global uniqueness is intentionally out of scope, downgrade the docstring (lines 24-26) and Pass-2 comment (lines 95-96) to state only "unique within an overload group". Low priority: no production path consumes these names today (emitter uses RoutineBinding.name verbatim; overloads are diagnosed as BG_UNSUPPORTED_OVERLOAD_AMBIGUOUS), so this is hardening of a latent public-
