- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 9ac2e4992c), re-verified on main. Defense-in-depth latent bug: disambiguate_overloads enforced uniqueness only per-bucket (counts rebuilt fresh inside the per-bucket loop; singletons emitted verbatim) while the docstring/comment claimed a global 'every emitted pub fn name is unique' guarantee. Root-cause fix: hoisted a single cross-bucket `taken: BTreeSet<String>` above the bucket loop; Pass A seeds all singletons first (verbatim, giving real singletons precedence); Pass B disambiguates overload groups in deterministic BTreeMap order, routing every assignment (bare, ordinal, or suffix) through a new claim() helper that probes

title: Fix extract_owner_and_name to handle whitespace inside quoted Oracle identifiers in DBMS_METADATA loader
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs:1783 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** A DBMS_METADATA .sql file for an object whose Oracle identifier (name or owner) is a double-quoted name containing whitespace (e.g. "MY TABLE") is interned in the offline snapshot under a truncated key ("MY"); lookups by the real name miss it, two such objects in one schema collide (last-write-wins), and a whitespace-bearing quoted OWNER additionally misroutes the object into the PUBLIC default schema bucket.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs:1783 — `let token = after.split_whitespace().next()?;` terminates the [OWNER.]NAME token at the first interior space, so a quoted identifier with whitespace is cut short. Lines 1798-1811: after `split('.')` + `trim_matches('"')`, the truncated fragment "MY" passes the all-alphanumeric validity check (1803) and is returned as the name. Caller chain is public: load_from_dbms_metadata_dir (1324) -> classify_dbms_metadata_ddl (1408) -> extract_owner_and_name (1423); the returned object_text is interned (1427) and used as the HashMap key (1357 `schema_catalog.objects.insert(obj_name, obj)`). Doc comment at 1774-1779 advertises quoted-identifier tolerance ("CREATE TABLE \"HR\".\"EMP\" works") but only the spaceless case is handled; the sole existing test classify_quoted_table_owner_name_is_table (8432) uses "HR"."EMP". Empirical run of the verbatim function: "\"
**Repro:** Triggering input: a file containing `CREATE TABLE "HR"."MY TABLE" (id NUMBER);` fed to load_from_dbms_metadata_dir. Extracted the exact function body (lib.rs:1780-1812) into a standalone program (rustc -O), upper-casing the input the way upper_remainder() does. Output: `"\"HR\".\"MY TABLE\" (...)" => Some((Some("HR"), "MY"))` — name truncated to MY (expected "MY TABLE"). Owner-with-space variant `"MY OWNER"."EMP"` => `(None, "MY")` drops the owner (routes to PUBLIC) and truncates the name. Spaceless quoted and unquoted inputs return correct results, confirming the fault is specific to whitespace inside quoted identifiers.
**Suggested fix:** Replace the `after.split_whitespace().next()` tokenizer with a small scanner that treats a `"..."` segment as a single token that may contain spaces (runs to the closing `"`), and stops an unquoted segment at whitespace, `(`, `;`, or DDL punctuation. Split owner/name on the top-level (outside-quotes) `.`. For a quoted segment, relax the validity check (lines 1803-1810) to accept any non-empty content; keep the strict alphanumeric/`_` rule and trailing-punctuation trim for unquoted segments only. Add tests for `"HR"."MY TABLE"` (owner HR, name `MY TABLE`), `"MY OWNER"."EMP"` (owner `MY OWNER`, name EMP), fully-quoted unqualified `"MY TABLE"`, and a collision guard ensuring `"MY TABLE"` and `"MY OTHER"` intern as distinct keys.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit cd61197f44), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix in extract_owner_and_name (crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs:1780). Replaced the truncating `after.split_whitespace().next()` tokenizer with a quote-aware scanner: a `"..."` segment is a single token that may contain whitespace and runs to its closing quote; unquoted runs stop at the first non-identifier byte; owner/name split is the first top-level (outside-quotes) `.`. Validity check relaxed to accept any non-empty content for quoted segments while keeping the strict alphanumeric/`_` rule for unquoted segments. Unterminated quote returns None (malformed header). This stops `"HR"

title: Make is_synthetic_alias delimiter-aware so a single-quoted body validates only as 'sx_<hex8>'
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-accretion/src/tokscrub.rs:203 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** In the G8 re-scan backstop (gate.rs::residue_check), an un-scrubbed estate STRING literal whose body is exactly '7', '7.0', 'id_<hex12>', or 'sx_<hex8>' passes the I-PRIVACY residue gate as if it were a clean synthetic, so those low-entropy original bytes can survive into a persisted/accepted fixture without being flagged — a fail-open hole in a guard advertised as fail-closed. High-entropy secrets (card numbers, salaries, SSNs) are still correctly rejected, and on the normal build path the upstream scrub rewrites every string literal to 'sx_<hex8>' before the check runs, so no end-to-end leak is demonstrated under normal operation.
**Evidence:** tokscrub.rs:200-213 (verbatim):
  pub fn is_synthetic_alias(text: &str) -> bool {
      let body = text.trim_matches('\'').trim_matches('"');   // <- strips BOTH quote kinds unconditionally
      if let Some(hex) = body.strip_prefix("id_") { return hex.len()==12 && hex.bytes().all(|b| b.is_ascii_hexdigit()); }
      if let Some(hex) = body.strip_prefix("sx_") { return hex.len()==8  && hex.bytes().all(|b| b.is_ascii_hexdigit()); }
      body == "7" || body == "7.0"
  }
Docstring (184-189) says a '…'-delimited body should only validate as sx_<hex8> and a "…"-delimited body as id_<hex12>; the code enforces neither (no delimiter is recorded). synthesise (167) confirms Class::Str can ONLY emit 'sx_<hex8>'.

Both privacy layers import this one function on raw lexer token text with no quote-stripping at the call site:
  fixture.rs:543  if !is_synthetic_alias(text)        (text = TokVerdict::Est
**Repro:** Trigger (proven empirically above): a fixture/source string literal 'WHERE status = '\''7'\''' lexes to a StringLiteral whose verbatim text is '7'; token_verdicts emits EstateClass("'7'"); is_synthetic_alias("'7'") strips quotes to "7" and returns true, so gate.rs::residue_check passes it as clean despite synthesise() only ever emitting 'sx_<hex8>' for a Class::Str. Same for '7.0', 'id_0123456789ab', 'sx_0123abcd'. Bound on impact: '4111111111111111' (and any body not matching id_<hex12>/sx_<hex8>/7/7.0) is rejected, so only those low-entropy shapes leak, and only when an un-scrubbed fixture reaches G8 (upstream structure_preserving_scrub normally rewrites all string literals first, so the b
**Suggested fix:** Replace the blanket double trim_matches with delimiter-discriminated branches that tie each accepted shape to the delimiter synthesise() actually emits: a '…'-delimited body must match sx_<hex8> ONLY; a "…"-delimited body must match id_<hex12> ONLY; undelimited tokens may be id_<hex12> or the bare numerals 7 / 7.0. This rejects '7', '7.0', 'id_<hex12>', and 'sx_<hex8>'-as-string-content while still accepting every shape synthesise() produces. Note the existing positive-path unit tests pass bare bodies (gate.rs:1179 / fixture.rs:1018 / tokscrub.rs:485-489 use "sx_0123abcd" without quotes); a bare sx_ token cannot occur in practice because token_verdicts always carries the quotes for a StringLiteral, but to avoid breaking those tests either update them to the delimited forms ('sx_…', "id_…") or add an explicit undelimited sx_<hex8> branch — do NOT accept a single-quoted body as anything ot

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 7450e1f63a), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix in crates/plsql-accretion/src/tokscrub.rs::is_synthetic_alias. Replaced the blanket double `trim_matches('\'').trim_matches('"')` with delimiter-discriminated branches: a single-quote-delimited body validates ONLY as 'sx_<hex8>' (the sole Class::Str synthetic); a double-quote-delimited body ONLY as "id_<hex12>"; undelimited tokens as id_<hex12> / 7 / 7.0, plus an explicit undelimited sx_<hex8> branch (dead on the production path since token_verdicts always carries the '...' for a StringLiteral) so the pre-existing bare-body unit tests (gate.rs:1179, fixture.rs:1018, tokscrub.rs:

title: Add len<2 guard to recognise_datetime_literal to stop [1..0] slice panic on lone quote
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/expr.rs:216 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** A malformed or parser-recovered expression fragment such as `v_d := DATE';` panics the analyzer at expr.rs:216 ("begin > end (1 > 0) when slicing"), aborting analysis of the entire compilation unit instead of degrading to Expr::Raw as the documented no-panic contract requires.
**Evidence:** Cited code (crates/plsql-ir/src/expr.rs:194-220):
  212  let trimmed = after.trim_start();
  213  if !trimmed.starts_with('\'') || !trimmed.ends_with('\'') {
  214      return None;
  215  }
  216  let body = &trimmed[1..trimmed.len() - 1];
For a lone `'`, starts_with('\'') AND ends_with('\'') both inspect the same single byte and are true, so line 213 does NOT early-return; line 216 computes &trimmed[1..0].

Sibling recognise_string_literal (line 164) DOES guard: `if !text.starts_with('\'') || !text.ends_with('\'') || text.len() < 2 { return None; }` — recognise_datetime_literal omits the equivalent len<2 guard.

Real-crate test through public lower_expression entry, run via `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=$PWD/target cargo test -p plsql-ir`:
  thread '...dt_degenerate_does_not_panic' panicked at crates/plsql-ir/src/expr.rs:216:24:
  begin > end (1 > 0) when slicing `'`
  test result: FAILED. 0 passe
**Repro:** Triggering input X -> wrong output Y: lower_expression("DATE'") (or "TIMESTAMP '", "INTERVAL '", "DATE   '") panics with "begin > end (1 > 0) when slicing `'`" at expr.rs:216 instead of returning an Expr. Trace: recognise_keyword_literal returns None (upper "DATE'" != NULL/TRUE/FALSE); recognise_string_literal returns None (text starts with 'D' not '\''); recognise_datetime_literal matches keyword "DATE", after="'", next_byte '\'' is not alnum/_/$/# so no early return, trimmed="'", line 213 passes both quote checks on the single byte, line 216 slices [1..0] -> panic. Contrast: lower_expression("DATE'2020-01-01'") returns Expr::DateTimeLit fine.
**Suggested fix:** Mirror recognise_string_literal's guard at line 213: change `if !trimmed.starts_with('\'') || !trimmed.ends_with('\'') {` to `if trimmed.len() < 2 || !trimmed.starts_with('\'') || !trimmed.ends_with('\'') {`. The len<2 check rejects the degenerate single-quote case before the slice, so lower_expression falls through to Expr::Raw as contracted. (len is byte length; both quotes are single-byte ASCII so the comparison is correct.) Add regression tests asserting lower_expression("DATE'"), lower_expression("TIMESTAMP '"), and lower_expression("INTERVAL '") return non-panicking Expr variants.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit a9ab5913d6), re-verified on main. Added `trimmed.len() < 2` guard to recognise_datetime_literal (expr.rs:213) mirroring recognise_string_literal. Root cause: for a lone `'`, starts_with('\'') AND ends_with('\'') both inspect the same single byte and are true, so the guard didn't early-return and &trimmed[1..0] panicked. With the guard, lower_expression("DATE'") etc. fall through to a non-panicking Expr.

title: Validate every param=value pair in is_allowed_alter_session, not just the first token (allowlist bypass)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/enforcement.rs:64 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** An agent holding any session lease can smuggle non-allowlisted ALTER SESSION parameters (SQL_TRACE=TRUE, EVENTS='10046 ...', STATISTICS_LEVEL=ALL, etc.) past the documented content allowlist by prefixing one allowlisted parameter, enabling trace/diagnostic file generation (info disclosure) and session-context changes that the guard explicitly promises to reject.
**Evidence:** enforcement.rs:58-66 — `pub fn is_allowed_alter_session(stmt: &str) -> bool { let upper = stmt.trim().to_ascii_uppercase(); let Some(rest) = upper.strip_prefix("ALTER SESSION SET ") else { return false; }; let param = rest.split(['=', ' ', '\t']).next().unwrap_or("").trim(); ALTER_SESSION_ALLOWLIST.contains(&param) }` — only the FIRST token before any `=`/space is checked. Module/function doc (enforcement.rs:54-56) promises "Anything outside the allowlist (e.g. statements that change security/audit context) is rejected." Sole-gate path confirmed: session_tool.rs:241 `if !is_allowed_alter_session(&statement) { return Err(...) }` then session_tool.rs:248 `apply_session_statement(...)`, and lease.rs:305-307 `pub fn apply_session_statement(...) { self.with_lease(&id.0, |lease| { lease.conn.execute(statement, &[])?; ... }) }` runs the raw statement verbatim, no re-parse. Standalone reproducti
**Repro:** Triggering input X -> wrong output Y: is_allowed_alter_session("ALTER SESSION SET CURRENT_SCHEMA=HR SQL_TRACE=TRUE") returns true (should be false) because split's .next() yields only "CURRENT_SCHEMA", which is allowlisted; the trailing SQL_TRACE=TRUE is never examined. Same for EVENTS='10046 ...'. Confirmed the gate is correct for the trailing param in isolation: is_allowed_alter_session("ALTER SESSION SET SQL_TRACE = TRUE") returns false. Oracle accepts multiple space-separated param = value pairs in a single ALTER SESSION SET (documented syntax: ALTER SESSION SET param=value [param=value]...), so the smuggled statement executes verbatim at lease.rs:307 rather than erroring.
**Suggested fix:** Replace the single-token check with a string-literal-aware parser that splits the post-prefix `rest` into successive `param = value` clauses, extracts the identifier immediately preceding each top-level `=`, and requires every one to be in ALTER_SESSION_ALLOWLIST. Fail closed: reject any statement that cannot be cleanly parsed into known param=value pairs, and reject if zero assignments are found (so values containing `=`/spaces inside quotes, e.g. EVENTS = '10046 ...', are not mis-parsed as parameter names). Add regression tests asserting ALTER SESSION SET CURRENT_SCHEMA=HR SQL_TRACE=TRUE and CURRENT_SCHEMA = HR EVENTS = '10046 ...' both return false, plus a positive multi-param case (CURRENT_SCHEMA = HR OPTIMIZER_MODE = ALL_ROWS) returning true.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit fcb67103bb), re-verified on main. Root cause: enforcement.rs is_allowed_alter_session() only checked the FIRST token via `rest.split(['=',' ','\t']).next()`, so `ALTER SESSION SET CURRENT_SCHEMA=HR SQL_TRACE=TRUE` (and ... EVENTS='10046 ...') passed because only CURRENT_SCHEMA was inspected. Fix: added a string-literal-aware top-level tokenizer (alter_session_params) that splits the post-prefix remainder into Word/Eq/Str tokens (single-quoted literals opaque, doubled-'' escapes honored, unterminated literal -> None), walks repeated `WORD = (WORD|STR)` clauses, collects the identifier before each top-level `=`, and requires EVE

title: Wire OAuth ScopeGrant into HTTP dispatch level gate or remove the enforced-control doc claim
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http.rs:79 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=medium
**Impact:** If/when oraclemcp_core's HTTP transport is wired into a server binary with OAuth enabled, a client presenting a narrowly-scoped bearer token (e.g. oracle:read) would have its scope captured into ScopeGrant and then silently dropped — the session operating-level ceiling is NOT lowered as the doc comment promises, so the read-scoped principal could reach write/DDL tools whenever the profile/session default ceiling permits (fail-open vs. the documented least-privilege contract). No production binary triggers this today, so it is latent.
**Evidence:** http.rs:84-85 defines `pub struct ScopeGrant(pub Vec<String>)`; http.rs:122-125 the only insertion: `request.extensions_mut().insert(ScopeGrant(scopes));`. Workspace grep for ScopeGrant returns ONLY: lib.rs:58 (re-export), http.rs:85 (def), http.rs:125 (insert) — NO reader. `rg 'extensions\(\)\.get|get::<ScopeGrant>'` returns nothing. apply_oauth_scopes (scope.rs:39, monotone-down `min(existing, scope ceiling)`) is called ONLY in scope.rs tests (lines 81/84/91/107) plus the doc comment at http.rs:81 — `rg apply_oauth_scopes crates/` confirms no non-test caller. server.rs:232-240 `call_tool` ignores `_context` and passes name/args straight to `run_tool`; no SessionLevelState in the HTTP dispatch path. build_router/serve_http callers: only tests (e2e_mcp.rs:90/152) and within http.rs; the production plsql-mcp/src/main.rs run_serve uses stdio + `tcp::serve` (main.rs:91 "transports":["stdio"
**Repro:** Triggering input (latent path): mount oraclemcp_core::http::build_router with HttpTransportConfig.oauth=Some(...) into a server, then POST /mcp tools/call for a write/DDL-tier tool carrying `Authorization: Bearer <jwt with scope=oracle:read>`. The token validates (oauth_guard, http.rs:113-116), ScopeGrant(["oracle:read"]) is inserted (http.rs:125), but call_tool (server.rs:235) discards _context and run_tool never consults the scope or any SessionLevelState — so the write/DDL tool is reachable instead of being blocked. Disproof of present-day exploitability: no shipping binary calls build_router/serve_http (only tests/e2e_mcp.rs and the in-crate test module); plsql-mcp uses tcp::serve/stdio.
**Suggested fix:** Either (a) implement the control: plumb a per-session SessionLevelState into the HTTP dispatch path, read ScopeGrant from the rmcp RequestContext.extensions in call_tool (server.rs:232), call oraclemcp_auth::apply_oauth_scopes (monotone-down) on it, and gate the resolved tool's required OperatingLevel before dispatch — plus an e2e test presenting an oracle:read token and asserting a write/DDL tool is blocked; or (b) the minimal correctness fix: delete the misleading enforced-control prose at http.rs:79-83 and 122-123 and mark the ScopeGrant insertion as not-yet-enforced (or remove it) so no integrator assumes a defense-in-depth that is absent.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
notes: PASS 2026-06-02 (partial): Applied the directed MINIMAL CORRECTNESS fix (option b), not the full control. Verified via grep that ScopeGrant has NO reader anywhere (only def at http.rs:85, insert at http.rs:125, re-export at lib.rs:58) and apply_oauth_scopes is called only in scope.rs tests; server.rs:235 call_tool discards _context, so no scope->operating-level gate exists in the HTTP dispatch path. Rewrote the misleading 'enforced control' doc prose on ScopeGrant (http.rs:79-83) and the inline comment in oauth_guard (http.rs:122-123) to mark the grant as captured-but-NOT-yet-enforced, explicitly warning integrators tha

title: Classify SELECT ... FOR UPDATE as Guarded/ReadWrite (inspect query.locks in Query arm)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs:550 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** A lock-taking, transaction-holding SELECT ... FOR UPDATE (including OF/NOWAIT/SKIP LOCKED) is classified Safe/ReadOnly, so on a permissive/READ_WRITE session it dispatches ungated with no step-up, and on a ReadOnly-mode schema policy it slips past the danger>=Guarded deny check (policy.rs:130) that a correctly-classified Guarded statement would hit.
**Evidence:** classifier.rs:550-591 Statement::Query arm consults only user_defined_calls()/routine_purity() and statement_purity(); it never reads query.locks, so a UDF-free SELECT ... FOR UPDATE takes the stmt_pure branch -> DangerLevel::Safe / OperatingLevel::ReadOnly.
levels.rs:93 doc: DangerLevel::Guarded "...MERGE, CTAS, `SELECT … FOR UPDATE`, COMMIT/ROLLBACK, EXPLAIN PLAN." — explicit contract violated.
sqlparser 0.62 (Cargo.toml:16); query.rs:52 `pub locks: Vec<LockClause>` — signal is present in the AST, unused.
Stage A does not rescue it: PLSQL_SIDE_EFFECT_MARKERS (classifier.rs:119-129) contains no FOR UPDATE; default block_patterns empty; "SELECT ... FOR UPDATE" does not start with DECLARE/BEGIN/CREATE -> falls through to StageA::PureSql -> classify_statement.
Runtime probe (cargo run --example, real Classifier::default().classify, then removed):
  SELECT * FROM t                        da
**Repro:** Trigger X->Y: input `SELECT * FROM t FOR UPDATE` (any FOR UPDATE variant) -> classify() returns danger=Safe, required_level=Some(ReadOnly), identical to plain `SELECT * FROM t`. Correct output per levels.rs:93 is danger=Guarded, required=ReadWrite. The AST carries query.locks=[LockClause{lock_type:Update,..}] which the Query arm ignores. Verified by building and running a temporary example against the real crate (output above); example removed and `git diff crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs` confirms source unchanged from HEAD.
**Suggested fix:** In the Statement::Query arm (classifier.rs ~550-591), before the stmt_pure decision add `let has_row_lock = !query.locks.is_empty();` and require `stmt_pure && !has_row_lock` for the Safe branch; otherwise fall to guarded_rw. This maps every SELECT ... FOR UPDATE (incl. OF/NOWAIT/SKIP LOCKED, all populating query.locks, and round-tripped via to_string() in the multi-statement split path) to Guarded/ReadWrite, matching levels.rs:93. Add an assertion test covering all five variants.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit fcb67103bb), re-verified on main. Root cause: classifier.rs Statement::Query arm never inspected query.locks, so a UDF-free `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` took the stmt_pure branch -> Safe/ReadOnly, contradicting levels.rs:93 (Guarded) and slipping past policy.rs:130 danger>=Guarded deny. Fix: `let has_row_lock = !query.locks.is_empty();` and require `stmt_pure && !has_row_lock`; FOR UPDATE / OF / NOWAIT / SKIP LOCKED all populate query.locks (and survive the to_string() round-trip in the multi-statement split path) -> Guarded/ReadWrite.

title: Make suppress.rs comment-start detection string/block-comment aware so `--` inside a literal cannot host a directive
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-sast/src/suppress.rs:131 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** A reviewer or attacker who can get text into a scanned PL/SQL file can plant a suppression directive inside a string literal or block comment (e.g. v := '-- plsql-scan:ignore SEC001 '; or /* -- plsql-scan:ignore * */) that silences genuine SEC001/SEC006 security findings file-wide via the line-0 fallback — a fail-open in the suppression layer. Currently dormant: no production code calls apply_suppressions yet, so nothing triggers it in the shipped scan/SARIF/MCP pipeline today.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-sast/src/suppress.rs:121-136 index_source(): `let Some(mpos) = lower.find(MARKER) else { continue; }; ... let Some(cpos) = raw.find("--") else { continue; }; if mpos < cpos { continue; }`. raw.find("--") matches a `--` anywhere, including inside a single-quoted string or /* */ block comment.

Standalone probe replicating the exact index_source/parse_rule_list/RuleSet logic (compiled with rustc -O):
[REGISTERED] report payload 1 (glued ;)   raw="v := '-- plsql-scan:ignore SEC001';"  mpos=Some(9) cpos=Some(6) -> matches(SEC001)=false all=false ids=["SEC001';"]
[REGISTERED] report payload 2 (glued ;)    raw="v := '-- plsql-scan:ignore *';"      mpos=Some(9) cpos=Some(6) -> matches(SEC001)=false all=false ids=["*';"]
[REGISTERED] verifier payload A (trail sp) raw="v := '-- plsql-scan:ignore SEC001 ';" mpos=Some(9) cpos=Some(6) -> matches(SEC001)=TRUE all=false
[REGISTERED] verif
**Repro:** Triggering input (file scanned via apply_suppressions with a SEC006 line-0 finding for the file): a single line `v := '-- plsql-scan:ignore * ';` (note trailing space before the closing quote) — or `/* -- plsql-scan:ignore SEC006 */`. For raw="v := '-- plsql-scan:ignore * ';": mpos(marker)=9, cpos(first `--`)=6, so `mpos < cpos` is false and the line is registered as a real directive with all=true, suppressing every span-less finding in the file. Disproof of the report's specific payloads: `v := '-- plsql-scan:ignore SEC001';` does NOT suppress because parse_rule_list tokenizes the glued `';` into the rule id, producing "SEC001';" != "SEC001" (matches=false). The underlying guard defect is n
**Suggested fix:** Replace the naive `raw.find("--")` at line 131 with a single-quote-aware (and ideally /* */-aware) scan that returns the byte offset of the first `--` that begins a genuine Oracle line comment, skipping any `--` inside a single-quoted string literal (honoring the `''` escaped-quote convention) and inside block comments. Keep the existing `if mpos < cpos { continue; }` guard. Add regression tests for: (1) `--`-inside-string directive with a clean trailing token (`v := '-- plsql-scan:ignore SEC001 ';`) must NOT suppress, covering both the precise-line and the line-0 file-scoped fallback paths; (2) the wildcard in-string variant; (3) a block-comment carrier; (4) the legitimate directive-after-string case (`v := 'foo -- bar' || baz; -- plsql-scan:ignore SEC001`) must still suppress. Note: not live-exploitable today (no production caller of apply_suppressions), so fix this before wiring the s

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit df3dcfd601), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix in crates/plsql-sast/src/suppress.rs. index_source() previously located the Oracle line comment with raw.find("--"), which matches a `--` anywhere on the line — including inside a single-quoted string literal or a /* */ block comment. With the line-0 file-scoped fallback (inline_reason_for), a marker buried in such a literal/comment could register a `plsql-scan:ignore` directive (e.g. `v := '-- plsql-scan:ignore * ';` with a trailing space parses a clean `*` token, or `/* -- plsql-scan:ignore SEC001 */`) and silence genuine SEC001/SEC006 findings file-wide — a fail-open. Replace

title: Replace zero-output assert! in compute_return_type with a typed emit diagnostic for degenerate functions
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-bindgen/src/emit.rs:549 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** An operator (or upstream tool) feeding plsql-bindgen a serde-valid-but-degenerate plan — a function with return_type=null and no OUT/IN OUT params — crashes the codegen CLI with a panic/SIGABRT instead of the documented exit-1 typed failure, breaking the "never panic on bad input" contract and any CI gate that distinguishes parse/emit errors (exit 1) from crashes.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-bindgen/src/emit.rs:546-554:
  match parts.len() {
      0 => {
          // Procedure with no OUT params.
          assert!(matches!(r.kind, RoutineKind::Procedure));  // line 549
          "()".to_string()
      }
emit.rs:154 calls `let return_type = compute_return_type(r);` UNCONDITIONALLY, before the only guard (unsupported_type at emit.rs:159, which checks scalar marshalling, not kind/return-type consistency). emit_wrappers (emit.rs:53-68) performs no validation. main.rs:212 deserializes BindingPlan via plain serde_json::from_str with no validation; RoutineBinding.return_type is `Option<RustTypeRef>` (lib.rs:69) with default derive.

Live repro (debug): exit 101
  thread 'main' panicked at crates/plsql-bindgen/src/emit.rs:549:13: assertion failed: matches!(r.kind, RoutineKind::Procedure)
Live repro (release, panic="abort" at Cargo.toml:71): exit 134 (SIGABRT) — proving 
**Repro:** echo '{"package_id":"hr.p","package_name":"P","routines":[{"name":"f","kind":"function","parameters":[],"return_type":null,"autonomous_transaction":false}],"diagnostics":[]}' | ./target/debug/plsql-bindgen  → exit 101, panic at emit.rs:549. Same input to ./target/release/plsql-bindgen → exit 134 (SIGABRT). Expected per contract: exit 1 with a typed BINDING_* error / emit-error message on stderr.
**Suggested fix:** Do not assert on untrusted-input shape. Either (1) in the len==0 arm, when r.kind is Function, signal emit_routine to emit a typed-error body (mirroring the unsupported_type path at emit.rs:159-171, e.g. code "BINDING_DEGENERATE_FUNCTION") instead of asserting — keeping exit 0 with valid Rust + a bindgen diagnostic; or (2) add a validation pass over the deserialized BindingPlan in main.rs between serde_json::from_str (line 212) and emit_wrappers (line 228) that rejects a Function with return_type==None and no OUT/IN OUT params, returning ExitCode::from(1) with a stderr message — matching the documented exit-1 contract. Add a unit test feeding the degenerate function plan asserting non-panicking, contract-conformant output.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 9ac2e4992c), re-verified on main. Root cause: compute_return_type (emit.rs:549) asserted matches!(r.kind, RoutineKind::Procedure) on the parts.len()==0 arm. A function plan with return_type=null and no OUT/IN OUT params is untrusted-input-reachable because main.rs deserializes the BindingPlan via plain serde with no validation; it panicked (debug exit 101, release SIGABRT exit 134), breaking the never-panic contract. Fix (suggested option 1): added is_degenerate_function(r) in emit_routine that diverts such a routine to a typed-error body emitting code BINDING_DEGENERATE_FUNCTION (mirroring the unsupported_type path) before th

title: Poison Auditor on durable flush failure so a forked/duplicate-seq audit chain cannot be re-issued
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/sink.rs:159 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** On a transient fsync failure (EIO writeback error / ENOSPC) during a Guarded/Destructive oracle_query_execute, the seq=N line is already written to the audit JSONL but the Auditor's in-memory state is not advanced; the next durable tool call re-issues the same seq=N reusing prev_hash=H_{N-1}, forking the tamper-evident hash chain so the audit log fails its own walk-and-verify integrity check exactly on the I/O-failure path the fsync-before-execute design exists to harden.
**Evidence:** crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/sink.rs:155-164 (Auditor::append): state advanced only AFTER flush:
  155  let mut state = self.state.lock().expect("audit state poisoned");
  156  let seq = state.seq + 1;
  157  let record = AuditRecord::chained(draft, seq, &state.last_hash, timestamp);
  158  self.sink.append(&record)?;
  159  if durable {
  160      self.sink.flush()?;          // early return on fsync failure
  161  }
  162  state.seq = seq;                 // NOT reached on flush err
  163  state.last_hash = record.entry_hash.clone();
sink.rs:62-77 (FileAuditSink): append uses f.write_all on a raw std::fs::File (no BufWriter -> write(2) to page cache, visible immediately); flush is a separate f.sync_all() (fsync) -> genuinely separable failures.
query_execute.rs:127-129: auditor.append(&pre, now(), true) with durable=true, error propagated via ? BEFORE executor.execute at line 132 (fail-c
**Repro:** Standalone transcription of Auditor::append control flow with a sink whose flush() fails once after a successful append(). Output:
  call1 result: Err("EIO: fsync failed")   <- flush failed, state NOT advanced
  call2 result: Ok(Record { seq: 1, prev_hash: "genesis", ... })  <- reused seq=1
  ON-DISK RECORDS:
    seq=1 prev_hash=genesis
    seq=1 prev_hash=genesis
  DUPLICATE SEQ? true   SAME PREV_HASH? true (both fork off 'genesis')
Concrete X->Y: append seq=N succeeds (line written), flush errors -> Err returned, state stays at N-1; next durable append re-issues seq=N with identical prev_hash=H_{N-1}. The crate's own file_sink_persists_and_chain_verifies (sink.rs:261-268) asserts rec.seq==
**Suggested fix:** Poison the Auditor when a durable flush fails so no subsequent append can reuse seq N (the seq=N bytes are already in the file but not durably synced; re-issuing N forks the chain). Add `poisoned: bool` to ChainState (init false); at the top of append after locking, return Err if poisoned; wrap the durable flush so on Err set state.poisoned=true before propagating, advancing state.seq/last_hash only after a successful flush. This preserves fail-closed semantics (executor still never runs un-logged) while guaranteeing the on-disk chain never contains two records with the same seq forking off the same prev_hash. Add a regression test using a sink whose flush() returns Err once: assert the next append() Errs (poisoned) and the sink never holds a duplicate seq. Do NOT instead advance state on flush failure (that would claim a non-durable record as committed).

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit daaaebd1e0), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix in crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/sink.rs. Added `poisoned: bool` to ChainState (init false) and a new `AuditError::Poisoned` variant. In Auditor::append: (1) check `state.poisoned` immediately after locking and fail closed with Err(Poisoned) if set; (2) wrap the durable `self.sink.flush()` so on Err it sets `state.poisoned = true` BEFORE propagating the original I/O error; (3) state.seq/last_hash are advanced only after a successful flush (unchanged). This preserves the fail-closed/fsync-before-execute guarantee (executor still never runs un-logged; non-durable record is never clai

title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT — plsql-doc serve_preview_blocking has no public-bind guard, unlike plsql-mcp
description:
crates/plsql-doc/src/serve.rs:38-43 -- the public, re-exported (lib.rs:20 'pub use serve::serve_preview_blocking') function serve_preview_blocking<A: ToSocketAddrs>(addr, set, project_label) binds whatever address the caller passes directly via TcpListener::bind(addr), with no validation.

The module doc (serve.rs:6) states the server 'binds 127.0.0.1 by default ... SHOULD NOT be' exposed -- but that is only a convention; the function imposes no guard. If a caller passes 0.0.0.0 or a routable address, the preview server -- which serves rendered PL/SQL source, schema docs, and dependency graphs over plain HTTP with NO authentication -- is exposed on all interfaces.

Contrast with crates/plsql-mcp/src/tcp.rs:parse_listen_target, which deliberately REFUSES public-internet binds (0.0.0.0 / ::) unless explicitly overridden. The two TCP-serving crates in the same workspace have inconsistent bind-safety postures.

Why it matters: defense-in-depth. For an OSS release, a public API that can trivially expose unauthenticated source/schema content on a public interface, while a sibling crate guards against exactly that, is an inconsistent and leaky abstraction a security-minded reviewer will flag. The 'SHOULD NOT' doc is not a control.

Suggested fix: mirror the plsql-mcp pattern -- have serve_preview_blocking (or a wrapper) resolve the addr and refuse non-loopback/non-private binds by default, with an explicit opt-in parameter to override. At minimum, resolve the addr and emit a loud warning when binding a non-loopback address.
close_reason: TDD-implemented public-bind guard in crates/plsql-doc/src/serve.rs mirroring plsql-mcp/src/tcp.rs (is_safe_bind + guard_bind). serve_preview_blocking now takes allow_public_bind: bool; refuses 0.0.0.0/:: and routable public addrs by default with BindGuardError -> PermissionDenied io::Error. 13 new bind-guard tests (loopback v4/v6, RFC1918, link-local, ULA v6, unspecified v4/v6 refusal, routable v4 refusal, override unlocks, IO error conversion, end-to-end refusal). cargo test -p plsql-doc: 90+4 pass; cargo clippy -D warnings: clean.

All dynamic text XML-escaped (&<>"'), XML-1.0-forbidden control chars stripped (tab/nl/cr kept), deterministic order from sorted ScanReport. 6 tests (empty well-formed, finding->failure, skip->skipped, special-char+suite-name escaping, count summing, control-char strip); full plsql-sast suite 49 pass; cargo clippy --no-deps -D warnings clean. title: PLSQL-OSS-UX — plsql-bindgen reports 'unknown flag --version' from --robot-docs handler instead of clean --version
description:
CLI: plsql-bindgen (crates/plsql-bindgen/src/main.rs).

What's missing/awkward: 'plsql-bindgen --version' returns 'error: unknown flag "--version"; valid flags: -h, --capabilities, --help, --input, --output, --package, --robot-docs, --robot-json, --target' on stderr exit 1. The error message is structurally good (it lists valid flags — nice) but '--version' is so universally expected that returning an error is a friction point. The capabilities document includes a 'version: "0.1.0"' field, so the data is available.

Why an agent or human would trip on it: Same class as the plsql-mcp/plsql-engine --version finding (oracle-2rf6), but for the hand-rolled parser path. An agent doing 'plsql-bindgen --version' to feature-detect must instead call 'plsql-bindgen --capabilities | jq -r .version'. A trivial '/bin/zsh --version' shell idiom doesn't work here.

Suggested fix: Add a '--version' flag to the hand-rolled parser that prints 'plsql-bindgen <version>' to stdout and exits 0. Add it to the 'valid flags' list. Also: the error message's 'valid flags' list does not include '--version' (correctly), so the listing infrastructure is in place — just add the handler.
close_reason: Added --version/-V handler to hand-rolled parser in crates/plsql-bindgen/src/main.rs. Emits 'plsql-bindgen 0.1.0' (CARGO_PKG_VERSION) to stdout and exits 0. Both --version and -V added to VALID_FLAGS list. Smoke test: 'plsql-bindgen --version' → 'plsql-bindgen 0.1.0' (exit 0); 'plsql-bindgen -V' → same. New unit tests: version_flag_parses_long_and_short, version_flags_are_in_valid_flags_list (both passing). cargo build/test/clippy all green.

title: PLSQL-ENGINE real-estate gap: clean completeness report contradicts empty dep_graph + 53K diagnostics on a private Oracle estate
description: First run of `plsql-engine analyze` against a real Oracle estate (a private Oracle estate: 4251 files, 329MB, 713 .pkg + 3847 .trg + APEX exports). HONEST RESULT: (+) ran clean, exit 0, ZERO panics across the whole estate (tolerant contract + this session's UTF-8 fixes hold on real input — genuine robustness win). (−) but the SEMANTIC output is hollow on real code: dep_graph = 0 nodes / 0 edges, fact_store = 0 facts, dynamic_sql_sites = 0, unresolved_references = 0, db_link_edges = 0, semantic_model = just declaration_count, flow_summary all 0 — WHILE emitting 53,164 diagnostics. ROOT CAUSE: the deep ParseBackend is not wired (parser_backend reports 'Antlr4Rust' but the real path is the text-scanning pre-parser; the ANTLR backend is Java-gated/un-wired per PLSQL-PARSE-000A), so no semantic structure is extracted from real PL/SQL — the product's core 'know what breaks' value (dep graph / facts / lineage) cannot be delivered on a real estate today. HONESTY BUG (the actionable, in-scope part, plan §1.5 Evidence-UX commitment): the CompletenessReport headline says files_parsed_cleanly=4251 / files_recovered=0 / unresolved_references=0 / skipped_token_ratio=0.0 — a misleadingly PRISTINE picture that directly contradicts 53,164 diagnostics and an empty dep_graph/fact_store. The Trust Block must reflect 'we extracted ~nothing semantically here' (high uncertainty), not '0 unknowns'. Repro: plsql-engine analyze <real estate> --out x.json; jq .payload.completeness vs .payload.diagnostics|length vs .payload.dep_graph. Acceptance: completeness/Trust Block honestly reflects diagnostic volume + empty semantic subsystems (no false-clean); + a discovered-from child for wiring the deep backend (PLSQL-PARSE-000A scope).
close_reason:
Honest CompletenessReport/Trust Block landed in 02eb049. RE-RUN EVIDENCE (cargo run -q -p plsql-engine -- analyze ~/projects/private-estate --out /tmp/xc_p2.json; jq .payload.completeness):

BEFORE (false-clean): files_parsed_cleanly:4224, files_recovered:27, skipped_token_ratio:0.0, dynamic_sql_sites:0, opaque_dynamic_sql_sites:0, db_link_edges:0, unresolved_references:0, wrapped_units:0, missing_package_bodies:0 — a pristine all-zeros picture WHILE 6784 diagnostics / 6609 IR_UNCLASSIFIED_DECL.

AFTER (honest): posture:'LowConfidence', objects_unrecognized:6609, diagnostics_total:6784, objects_with_extracted_semantics:4123, extracted_semantics_ratio:0.38417816; wrapped_units/missing_package_bodies/dynamic_sql_sites/opaque_dynamic_sql_sites/db_link_edges/unresolved_references all now {"unmeasured":true} (NOT a misleading 0); schema_version 1.1.0 (additive bump).

Honesty cuts both ways (asserted in tests): clean synthetic corpus still reads Clean/Partial with ratio ~1.0; demo-no-db passes. Gates: cargo test --workspace 1621 passed / 0 suite failures (one TCP-socket flake green on isolated re-run, unrelated — zero plsql-mcp diff); cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings clean. Tests failing-first then green: plsql-core low_extraction_run_is_never_clean / clean_input_still_reads_clean / unmeasured serialization / degraded_when_nothing_understood; plsql-engine bh4p_low_extraction_run_reports_honest_non_clean_completeness + bh4p_clean_corpus_stays_honest_but_healthy. Deep-backend wiring remains separate scope (PLSQL-PARSE-000A); this fix is purely the reporting-layer honesty.

title: PLSQL-CAT-NEW-2 — Add ALL_POLICIES (VPD) loader for fine-grained access security (/oracle audit)
description:
Audit finding from /oracle skill applied to crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs.

Gap. Catalog has no VPD/RLS coverage. ALL_POLICIES (and ALL_POLICY_CONTEXTS, ALL_POLICY_GROUPS) define DBMS_RLS predicates that change the effective table contents per session — a SELECT against a VPD-protected table reads through a generated WHERE clause. Lineage that ignores VPD will report the table as having only the columns/rows we see; auditors see the policy as silent.

Source. /oracle skill — DATABASE-REFERENCE.md routing for security options + SECURITY-OPTIONS-REFERENCE.md (VPD). Local catalog file: crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs around the load_catalog_grants block at 2571.

Proposed shape. load_catalog_policies(conn, scope) -> Vec<VpdPolicy> over ALL_POLICIES (OBJECT_OWNER, OBJECT_NAME, POLICY_GROUP, POLICY_NAME, PF_OWNER, PACKAGE, FUNCTION, SEL/INS/UPD/DEL/IDX flags, CHK_OPTION, ENABLE, STATIC_POLICY, POLICY_TYPE). Plumbed into CatalogSnapshot::vpd_policies. Lineage flags VPD-protected objects with UnknownReason::VpdPolicyApplied so downstream consumers know the read surface is dynamic.

Cite. Oracle Database Reference 26, ALL_POLICIES view + Database Security Guide chapter on VPD.
close_reason: Added VpdPolicy {object_owner, object_name, policy_group, policy_name, function_owner, function_package, function_name, on_select, on_insert, on_update, on_delete, enabled} struct + SchemaCatalog::vpd_policies (behind #[serde(default)]). load_catalog_vpd_policies queries ALL_POLICIES filtered by object_owner. apply_vpd_policy_row decodes YN/YES/NO mix from dictionary text. Wired between load_catalog_editioning_views and load_catalog_dependencies. 3 orchestration fixtures updated. Test verifies BILLING_TENANT_ISOLATION policy on INVOICES routed through SECURITY.RLS_PKG.TENANT_PREDICATE with SELECT-only flags + enabled correctly decoded. 28 catalog tests pass; clippy --all-targets -D warnings clean. Cite: /oracle skill audit, SECURITY-OPTIONS-REFERENCE.md (VPD chapter); Oracle Database Reference 26 ALL_POLICIES view.

title: PLSQL-OSS-SEC — audit_insert_sql interpolates unvalidated table_name into SQL (identifier injection)
description:
FILE: crates/plsql-mcp/src/audit.rs:100-104 (with_audit_table), 155-165 (audit_insert_sql)

THREAT (SQL identifier injection): audit_insert_sql builds the audit-row INSERT by string interpolation:
  format!("insert into {table} (tool_name, agent_program, agent_model, session_id, recorded_at) values (:1,:2,:3,:4, systimestamp)")
'table' is self.sink.table_name. The doc comments CLAIM the value is validated — line 60 says 'the AuditPlan builder verifies the value is non-empty + matches a simple OWNER.NAME or NAME shape' — but with_audit_table (line 102) does NO validation whatsoever; it just stores the string. audit_insert_sql only trims and checks non-empty. So whatever ends up in table_name (a connections.toml field, a config value, or an agent-supplied argument once the tool is wired) is spliced verbatim into executable SQL.

A table_name carrying extra columns + a statement terminator + a second DDL/DML statement + a line comment, or a DBLINK suffix (X@SOMELINK), or an embedded subquery, escapes the intended INSERT shape. The VALUES are correctly bound (:1..:4) but the table IDENTIFIER itself is not — identifier positions cannot be bind-parameterised in Oracle, which is exactly why the doc demanded a SIMPLE_SQL_NAME-style validation that was never implemented. patch.rs::is_simple_sql_name is the existing, correct validator for this in the same crate.

FIX: Validate table_name in with_audit_table (or a builder finalisation step) before it can reach audit_insert_sql. For an OWNER.NAME shape: split on exactly one '.', run each half through is_simple_sql_name (reuse patch.rs's, or lift to a shared module). Reject anything else. Make audit_insert_sql return None/error for an unvalidated name. Fix the false doc claim either way.

SEVERITY: Medium — DDL/DML injection into the audit path; the audit table is operator-configured today (lower attacker control) but the doc-vs-code divergence means a future wiring that lets an agent set it would be directly exploitable, and the gap silently contradicts a stated invariant.
close_reason: audit.rs with_audit_table now validates table_name (strict OWNER.NAME/NAME identifier shape, each segment a DBMS_ASSERT.SIMPLE_SQL_NAME) and returns Option<Self>, rejecting None on injection. is_valid_audit_table_name + is_simple_sql_name added; false doc claim on AuditSink::table_name corrected. New tests with_audit_table_accepts_valid_identifiers and with_audit_table_rejects_identifier_injection (13 attack vectors: terminators, @DBLINK, extra columns, subquery, double-dot, etc). All green.

title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT — README over-promises the MCP server's tool-execution capability
description:
README.md:270-272 states: 'The MCP server (plsql-mcp) is a single crate exposing the full tool surface: foundation static-analysis tools, change-impact and release-gate tools, and the feature-gated live-DB surface.' README.md:333-339 documents 'plsql-mcp serve' / 'serve --listen ADDR' as 'stdio MCP loop' / 'TCP MCP loop' with no caveat.

But the MCP tool-execution path is NOT implemented: crates/plsql-mcp/src/mcp_protocol.rs:206-236 handle_tools_call validates the tool name against the registry and then returns a STATIC placeholder ('tool {name} is registered but execution is gated on the per-tool MCP-LIVE wiring') for EVERY tool. main.rs:4 confirms 'serve reaches into the transport layer once the per-tool beads land'. So an MCP client can connect, run initialize and tools/list, but every tools/call returns a non-executing stub.

(NOTE: the handle_tools_call placeholder itself is already tracked as a known issue and is NOT being re-filed here. This bead is specifically about the README/docs over-claiming.)

Why it matters: the README presents plsql-mcp as a working MCP server 'exposing the full tool surface'. A user who installs it and wires it into Cursor/Claude Desktop will find tool discovery works but no tool actually runs. For an OSS release this is a documentation-honesty gap -- the prose promises a capability the binary does not yet have.

Suggested fix: until the per-tool dispatch is wired, the README should state plainly that the MCP server currently supports the discovery handshake (initialize / tools/list) and that tool *execution* is in progress / not yet wired. Match the honest framing already used elsewhere in the tree (e.g. plsql-mcp/src/lib.rs:8 'Per-tool engine integration lands in subsequent beads', and the honest SKIP language in PUBLISHING.md).
close_reason: README MCP claim corrected: now states the server completes the handshake + advertises the full surface over tools/list, with per-tool tools/call wiring in progress; matching note added to Limitations. No longer overclaims.

Each emits AstDecl::Ddl{kind, span} with a meaningful kind label (e.g. 'ALTER TABLE', 'DROP INDEX', 'GRANT SELECT', 'REVOKE SELECT', 'COMMENT ON'). New helper lower_simple_ddl consolidates the verb+target pattern. 7 new unit tests cover each verb individually + a mixed-DDL roundtrip; 40 parser-antlr tests pass; clippy --no-deps + fmt clean.

title: PLSQL-OSS-MOCK2 — plsql-support::minimize_repro is a publicly-exported skeleton module; plan_minimize emits hardcoded 5-step boilerplate, never minimises
description: crates/plsql-support/src/minimize_repro.rs:1-21 (module-level docstring) and :89-148 (plan_minimize / plan_for_blob). The module docstring explicitly self-declares: 'plsql support minimize-repro skeleton' and 'the actual ddmin / delta-debug loop lands in a follow-up bead' and 'The actual byte-level minimisation isn't implemented here'. plan_for_blob always returns the exact same hardcoded vec of 5 MinimizationStep entries regardless of input bytes — it's not a plan, it's a static template echoed for every blob. The module is reexported (`pub use minimize_repro::{...}`) in crates/plsql-support/src/lib.rs so it appears in the crate's public surface. No CLI or MCP tool calls plan_minimize in this workspace, but the module is publicly available and shows up in rustdoc as 'minimize_repro'. An OSS reviewer auditing plsql-support would find a 260-line module whose docs say 'follow-up bead' and whose code is a fixed 5-step template — a textbook placeholder. Suggested fix: either land the real ddmin/delta-debug loop now, or downgrade visibility to `pub(crate)` / remove from `pub use` and add a `#[doc(hidden)]` until the worker lands. Also strip the 'skeleton' framing from the docstring or replace with explicit 'planning-only' wording that doesn't promise more than the function delivers.
close_reason: Picked option (b): no external consumers of plan_minimize exist in the workspace (grep clean), so renamed plan_minimize -> minimize_repro_plan, dropped the pub use minimize_repro::{...} re-export from crate root (keeping the module pub for in-workspace opt-in), and rewrote the module-level + function-level + helper docs to honestly describe the limited scope: a content-independent structural plan template (canonical strategy ordering) emitted for downstream consumers; no byte-level minimisation is performed by this module. All 'skeleton' / 'follow-up bead' language removed. Verified: cargo build -p plsql-support OK; cargo test -p plsql-support 95 passed; cargo clippy -p plsql-support --all-targets -- -D warnings OK.

title: PLSQL-SAST-003 — Implement SEC001 (`EXECUTE IMMEDIATE` injection) using flow taint/string-shape evidence + tests
close_reason: Implemented Sec001ExecuteImmediateInjection in crates/plsql-sast/src/rules.rs (commit e67b3e7). For each DynamicSqlEvidence site: taint-reachable via FlowQuery::taint_of (uncleansed) => Critical injection finding with taint-kinds + USING/DBMS_ASSERT remediation; Opacity fact on site => skip OpaqueConstruct (R13 — never assert safe on opaque); no string/taint evidence => skip MissingFlowFacts (R13); pure Literal/Empty StringShape => provably safe (no finding/skip). Non-tainted concatenation deliberately deferred to SEC002 so SEC001 only asserts proven taint-to-sink (high precision). required_facts=[DynamicSqlEvidence], default completeness (intra-unit flow). 5 tests including a REAL taint built through plsql_ir::analyze_flow over lowered statements (tainted->finding / opaque->skip / no-evidence->skip / literal->safe / no-dynsql->required-facts gate). Full plsql-sast suite 27 pass; cargo clippy --no-deps -D warnings clean. Cross-checked vs /oracle SECURITY-OPTIONS-REFERENCE.md (SQL injection/dynamic SQL) + DATABASE-REFERENCE.md (EXECUTE IMMEDIATE, DBMS_ASSERT cleansers).

It calls plsql_catalog::synthetic::billing_schema() (already used by 4 unit tests) and exports through plsql_catalog::export_snapshot_to_json so the fixtures are byte-identical-on-rerun until the synthetic builder itself changes. Ran the example once: emitted corpus/lab/snapshots/l1_billing.json (1 schema, 9 objects — 4 tables, 1 view, 2 packages, 1 procedure, 1 sequence; schema_id=plsql.catalog.snapshot, schema_version=1.1.0), corpus/lab/snapshots/l2_billing_extended.json (slot reserved, currently mirrors L1 until the L2 corpus grows), and corpus/lab/plscope/l1_billing.json (PL/Scope skeleton with empty identifier/reference/statement arrays + IdentifiersAndStatements availability). corpus/lab/README.md documents the refresh command + the round-trip guarantee via the existing catalog_snapshot_round_trips_through_versioned_json_document test (so any future schema bump breaks the test before it breaks consumers). corpus-license-check passes (56 entries / 1 enforced root; corpus/lab/ is not an enforced root per the existing policy).

title: PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-008 — Implement named safety profiles (`inspect_only` default, `ddl_guarded`, `session_write_enabled`) + read-only-by-default session state + `enable_writes` requiring operator confirmation token + `disable_writes`
close_reason: Extended plsql-mcp::safety with SessionSafetyState wrapping the SafetyProfile from MCP-001. Session-level surface: profile (active SafetyProfile, defaults to inspect_only), session_writes_enabled (read-only-by-default), permanently_read_only (hard guard carried from ConnectionProfile), active_token (Option<EnableWritesToken>). EnableWritesToken carries token string + connection + operation_summary + issued_at (unix seconds) + ttl_seconds; ENABLE_WRITES_TOKEN_TTL_SECONDS = 60s default per §13A.3. State transitions: mint_token (refused for permanently_read_only, otherwise creates fresh token + invalidates prior), enable_writes(token, connection, now) (single-use; clears active_token on success, also on expired-token detection so a fresh mint is required; rejects mismatched token/connection without consuming), disable_writes (idempotent — returns AlreadyReadOnly when not write-enabled; success reverts profile to InspectOnly + clears token). writes_allowed() centralizes the gate; permanently_read_only always returns false regardless of any other state. Four new tool descriptors registered: current_safety_profile, set_safety_profile, enable_writes, disable_writes (ToolTier::FoundationLiveDb). 7 new unit tests cover: default state, valid-token consume + single-use, expired token + active_token cleared, permanently_read_only refusal (mint AND enable), token-string mismatch, connection mismatch, disable_writes idempotency, and disable_writes profile revert. 30/30 mcp tests pass; clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings clean.

title: PLSQL-DEPS-002 — Bump toml 0.8 -> 1.1 with TOML 1.1 spec support (/library-updater)
description:
Audit finding from /library-updater pass on Cargo.toml + Cargo.lock.

Gap. toml crate is at 0.8.23 (latest 0.8.x); 1.1.2+spec-1.1.0 is GA. Used by:
- crates/plsql-mcp/Cargo.toml:31: toml = "0.8"

A 1.x bump is a major version change and brings TOML 1.1 spec support (inline tables, integer/float corner cases). plsql-mcp's config loading is the only consumer; the new toml::value::Value API may differ slightly.

Audit needed:
1. Scan plsql-mcp/src/config.rs for toml:: imports — check Deserialize derive vs ValueAccess trait churn between 0.8 and 1.x.
2. Verify that downstream consumers of plsql-mcp don't expose the toml::Value type in any public API (if they did, this becomes a breaking surface for plsql-intelligence consumers too).
3. Run plsql-mcp tests after bump — config-file round trip is the highest-leverage smoke test.

P3 because no known CVE on toml 0.8.x at audit time and the config-loading surface is small.

Cite. toml-rs 1.0 release notes (https://github.com/toml-rs/toml/releases) — verify before bumping.

title: PLSQL-OSS-UX — usr-loop missing 'capabilities' and 'robot-docs' subcommands (Axioms 8/9)
description:
CLI: usr-loop (tools/usr-loop/src/main.rs).

What's missing/awkward: 'usr-loop capabilities' returns 'error: unrecognized subcommand capabilities'; 'usr-loop robot-docs' same. The 'doctor' subcommand DOES print a sizable JSON document with exit_codes/schema/dependency_posture — so the data exists — but an agent that doesn't already know 'doctor doubles as capabilities' will not find it. Both plsql-mcp and plsql-engine HAVE these subcommands; usr-loop is the outlier (P5/P6 of the spec emphasizes agent-driven invocation, so this is high-leverage to fix).

Why an agent or human would trip on it: Per Axiom 9, every agent-targeted CLI should expose 'capabilities' + 'robot-docs'. usr-loop has the highest density of subcommands of any binary in this repo (scan/cluster/propose/ledger {append|verify|index|tripwire}/gate/land/doctor + many flags). An agent landing on this binary cold should be able to bootstrap from 'usr-loop --robot-triage' or 'usr-loop capabilities' or 'usr-loop robot-docs' — none of those exist today.

Suggested fix: (1) Add 'usr-loop capabilities' that splits the current 'doctor' JSON into the agent-contract half (schema, subcommands, exit_codes, global flags) vs the health half (blockers, dependency_posture). (2) Add 'usr-loop robot-docs' that prints the doc-comment block from main.rs (stages [A]–[G], exit-code dictionary, how to drive the loop). (3) Optional: a '--robot-triage' mega-call returning {capabilities, health, quick_ref} like plsql-mcp's. Without this, agents have to read the source to learn the surface.
close_reason: usr-loop gains 'capabilities' and 'robot-docs' subcommands plus a '--robot-triage' global mega-flag (mirrors plsql-mcp shape). 'capabilities' splits the agent contract (subcommands, schemas, exit_codes, global_flags) from the health half (dependency posture, blockers). 'robot-docs' is the paste-ready handbook covering stages [A]–[G]. '--robot-triage' returns {capabilities, health, quick_ref} in one round-trip; exit 2 if doctor reports a blocker. Doctor JSON refactored into shared doctor_report_json() so the standalone and triage paths cannot diverge. Drift-guard tests pin contract_version=1, single-line JSON, subcommand set must match clap enum. Verified: 'usr-loop capabilities' OK, 'usr-loop robot-docs' OK, 'usr-loop --robot-triage' OK. 16 tests pass (9 unit + 7 integration: scan, cluster_ledger, error_envelope_integration with 5 new).

title: PLSQL-OSS-SEC — MCP TCP transport binds RFC1918/link-local with zero authentication
description:
FILE: crates/plsql-mcp/src/tcp.rs:96-104 (is_safe_bind), 142-200 (process_stream / serve)

THREAT (unauthenticated remote access to the tool surface): parse_listen_target refuses 0.0.0.0/:: and public IPs by default (good) but treats every RFC1918 private address (10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16), every IPv4 link-local (169.254/16), and IPv6 ULA/link-local as 'safe by default' — bindable with NO --allow-public-bind flag. The TCP accept loop (serve_with_listener -> handle_connection -> process_stream) performs NO authentication, NO token, NO TLS, NO peer check: any line-delimited JSON-RPC request from any host that can reach that ip:port is dispatched straight into handle_request_line and the full ToolRegistry.

On a shared LAN, a corporate VPN subnet, a cloud VPC, or a multi-tenant container network, 'private' is not 'trusted' — any co-resident host can drive the MCP server. The whole live-DB tool family (connect, query, patch_*, create_or_replace, execute_approved, enable_writes) is reachable. The skill's Axiom 6 applies: there is no cryptographic verification of who the peer is; reachability == authorization.

Also note: --allow-public-bind is referenced in the error text and tcp.rs but is NOT wired as a CLI flag in main.rs (run_serve hardcodes parse_listen_target(&raw, false)), so today public bind is simply impossible — but the private-range hole is wide open and needs no flag.

FIX (pick at least one, layered):
1. Default-deny everything except loopback (127.0.0.0/8, ::1); require an explicit opt-in flag for ANY non-loopback bind, RFC1918 included. The 'one agent per process / local dev transport' model in the module doc is a loopback model.
2. If non-loopback binds are intentionally supported, require a shared-secret / bearer token on each JSON-RPC frame (constant-time compared) before dispatch, or mutual-TLS.
3. Document prominently in README/PUBLISHING.md that --listen is loopback-only-safe and any wider bind exposes an unauthenticated tool surface.

SEVERITY: Medium-High depending on deployment — for an OSS release the safe default and the doc warning are the blockers; today's loopback+private default is too permissive for an unauthenticated protocol.
close_reason: Tightened tcp.rs is_safe_bind to loopback-only (ip.is_loopback()). RFC1918 (10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16), IPv4 link-local (169.254/16), IPv6 ULA (fc00::/7) and link-local now require explicit --allow-public-bind. Updated PublicBindRefused message, parse_listen_target/module doc, describe() tag (loopback-only), the --allow-public-bind CLI flag doc and serve help text to state the transport is unauthenticated and a non-loopback bind exposes the full tool surface. TDD: rewrote 3 tests that asserted the vulnerable RFC1918/link-local-accepted behavior into private_rfc1918_refused/link_local_refused/unique_local_v6_refused + added rfc1918_and_link_local_bind_with_override; watched 4 fail, then GREEN. cargo build/test/clippy green.

Rule trait extended with DEFAULTED required_facts()+minimum_completeness() (non-breaking — all existing SAST-001 rules/tests compile + pass unchanged). title: PLSQL-OSS-UX — plan-lint / corpus-bench / corpus-grow / corpus-license-check lack capabilities + robot-docs + --version
description:
CLIs: tools/plan-lint, tools/corpus-bench, tools/corpus-grow, tools/corpus-license-check (all hand-rolled arg parsers).

What's missing/awkward: None of these four tools has a 'capabilities' subcommand or '--capabilities' flag; none has 'robot-docs' or '--robot-docs'; none accepts '--version' (it errors as 'unknown argument'). They all DO have a clean '--help', a documented exit-code dictionary in --help, and a '--robot-json' that emits a stable-schema (schema_id+schema_version) envelope — so they are halfway there. The pattern plsql-bindgen uses ('--capabilities' and '--robot-docs' as flags on a hand-rolled parser) is already proven inside the same codebase and is a 1-file lift per tool.

Why an agent or human would trip on it: An agent calling 'plan-lint --version' to feature-detect or pin a schema version gets an error. There is no in-tool machine-readable contract; the exit-code dictionary lives only in the --help string (no schema_id, no per-tool stable JSON). Agents must parse --help to know the schema_id; this works but is fragile across versions.

Suggested fix: For each of the four tools, copy the plsql-bindgen pattern: add '--capabilities' returning the contract JSON ({binary, version, flags, exit_codes, stdout_contract, schema_id of the robot-json output}) and '--robot-docs' returning a paste-ready handbook. Add '--version' (print '<name> <version>' to stdout, exit 0). One commit per tool, each ~40 lines.
close_reason: Added --capabilities, --robot-docs, --version/-V to plan-lint, corpus-bench, corpus-grow, corpus-license-check (mirrors plsql-bindgen pattern: hand-rolled parser, contract_version pinned by drift-guard tests, single-line JSON valid). Each binary gains a CAPABILITIES_CONTRACT_VERSION=1, capabilities_json() with binary/contract_version/version/flags/exit_codes/stdout_contract/report_schema, robot_docs_text() handbook, and version banner. Drift-guard + parse-side tests pin every contract. Verified: 'plan-lint --version' → 'plan-lint 0.1.0', 'corpus-bench --capabilities' → valid JSON envelope, 'corpus-license-check --robot-docs' → paste-ready handbook. cargo build/test/clippy all green (77 tests across the 5 target crates).

Config channel: SuppressionConfig.global_rules + per_path_rules. Inline channel: '-- plsql-scan:ignore RULE[,RULE]' => same source line, '-- plsql-scan:ignore-next-line RULE' => following line, '*'/'all' => any rule; marker case-insensitive and MUST be inside a -- comment (literal containing marker text does not suppress, proven by test); comma/space/tab rule lists; audit trail records directive's own comment line + same-line vs next-line kind. Deterministic: suppressed sorted (rule_id,file,line); skipped/counts pass through. 11 tests (config global/per-path, inline same/next-line incl reason+comment_line, wildcard, non-match kept, marker-in-string ignored, list parsing, sort determinism, JSON round-trip); full plsql-sast suite 59 pass; cargo clippy --no-deps -D warnings clean. title: PLSQL-OSS-UX — usr-loop top-level subcommand help text mixes Markdown bold (**...**) into plain TTY output
description:
CLI: usr-loop (tools/usr-loop/src/main.rs).

What's missing/awkward: The doc-comments for several usr-loop subcommands embed Markdown ('**cluster/dedup**', '**Stage [F] LAND**', '**§4 monotonic tripwire**') which clap copies verbatim into '--help' output. In a plain terminal these render as literal asterisks ('**Stage [F] LAND**'). This affects: cluster, land, ledger tripwire, and the long descriptions of gate / propose.

Why an agent or human would trip on it: An agent reading 'usr-loop --help' sees noisy '**text**' tokens that aren't real syntax. It's a small cosmetic issue, but it makes the help block harder to scan and risks confusing an agent that thinks the asterisks are positional argument shorthand. plsql-mcp/plsql-engine help text avoids this.

Suggested fix: Strip the '**' bold markers in the doc-comments for the affected enum variants (they're only useful in source-code reading). Alternatively, use clap's long_about/about split so the bold-styled text lives only in long_about (Markdown-friendly when rendered, e.g. man pages) and the short about used in --help is plain. Affects ~6 sites in tools/usr-loop/src/main.rs.
close_reason: Stripped literal Markdown bold markers (**...**) from usr-loop /// doc-comments that clap renders into --help. Removed '**cluster/dedup**' (Cluster), '**Stage [F] LAND**' (Land), '**public corpus**' (Index), '**§4 monotonic tripwire**' (Tripwire), and also '**deterministic stub proposer**' / '**public corpus**' in non-clap-rendered doc-comments so source readers and clap output stay consistent. New regression test clap_help_is_markdown_free renders Cli::command().write_long_help() and asserts the output contains zero literal '**' tokens; fails loudly if Markdown bold leaks back in. Verified: 'usr-loop --help' shows clean 'cluster/dedup' / 'Stage [F] LAND' / etc.

title: PLSQL-CAT-NEW-3 — Add ALL_EDITIONS + ALL_EDITIONING_VIEWS for EBR awareness (/oracle audit)
description:
Audit finding from /oracle skill applied to crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs.

Gap. Catalog flattens to the current edition only. Edition-Based Redefinition (EBR) lets a single object exist in multiple editions with different bodies — DEFAULT_EDITION and child editions. ALL_OBJECTS already shows EDITION_NAME, but we don't fetch ALL_EDITIONS (the edition tree) or ALL_EDITIONING_VIEWS (the editioning view shape that masks the underlying table). Lineage in an EBR shop will miss the version-pinned alternatives.

Source. /oracle skill — DATABASE-REFERENCE.md routing for EBR + the existing CatalogSnapshot.edition column on objects. Local file: crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs:1977 (load_catalog_objects already SELECTs edition_name from all_objects).

Proposed shape. Two loaders:
- load_catalog_editions(conn) -> Vec<Edition> over ALL_EDITIONS (EDITION_NAME, PARENT_EDITION_NAME, USABLE).
- load_catalog_editioning_views(conn, scope) -> Vec<EditioningView> over ALL_EDITIONING_VIEWS (OWNER, VIEW_NAME, TABLE_NAME).
Plumbed into CatalogSnapshot::editions / ::editioning_views. CompletenessReport gains an UnknownReason::EditionFlattened when an object has child editions we did not enumerate.

Cite. Oracle Database Reference 26, ALL_EDITIONS and ALL_EDITIONING_VIEWS views + Database Development Guide chapter on EBR.
close_reason: Added Edition {edition_name, parent_edition_name, usable} + EditioningView {owner, view_name, table_name}, CatalogSnapshot::editions (database-wide) and SchemaCatalog::editioning_views (per-schema, both behind #[serde(default)]). Two new loaders: load_catalog_editions (no schema filter — database-wide), load_catalog_editioning_views (per-schema). Wired into build_catalog_snapshot between table_comments and dependencies. 3 orchestration fixtures updated (the fragment-loop variants special-case all_editions to use no params). Test asserts ORA root + PATCH_2026_05 child + INVOICES_E1 editioning view all materialize with correct parent edges. 28 catalog tests pass; clippy --all-targets -D warnings clean. Cite: /oracle skill audit, Oracle Database Reference 26 ALL_EDITIONS + ALL_EDITIONING_VIEWS; Database Development Guide chapter on EBR.

title: PLSQL-OSS-MOCK2 — plsql-lineage::classify_git_diff public docstring self-declares 'This is a stub' even though function is real (limited-scope) work; doc-rot misleads OSS reviewers
description: crates/plsql-lineage/src/lib.rs:442-446. The public `pub fn classify_git_diff` docstring reads: 'This is a stub — full semantic classification requires the parser (Layer 1). Currently classifies by file extension and diff status.' The function actually does real, contract-satisfying work: it runs `git diff --name-status`, walks the lines, and emits typed ChangeRecord::{Created,Dropped,Body} variants by leading status byte (with R/C rename/copy handled). It's not a stub — it's a coarse-grained classifier whose 'semantic' work is limited to file-system status, by design. But because the docstring says 'This is a stub', an OSS reviewer / agent reading rustdoc concludes the function shouldn't be relied on, and the bug-hunting heuristic flags it as a placeholder. classify_dir_diff (called by the MCP classify_change tool via change_tools.rs) has the same shape but no 'stub' framing. Suggested fix: rewrite the docstring to describe what the function actually does ('Classifies changes between two git refs by parsing the file-status output of `git diff --name-status`; emits Created/Dropped/Body records per PL/SQL file. Body records carry git-ref hashes (`git:<ref>`) only, not content-based body diffs') and drop the 'This is a stub' sentence; if richer semantic classification is desired, file a separate bead for that work and add a 'See also <bead-id> for parser-aware classification' line. No code change required, just doc-string accuracy.
close_reason: Rewrote the classify_git_diff docstring to honestly describe what it does today: a structural git-diff classifier that shells to 'git diff --name-status', emits ChangeRecord::{Created,Dropped,Body} per PL/SQL file by leading status byte (A/D/M, with R*/C* rename/copy decomposed into Dropped(old)+Created(new) so renames aren't treated as body changes), stamps old_run_id / new_run_id with the supplied refs, and intentionally does NOT body-diff (parser-level semantic body classification is out of scope). The 'This is a stub' lie is gone. Verified: cargo build -p plsql-lineage OK; cargo test -p plsql-lineage all green; cargo clippy -p plsql-lineage --all-targets -- -D warnings OK (no broken intra-doc links — original draft referenced the private is_plsql_file fn, rewritten to plain prose to keep rustdoc clean).

title: PLSQL-LIN-022 — Lab fixture: deliberately orphan-vs-not-orphan packages in `corpus/lab/` (L2 expansion) with golden expected report
PLSQL-OSS-UX — plsqld treats --help as a cache-dir path (Axiom 0 violation)
description:
CLI: plsqld (crates/plsql-store/src/bin/plsqld.rs).

What's missing/awkward: 'plsqld --help' produces 'plsqld: cache directory --help does not exist (create it explicitly first)' and exits 2. Same for '-h', '--version', '--robot-json'. Bare 'plsqld' (no args) prints 'usage: plsqld <cache-dir>' exit 2 — that's tolerable, but every flag-shaped argument is mis-parsed as a literal cache-dir path. There is no 'capabilities', no 'robot-docs', no 'doctor', no env-var documentation.

Why an agent or human would trip on it: An agent calling 'plsqld --help' to discover the daemon's surface gets a confusing error that suggests creating a literally-named '--help' directory. Worst-case, a less careful agent might 'mkdir --help' then re-run. The error message points the wrong way. Also: once started ('plsqld /tmp/foo'), it blocks indefinitely listening on a Unix socket with zero discovery surface — no banner explaining how to talk to it.

Suggested fix: Treat any first-arg starting with '-' as a flag. Add a real '--help' that documents the positional cache-dir, the socket path it creates, the wire protocol, plus '--version' and an inline pointer to docs. Add 'capabilities --json' even if it just returns {binary, version, listen_socket_template, exit_codes}. Optional: 'plsqld doctor /tmp/foo' to inspect an existing store.
close_reason: plsqld gains --help/-h, --version/-V, --capabilities, --robot-docs (mirrors plsql-bindgen pattern). Any first-arg starting with '-' is treated as a flag before any cache-dir parsing — closes the regression where 'plsqld --help' tried to create a directory literally called '--help'. Dedicated regression test (help_does_not_attempt_to_create_a_help_directory) pins that exact bad-message string against future drift. Capabilities JSON documents the Unix-socket-only network footprint (R17), the wire protocol (serve_envelope), and the listen_socket_template. Unknown-flag handler points at --help instead of misinterpreting. 12 new tests pass (3 lib + 9 integration spawning the binary).

title: PLSQL-OSS-UX — plsql-mcp bare invocation runs an undocumented doctor-equivalent on stdout
description:
CLI: plsql-mcp (crates/plsql-mcp/src/main.rs).

What's missing/awkward: Bare 'plsql-mcp' (no subcommand) prints 3 lines (version banner + WARN/INFO findings) to stdout and exits 0. This is the same content as 'plsql-mcp doctor' (human mode). The behavior is undocumented in --help (which says the COMMAND is optional but never describes what 'no command' means). Capabilities lists 4 commands; bare invocation matches none of them.

Why an agent or human would trip on it: An agent that runs 'plsql-mcp' to 'see if it works' gets diagnostic output on stdout with exit 0 — looks like success, but the content is warnings ('LD_LIBRARY_PATH not set', 'no connections.toml'). An MCP harness that auto-launches the binary expecting JSON-RPC over stdio (because '--help' says 'stdio by default') will instead get human-readable WARN/INFO text on stdout and the process exits — no server. The implicit 'no-command = doctor' shortcut also violates Axiom 4 (stdout should be data; these are diagnostics).

Suggested fix: One of (a) make bare invocation exit 2 with 'usage: ...; for the canonical agent start, run: plsql-mcp --robot-triage' on stderr; (b) make bare invocation start the stdio server (matches --help's wording of 'stdio by default' and matches what an MCP launcher expects); or (c) explicitly document the 'no-command = doctor' shortcut in --help and route the output to stderr / exit 2 if blocker found. Pick one and pin a regression test.
close_reason: Fixed: bare 'plsql-mcp' no longer silently runs doctor on stdout. Now prints clap long help + a 'no subcommand' hint to stderr and exits 2. stdout stays empty (verified: 0 bytes). Regression test main.rs::bare_invocation_does_not_silently_run_doctor_on_stdout pins the contract.
