title: OMCP — oraclemcp: open-source Oracle MCP server in Rust (engine-backed, safe-by-default)
description:
ROOT EPIC — the whole oraclemcp initiative. Read this first; it is the single source of project context for every descendant bead.

## What we are building
A long-lived **MCP server** (Model Context Protocol, JSON-RPC 2.0) that exposes an Oracle database to AI coding agents through a small, well-designed tool surface, with **deep PL/SQL intelligence** (real ANTLR parser -> IR -> dependency graph -> lineage -> SAST -> change-impact) and **fail-closed safety**. Harness-agnostic: speaks stdio + Streamable HTTP, so Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, Hermes, Cursor, VS Code Agent Mode etc. all use it with no bespoke integration.

## Strategic frame (load-bearing decisions)
- **Improve in place now, extract later.** All work happens inside the existing `plsql-intelligence` Cargo workspace (the `crates/plsql-mcp` crate + the `plsql-*` engine crates). We do NOT split repos or publish to crates.io yet. Reason: the split-and-publish-back is a one-way door (semver treadmill, doubled CI); doing the same modular extraction inside one workspace first is a two-way door that yields 100% of the modularity with none of the coordination tax. Extraction to a separate `oraclemcp` repo + crates.io publish is deferred to the trigger-gated Phase E.
- **Naming:** the repo/platform stays `plsql-intelligence`; **`oraclemcp` is the name of the MCP-server binary** (the search-term-owning surface). Market as MCP, build as platform.
- **License:** uniformly Apache-2.0 OR MIT. No commercial/FSL tier (the former plsql-mcp-pro idea is dropped).
- **One full product binary.** The shipped `oraclemcp` binary ALWAYS includes the full engine; the engine is never an optional build-out. The only feature toggle is `live-db` (the Oracle driver + Instant Client dependency): with it off you get a pure offline static-analysis server with zero native deps. The `oraclemcp-*` core crates and `plsql-*` engine crates are also reusable libraries (e.g. `cargo add plsql-parser-antlr`).
- **One-way dependency, enforced now:** the MCP/core depends on the engine; the engine never depends on the MCP. Engine tools implement the core's Tool/registry trait. A CI dependency-lint enforces this so Phase E extraction is mechanical.

## The differentiator + the safety spine
- **Engine-aware fail-closed classifier:** because we ship a real PL/SQL call-graph, the classifier can PROVE purity (never assume it). A statement clears to `Safe` only on an explicit `ProvenReadOnly` verdict; unknown/`Unmeasured`/opaque-dynamic/cycle -> fail-closed. This catches `SELECT side_effecting_fn() FROM dual`, side-effecting triggers/VPD on reads, and `EXECUTE IMMEDIATE` DML that no string/AST classifier can see. No off-the-shelf Oracle MCP can do this.
- **Operating levels (one user, all levels):** READ_ONLY (default) -> READ_WRITE -> DDL -> ADMIN, each escalation gated by an in-band human confirmation (DCG-style / MCP elicitation; device 2FA is OUT OF SCOPE). Per-target `max_level` ceiling; `protected` profiles get three independent locks.
- **Honesty laws:** the allow-once token is friction NOT a control; the server is never a sandbox; NUMBER->string (no f64); impact preview is execute-in-savepoint-then-rollback, not EXPLAIN-PLAN cardinality.

## Connectivity
Thick mode (ODPI-C + Oracle Instant Client) — the only path to wallet/Kerberos/mTLS/OCI IAM/Autonomous DB. Min Oracle 19c through 23ai. Not hermetic (needs Instant Client at runtime; Docker-first distribution amortizes the ~100MB).

## How to read the graph
Children are the 5 roadmap phases (Phase 0 substrate -> Phase 1 v1 -> Phase 2 hardening -> Phase 3 deferred -> Phase E extraction) plus a Testing & Quality epic. Each phase epic links its tasks; tasks carry `blocks` dependencies matching the plan's critical path. The source plan is docs/plans/2026-06-01-oraclemcp-shared-mcp-core.md, but these beads are intended to be self-contained — you should not need it.

## Success criteria for the whole initiative
A narrow, demonstrably-correct, fail-closed read-only core a DBA trusts, that any MCP agent can use over stdio, with the deep PL/SQL intelligence as the headline feature — shipped in place, then (Phase E) extracted and published.
notes: Root epic deferred: all IMPLEMENTABLE scope is closed (542 beads — engine-free oraclemcp-* core, both transports, cloud, custom tools, OAuth RS, TLS/mTLS, doctor, plscope/AWR/schema-diff, plugins, full §12 test gate, all 5 post-passes). The only remaining scope under this epic is Phase E (trigger-gated public extraction/publish), now deferred. Reopen when shipping publicly.

title: OMCP Phase 0 — Correctness substrate (must precede all feature work)
description:
PHASE 0 EPIC — the correctness substrate. Several "P0 features" are not implementable until these foundational choices are built, because they cascade. Nothing involving transactions, savepoints, DBMS_OUTPUT, temp tables, or correct value fidelity is right without them.

## Scope (tasks under this epic)
- P0-0 Boundary refactor: factor the generic, engine-free MCP core into clean modules/crates IN PLACE; CI dependency-lint enforcing the one-way boundary (core never imports plsql-* engine crates). This is a real but bounded refactor (relocating OracleConnection out of plsql-catalog; carving the ~5 engine-coupled tool handlers from the engine-free spine). Its completeness is what later makes Phase E mechanical.
- P0-1 Workspace scaffold + CI (clippy/fmt/nextest/audit/deny) + error envelope (thiserror).
- P0-2 Config + profiles (figment, strict precedence, validation, versioned schema, OCI wallet/IAM fields).
- P0-3 Oracle connectivity (oraclemcp-db): lift OracleConnection/RustOracleConnection, r2d2-oracle pool, spawn_blocking boundary, OCI/cloud connect, doctor posture.
- P0-4 Session-lease primitive (TTL on monotonic clock + forced rollback/return on expiry) — the #1 blocker for session-state coherence.
- P0-5 Type-mapping + NLS canonical serializer (NUMBER->string, ISO-8601, UTF-8) + golden tests.
- P0-6 Adopt rmcp in oraclemcp-core (replace hand-rolled mcp_protocol.rs/tcp.rs); stdio + init-token; oracle_capabilities. Begins the sync->async migration (engine stays sync behind spawn_blocking).
- P0-7 Operating-level core (current/max level, classifier->level mapping, level-gated dispatch).

## Sequencing rationale
P0-1 blocks everything. The async/sync boundary invariant (never hold an oracle::Connection across .await) is established here. The classifier (P1-1) deliberately lands on the EXISTING sync protocol first and is NOT blocked on the rmcp migration (P0-6) — closing the live fail-open footgun is higher priority than the protocol swap.

## Success criteria
Workspace builds green in CI with the dependency-lint passing; a leased session round-trips a typed value with correct NUMBER/NLS fidelity; rmcp stdio server answers oracle_capabilities.
close_reason: OMCP Phase 0 (correctness substrate) complete: all children closed — P0-0 boundary+lint, P0-1 scaffold+error envelope, P0-2 config, P0-3 connectivity (live), P0-4 leases (live), P0-5 type/NLS serializer (live), P0-6 rmcp ServerHandler+capabilities+init-token, P0-7 operating levels, P0-CLK monotonic clocks.

title: P0-0 — Boundary refactor: factor the engine-free MCP core in place + CI dependency-lint
description:
## Background
The future `oraclemcp-*` core crates are extracted from today's `crates/plsql-mcp` + `crates/plsql-catalog`. We do this IN PLACE now (no new repo) so Phase E becomes a mechanical `git filter-repo` + `cargo publish`, not a rewrite. This is the discipline that keeps the whole improve-in-place-then-extract strategy cheap.

## Technical approach
- Relocate the generic, non-PL/SQL pieces into cleanly-bounded modules/crates that import NO `plsql-*` engine crate: protocol, fail-closed guard/classifier, DB connection/pool/lease, audit, config.
- Lift `OracleConnection` + `RustOracleConnection` out of `plsql-catalog` (today they are co-located with `plsql-core` types).
- Carve the ~5 engine-coupled tool handlers (`change_tools.rs`, `parse_tools.rs`, `foundation_tools.rs`, `graph_tools.rs`, `analyze_project.rs`) away from the engine-free spine; they stay engine-side and register Tool impls into the core registry.
- Add a CI dependency-lint that FAILS if any core crate's Cargo.toml depends on a `plsql-*` engine crate (the one-way boundary, §0 hard rule 1).

## Why it's a real refactor (not a Cargo.toml edit)
It moves real code with test risk; budget it as engineering work. Its completeness is exactly what makes Phase E mechanical.

## Success criteria
Core modules/crates compile with zero `plsql-*` engine imports; the CI dependency-lint is green and would fail on a violation; existing plsql-mcp tests still pass.

## Test plan
- Unit: dependency-lint catches an intentionally-violating Cargo.toml.
- Integration: full workspace `cargo build` + existing test suite green after the move.

## Considerations
Do not over-claim "mechanical" for this task itself; only the LATER extraction is mechanical because of this work.
close_reason: P0-0 complete: the engine-free MCP core boundary is established and enforced. (1) 8 oraclemcp-* core crates compile with zero plsql-* engine imports; (2) CI dependency-lint (scripts/oraclemcp_boundary_lint.sh, wired into ci.yml) is green and verified to fail on an injected 'use plsql_engine' violation; (3) existing plsql-mcp tests still pass (233 lib tests green). Concrete first relocation: the generic tool-registry contract (ToolTier/ToolDescriptor/ToolRegistry) moved verbatim to oraclemcp-core with a re-export shim from plsql-mcp/src/tools.rs, establishing the intended plsql-mcp(product binary) -> oraclemcp-core(engine-free core) dependency. Per the improve-in-place discipline, the remaining generic modules (config/db/audit/guard/protocol) are relocated into their target crates by their upgrade beads (P0-2/P0-3/P1-4/P1-1/P0-6) as the upgraded versions land, rather than verbatim-moving code that is about to be rewritten. Recon-verified: only analyze_project.rs + foundation_tools.rs import plsql_engine (both correctly engine-side). Boundary map: docs/plans/oraclemcp-extraction-boundary-map.md.

title: P0-1 — Workspace scaffold + CI gates + structured error envelope
description:
## Background
Foundation that blocks everything. Establishes the CI gates and the error model used by every later bead.

## Technical approach
- Cargo workspace scaffold for the oraclemcp-* core crates (thin-bin-crate / fat-lib-crate split for compile speed).
- CI: `cargo clippy -D warnings`, `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo nextest`, `cargo audit`, `cargo deny`, plus the P0-0 dependency-lint.
- Structured error envelope with `thiserror`; agent-facing errors are `isError:true` with actionable next steps (not JSON-RPC error codes), e.g. ORA-00942 -> {error_class:OBJECT_NOT_FOUND, suggested_tool, fuzzy_matches}.

## Success criteria
CI is green on an empty scaffold; the error envelope type exists and serializes to the documented shape.

## Test plan
- Unit: error envelope serialization golden tests (insta).
- CI: all gates run in the pipeline.

## Considerations
`panic = "abort"`, no `unwrap()` on DB ops (enforced later); set the structured-JSON-line test logging convention here.
close_reason: P0-1 complete: scaffolded 8 engine-free oraclemcp-* core crates (error/config/audit/telemetry/db/guard/auth/core) as workspace members; implemented the structured agent-facing ErrorEnvelope + ErrorClass + ORA-code classifier in oraclemcp-error (7 unit tests, golden serialization isError/error_class/suggested_tool/fuzzy_matches); added CI supply-chain gate (cargo deny + audit, deny.toml) and the one-way boundary dependency-lint (scripts/oraclemcp_boundary_lint.sh, wired into ci.yml). clippy -D warnings + fmt clean on all new crates.

title: P0-2 — Config + connection profiles (figment, precedence, validation, OCI fields)
description:
## Background
A single validated config surface prevents a top operational/security footgun (overlapping ad-hoc config). Profiles are how an agent connects without ever seeing raw credentials or Oracle connection syntax.

## Technical approach
- `figment`-layered config, strict precedence: built-in defaults < config.toml < env (ORACLEMCP_*) < CLI flags. One validated, versioned struct; reject unknown keys; validate at startup, fail fast.
- Named profiles (`~/.config/oraclemcp/profiles.toml`): connect_string (EZConnect / EZConnect-Plus / TNS alias), username, credential_ref, pool settings, login_script path, `max_level` (per-target ceiling, default READ_ONLY), `protected=true`, `read_only_standby`, `base` for inheritance (shallow-merge, cycle-detected). Plus OCI wallet/IAM profile fields.
- Hot-reload (notify) allowed ONLY for non-credential/non-security sections, via Arc<ArcSwap<Config>>; credential/auth/RBAC changes require restart. schema_version for migrations.

## Success criteria
A profiles.toml loads, validates, rejects unknown keys, resolves inheritance; list_profiles returns non-secret metadata (incl. max_level); secrets never materialize into returned metadata.

## Test plan
- Unit: precedence ordering, unknown-key rejection, inheritance cycle detection, validation errors.
- Property tests on config precedence.

## Considerations
default_level/max_level both default READ_ONLY. OCI fields feed P1-11.
close_reason: P0-2 complete: oraclemcp-config — figment-layered, validated, versioned config. OracleMcpConfig{schema_version, profiles} with strict precedence defaults<config.toml<env(ORACLEMCP_*)<CLI (via figment(); from_figment lets the binary merge CLI last). deny_unknown_fields rejects unknown keys; schema_version>SUPPORTED is rejected. ConnectionProfile (connect_string, username, credential_ref, login_script/login_statements, max_level/default_level default READ_ONLY, protected, read_only_standby, pool, OCI wallet/IAM fields, base) with shallow-merge base inheritance resolved + cycle/unknown-base/duplicate detection. Validation: connect_string required post-inheritance; protected profile must pin max_level=READ_ONLY (§6.6). list_profiles() returns ProfileMetadata that excludes credential_ref + username (secret refs never materialized, §8.4). 17 tests (precedence via figment::Jail, unknown-key rejection, inheritance + cycle, validation, secret-not-leaked). clippy -D warnings + fmt clean; boundary lint green.

title: P0-3 — Oracle connectivity (oraclemcp-db): driver + r2d2 pool + spawn_blocking + OCI connect + doctor
description:
## Background
The only place DB I/O happens. The `oracle` crate is synchronous, so all DB I/O must cross a spawn_blocking boundary and an oracle::Connection must NEVER be held across an .await (compiler-enforced by ownership).

## Technical approach
- Lift `OracleConnection` trait + `RustOracleConnection` from plsql-catalog (P0-0); `live-db` Cargo feature gates the driver (off = offline build, no ODPI-C, musl-able).
- `r2d2-oracle` pool: max_size=min(cpu*2+1,20), min_idle=2, acquire timeout 5s, statement_cache_size>=50, ping on recycle, auto-reconnect.
- spawn_blocking boundary with a Semaphore cap so the 512-thread blocking pool can't be exhausted.
- OCI/cloud connect: cloud wallet (mTLS, cwallet.sso + TNS_ADMIN) and OCI IAM database-token paths — no special code path, just profile config.
- `odpic-sys` escape-hatch crate kept for advanced ODPI-C surface not exposed by `oracle` 0.6.x (tracks ODPI-C 5.4.x).
- `oraclemcp doctor` posture report (keep plsql-mcp's existing doctor.rs Instant Client check; full doctor is its own bead).

## Success criteria
Connects to local XE and (in a tagged job) OCI Autonomous DB; pool health-checks on recycle; no Connection crosses an await (verified by ownership + a clippy-style check if feasible).

## Test plan
- Live-XE integration: connect, run a trivial SELECT, recycle, reconnect after listener drop (chaos).
- Unit: pool config defaults; semaphore cap behavior.

## Considerations
glibc target (ODPI-C dlopen incompatible with static musl) for live-db builds.
notes: SCOPE NARROWING (Codex review): P0-3 owns GENERIC ODPI-C/r2d2/local-XE connectivity + profile plumbing + the ability to INITIATE a wallet/IAM connection. The OCI Autonomous-DB HARDENING (wallet discovery, IAM token refresh, ADB connect-string validation, tagged OCI jobs) belongs to P1-11, not here — avoids duplication.
close_reason: P0-3 complete: oraclemcp-db connectivity, validated against live Oracle 23ai. OracleConnection trait (backend/ping/describe/query_rows/execute, binds always bound never interpolated) + RustOracleConnection over the oracle crate (ODPI-C), feature-gated oracle-driver; offline build returns BackendNotCompiled. r2d2 pool (OracleConnectionManager + OraclePool) with the tokio::task::spawn_blocking boundary so an oracle::Connection is never held across .await (ownership-enforced, §4.3). OCI/cloud: wallet folded into an EZConnect-Plus descriptor (no unsafe env::set_var); external/wallet auth path; IAM-token plumbed + typed-deferred to P1-11. detect_instant_client() offline-safe libclntsh posture probe (doctor check 1). DbError (independent of plsql CatalogError -> keeps the one-way boundary) -> ErrorEnvelope via the shared ORA-code classifier. Tests: 9 offline unit (bind/row/standby/error-classify/IC-posture) + live_oracle.rs integration (connect/ping/query/bind/describe + pool/spawn_blocking) which PASSED live: version 23.26.1.0.0, role PRIMARY, open_mode READ WRITE. clippy -D warnings clean offline+driver; fmt clean; boundary lint green. (Live runtime note: LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/tmp/instantclient_23_7 + libaio.so.1 symlink.)

title: P0-4 — Session-lease primitive (TTL + forced rollback/return) — the #1 blocker
description:
## Background
A pool hands out a DIFFERENT physical Oracle session per checkout, but transactions, savepoints, DBMS_OUTPUT, temp tables, package globals, and login-script settings are ALL session-scoped. begin on call 1 + commit on call 2 can land on different sessions = silent corruption that only appears under concurrency and is nearly undebuggable. This is the #1 blocker; nothing stateful is correct without it.

## Technical approach
- A lease = one physical session pinned to one agent for a unit of work: acquire_lease(profile, ttl) -> lease_id; release_lease; renew at 75% TTL.
- Every stateful tool takes an optional lease_id; if omitted -> autocommit-off single-statement mode, and any attempt to open a transaction/savepoint WITHOUT a lease is a STRUCTURED ERROR, never silent best-effort.
- TTL on a MONOTONIC clock (tokio::time::Instant). On expiry: roll back the open transaction, clear session state, return the session to the pool, emit an audit event.
- On acquire: run the profile login script (ALTER SESSION ...) and stamp DBMS_APPLICATION_INFO module/action with the agent identity.
- Stateless reads do NOT need a lease (check out, run, check in).

## Success criteria
A multi-call transaction stays on one physical session; lease expiry with an open txn rolls back (chaos test); no-lease transaction attempt returns a structured error.

## Test plan
- Chaos: lease-TTL expiry with open transaction asserts rollback + session returned.
- Unit: no-lease stateful op -> structured error; monotonic TTL not affected by wall-clock jump.

## Considerations
Multi-agent isolation over HTTP relies on this (per-agent lease = no cross-agent transaction bleed).
close_reason: P0-4 session-lease primitive (oraclemcp-db lease.rs), live-validated. LeaseManager pins one physical Oracle session per lease (the #1 blocker, §5.1): acquire/release/renew + lease_id plumbing (P0-4a); monotonic-deadline TTL (oraclemcp_guard::MonotonicDeadline) with force-rollback + session drop (state clear + return) on expiry, reaped by with_lease + reap_expired (P0-4b); login-script execution + DBMS_APPLICATION_INFO.SET_MODULE agent-identity stamp on acquire (P0-4c); require_lease_id() makes any transaction/savepoint attempt without a lease a structured DbError::LeaseRequired, never a silent best-effort (P0-4d). begin/commit/rollback/savepoint route to the pinned session (savepoint name validated). Driver-free lifecycle over the OracleConnection trait -> 7 mock unit tests; a live test exercises the full lifecycle (acquire->stamp->begin->savepoint->rollback->renew->release) on a real pinned Oracle 23ai session. commit/rollback added to the connection trait + driver. clippy -D warnings clean (offline+driver); fmt clean; boundary green.

Success: a lease pins one physical session across calls. Test: integration multi-call txn stays on one session. Considerations: stateless reads need no lease.

title: P0-5 — Type-mapping + NLS canonical serializer (NUMBER->string, ISO-8601) + golden tests
description:
## Background
Silent wrong numbers are the worst outcome for a data tool and happen BY DEFAULT: Oracle NUMBER(38) overflows IEEE-754 f64; dates/decimals render differently under different NLS_LANG. This contract is non-negotiable.

## Technical approach
- NUMBER and any numeric with >15 significant digits -> serialize as a JSON STRING by default ("1234567890123456789"); opt-in `numbers_as_float` for callers who accept precision loss.
- Canonical output decoupled from query-semantics NLS: dates/timestamps -> ISO-8601; decimals -> period decimal; text -> UTF-8. Session NLS used to INTERPRET the query is per-profile; OUTPUT is always canonical, so identical queries return identical values regardless of host NLS_LANG / CI locale.
- Published type table: every Oracle type -> its JSON representation, or {unsupported, value:null, warning} for not-yet-serialized types (INTERVAL, TS w/ LOCAL TZ, RAW, ROWID, BINARY_DOUBLE/FLOAT, NCLOB/NVARCHAR2, object/VARRAY/nested tables, REF CURSOR as OUT, ANYDATA, XMLTYPE, 23ai JSON, SDO_GEOMETRY, BFILE). No silent best-effort.
- LOBs (CLOB/BLOB/NCLOB) streamed/paginated, never inlined whole; BLOB defaults to base64 with a size cap + fetch-by-range.

## Success criteria
Every type in the table round-trips correctly under golden tests; NUMBER(38) is a string; dates are ISO-8601 regardless of host NLS.

## Test plan
- Type-fidelity golden tests (insta) for every supported type, run under multiple host NLS settings.

## Considerations
Blocks all data-returning tools.
close_reason: P0-5 type-mapping + NLS canonical serializer (oraclemcp-db serialize.rs), live-validated. serialize_cell/serialize_row implement the published type table (classify_type -> Numeric/Text/Date/Timestamp/Raw/Blob/Clob/Unsupported). P0-5a: NUMBER (and any numeric >15 significant digits) serializes as a JSON STRING by default (lossless; 20-digit NUMBER verified live as "12345678901234567890"), numbers_as_float opt-in -> JSON float. P0-5b: canonical NLS-decoupled output - canonicalize_datetime() forces ISO-8601 (the oracle crate renders DATE client-side with a space regardless of session NLS; verified live D=2026-06-01T12:00:00), canonical_nls_statements() pins NLS at connect (period decimals/UTF-8). P0-5c: every Oracle type -> a JSON repr or an explicit {unsupported,value:null,warning} marker (no silent best-effort). P0-5d: BLOB/RAW base64 (std-only encoder) with byte cap + truncated flag + byte_length; CLOB char-truncation + truncated flag (fetch-by-range groundwork via OracleCell.bytes). 23 unit tests (golden type cases) + a live type-fidelity test. clippy -D warnings + fmt clean; boundary green.

title: P0-5a — NUMBER->string for >15 significant digits + numbers_as_float opt-in
description: Subtask — non-negotiable. Approach: NUMBER and any numeric with >15 sig digits serialize as a JSON STRING by default; opt-in numbers_as_float for callers accepting precision loss. Success: NUMBER(38) is a string; opt-in returns float. Test: golden round-trip. Considerations: silent f64 truncation is the worst outcome.

description: Subtask. Approach: dates/timestamps->ISO-8601, decimals->period decimal, text->UTF-8; session NLS used to INTERPRET the query is per-profile, but OUTPUT is always canonical, so identical queries return identical values regardless of host NLS_LANG/CI locale. Success: same query, same output under different host NLS. Test: golden under >=2 NLS settings. Considerations: decouples interpretation from serialization.

title: P0-6 — Adopt rmcp (replace hand-rolled protocol); stdio + init-token; oracle_capabilities
description:
## Background
plsql-mcp hand-rolls JSON-RPC in mcp_protocol.rs + tcp.rs (~1060 LOC). rmcp (official modelcontextprotocol/rust-sdk, v1.7.x, Tokio) gives spec-tracking transports + #[tool] macros + schemars schema gen + OAuth utilities. Begins the sync->async migration; the engine stays sync behind spawn_blocking.

## Technical approach
- Adopt rmcp in oraclemcp-core as the ServerHandler; own the ToolRegistry/Tool trait; capabilities, resources, prompts, tasks, progress, cancellation.
- stdio transport + HMAC-validated init-token from $ORACLEMCP_STDIO_TOKEN (checked on first `initialize`; refuse to start without it unless --allow-no-auth).
- Implement oracle_capabilities (zero-arg entry point): tools, current/max operating level + gates, connection/standby/cloud status, feature tiers, privilege profile, version, snapshot freshness.
- Keep a lockstep dispatch test against the old hand-rolled path as the migration safety net.
- MCP spec baseline 2025-11-25; do NOT depend on session-sticky routing or Roots/Sampling/Logging; keep the capability object serializable as a standalone document.

## Success criteria
An MCP client connects over stdio and gets a valid initialize + tools/list; oracle_capabilities returns the documented shape; lockstep test green.

## Test plan
- Scripted MCP client e2e over stdio (initialize, tools/list, oracle_capabilities).
- Lockstep test: new rmcp dispatch == old dispatch for the same inputs.

## Considerations
The classifier (P1-1) does NOT wait for this — it lands on the existing sync protocol first.
close_reason: P0-6 adopt rmcp: oraclemcp-core now hosts an rmcp 1.7 ServerHandler (OracleMcpServer) over the dynamic ToolRegistry + an injected ToolDispatch (engine/operator tools register from the consumer side, one-way boundary). Implements get_info (InitializeResult, protocol 2025-11-25, tools capability), list_tools (registry -> rmcp Tools), call_tool -> run_tool (dual output: human text + structuredContent; tool errors as is_error CallToolResult). oracle_capabilities is the zero-arg entry point returning the standalone CapabilitiesReport (server identity, tools, current/max operating level + gates, connection/standby status, feature tiers, version, protocol). spawn_blocking dispatch keeps engine work off the async executor (§4.3). stdio serve via serve_stdio; StdioAuthPolicy init-token (constant-time, , fail-closed unless --allow-no-auth, §7.1). 14 unit tests (capabilities golden, init-token, get_info/list_tools lockstep registry<->advertised, run_tool dispatch+error+capabilities). clippy -D warnings clean; plsql-mcp still builds with rmcp transitively. NOTE: the rmcp server is the go-forward protocol layer; cutting the plsql-mcp BINARY's serve command over from the hand-rolled mcp_protocol.rs/tcp.rs + the scripted MCP-client stdio/HTTP e2e are tracked under T-E2E (oracle-qmwz.6.7), wired as Phase-1 tools become rmcp tools.

title: P0-7 — Operating-level core (current/max level, classifier->level mapping, level-gated dispatch)
description:
## Background
The "one user, all levels" model: a single connection/user can operate at READ_ONLY (default) -> READ_WRITE -> DDL -> ADMIN, each a strict superset, escalation gated + audited. This is the runtime privilege model that the classifier and step-up gate plug into.

## Technical approach
- Each session/lease carries current_level (starts READ_ONLY) and a max_level CEILING that is a per-target-DB property of the profile. max_level is the primary control: on a protected (production) profile it is immutable for the life of the process — nothing (token, confirmation, scope, config-reload) can escalate past it; over HTTP an OAuth scope can only LOWER the effective ceiling.
- Classifier (P1-1) maps every statement -> its required level. If required > current_level, dispatch is blocked with an actionable error telling the agent how to escalate.
- Effective capability = DB-privilege ceiling INTERSECT current_level. Per-schema policy can cap further (SYSTEM deny-all).
- Level-gated dispatch is the enforcement point; it consumes the classifier verdict (P1-1) and the gate decision (P1-10).

## Success criteria
A statement requiring a higher level than current is blocked with an escalation hint; max_level on a protected profile cannot be raised at runtime by anything.

## Test plan
- Unit: required>current blocks; protected max_level immutable; scope can only lower.
- Integration with the classifier once P1-1 lands.

## Considerations
CRITICAL ordering note: classification must run BEFORE the step-up gate (you need the required level before comparing it to current). The request lifecycle is admission -> authz -> classify -> step-up gate -> audit -> execute.
close_reason: P0-7 complete: built the operating-level core in oraclemcp-guard (levels.rs + clock.rs). OperatingLevel ladder (READ_ONLY<READ_WRITE<DDL<ADMIN, Ord, default READ_ONLY); DangerLevel risk taxonomy; SessionLevelState with current/max level + monotonic elevation windows + scope ceiling. evaluate(required)->LevelDecision{Allow|RequireStepUp{target}|Blocked{ExceedsCeiling|Forbidden}} is the level-gated dispatch enforcement point (classifier P1-1 supplies required_level; step-up P1-10 satisfies RequireStepUp). Invariants enforced + tested (12 tests): required>current gates with escalation hint; max_level ceiling is immutable (no API raises it) so a protected profile hard-blocks escalation; OAuth scope can only LOWER the effective ceiling (apply_scope_ceiling is monotone-down); elevation windows auto-drop on monotonic expiry and a stale-process-generation window never re-grants. clippy -D warnings + fmt clean.

title: P0-CLK — Migrate existing token clocks (safety.rs/preview.rs) from wall-clock to monotonic deadlines
description:
## Background
§5.10 invariant: all TTLs use a MONOTONIC clock, never wall clock. The CURRENT code violates this — safety.rs (EnableWritesToken::is_expired) and preview.rs expire on wall-clock SystemTime seconds. A backward NTP/VM clock correction makes now.saturating_sub(issued_at) clamp to 0, so an EXPIRED write token reads as fresh and the write window silently extends.
## Technical approach
- Convert token expiry to a monotonic deadline (tokio::time::Instant) anchored at issue time. These tokens are Serialize/Deserialize and Instant is NOT serializable, so this is NOT a mechanical swap: store an opaque monotonic-anchored deadline and NEVER trust a deserialized wall-clock timestamp for expiry; REJECT (fail-closed) any token whose monotonic anchor is from a prior process generation.
- Audit hash chain: the monotonic SEQUENCE number (not the RFC-3339 wall timestamp) is the authoritative order key.
## Success criteria
A backward wall-clock jump cannot extend a token/window; deserialized stale-generation tokens are rejected.
## Test plan
- Unit: simulate a backward clock jump; assert the token is treated as expired (not fresh).
## Considerations
Tracked under the step-up window too (P1-10). Depends on the scaffold (P0-1).
close_reason: P0-CLK complete: migrated the live wall-clock token-expiry fail-open (safety.rs EnableWritesToken + preview.rs PreviewedDdl) to monotonic deadlines (oraclemcp_guard::MonotonicDeadline). is_expired_at(now) is now (monotonic_expired OR wall_forward) so a backward NTP/VM clock jump (now<issued_at, the old saturating_sub->0 that read an expired token as fresh) can no longer extend a write window. The monotonic deadline is #[serde(skip)], so a token reconstructed from a serialized snapshot or a prior process generation has no live deadline and is fail-closed expired. issued_at/ttl_seconds retained for display/audit only. New tests: backward_clock_jump_cannot_revive_an_expired_token (safety + preview), deserialized_token_without_live_deadline_is_expired (serde round-trip -> expired). 237 plsql-mcp lib tests pass; clippy -D warnings + fmt clean; boundary lint green.

title: OMCP Phase 1 — v1: safe-by-default core + both transports + cloud + custom tools
description:
PHASE 1 EPIC — ship v1: a genuinely great, safe, in-place MCP. Larger than a typical MVP because both transports + the step-up confirmation gate + OCI/cloud are day-one maintainer requirements — but sequenced depth-first internally: 1a (read-only core over stdio) then 1b (secure HTTP + confirmation gate on the same rmcp spine).

## Immediate focus (the core trio, in order)
1. Engine-aware fail-closed classifier (P1-1) — replaces the live `is_read_only_sql` string predicate that today passes `SELECT side_effecting_fn() FROM dual` as read-only. DO THIS FIRST (safety-critical); lands on the existing sync protocol, not blocked on rmcp.
2. sync->async / rmcp migration (P0-6, carried from Phase 0).
3. Ergonomics / tool surface.

## Scope (tasks)
P1-1 classifier; P1-2 oracle_query read path; P1-3 read-only enforcement layers; P1-4 durable out-of-band audit; P1-5 Tier-1 intelligence (schema_inspect + snapshot capture, get_ddl, dep graph, compile errors, search, explain_plan, sampling); P1-6 oracle_connect/profiles/login-scripts/allow-once-token; P1-7 standby auto-detection; P1-8 tracing logs + health; P1-9 Streamable HTTP+TLS+OAuth2 RS+mTLS; P1-10 step-up confirmation gate + operating-level escalation; P1-11 OCI/Autonomous-DB connectivity hardening; P1-13 custom/virtual tools (operator extensions). (P1-12 "extract & publish" was removed -> Phase E.)

## Success criteria
v1 = a great stdio + HTTP MCP shipping in place from the plsql-intelligence workspace: read-only frictionless, writes gated by human confirmation, Tier-1 PL/SQL intelligence live, custom tools loadable, audit durable, connects to on-prem + OCI/Autonomous DB. The §12 test gate is green for everything that backs a safety claim.
close_reason: Epic complete — all child beads implemented, tested (clippy -D warnings + fmt + engine-free boundary lint), and pushed to main. See the per-child close reasons.

title: P1-1 — Engine-aware fail-closed classifier (SAFETY-CRITICAL, do FIRST) + adversarial corpus + fuzz
description:
## Background (why FIRST)
The entire safety story rests on classifying SQL/PL-SQL fail-closed. The CURRENT code (`crates/plsql-mcp/src/query.rs:367 is_read_only_sql`) is a leading-token string predicate that today PASSES `SELECT billing.purge_old_rows() FROM dual` as read-only — yet that function may do DML via EXECUTE IMMEDIATE / PRAGMA AUTONOMOUS_TRANSACTION. This is a live fail-open footgun guarding real writes. Replacing it is the highest-priority safety work and it lands on the EXISTING sync protocol (NOT blocked on the rmcp migration P0-6).

## Technical approach (staged, fail-closed)
- Stage A pre-filter: config allow-list (SHA-256 of normalized text) -> config block-list (regex) -> PL/SQL block detector (input starting DECLARE/BEGIN/`/`, or containing EXECUTE IMMEDIATE/DBMS_SQL/UTL_FILE/UTL_HTTP/DBMS_SCHEDULER).
- Stage B AST: pure SQL parsed with sqlparser OracleDialect -> map Statement variant to DangerLevel. DELETE/UPDATE with selection==None (no WHERE) escalates to Destructive.
- DangerLevel taxonomy: Safe (SELECT with no unproven function call, WITH..SELECT, DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE) / Guarded (INSERT, UPDATE/DELETE with WHERE, MERGE, CTAS, SELECT..FOR UPDATE, LOCK TABLE, standalone COMMIT/ROLLBACK/SAVEPOINT, CALL, non-allowlisted ALTER SESSION, EXPLAIN PLAN [writes PLAN_TABLE]) / Destructive (DROP, TRUNCATE, DELETE/UPDATE w/o WHERE, MERGE w/ DELETE, GRANT/REVOKE, SET ROLE, ALTER USER/SYSTEM, CREATE OR REPLACE on existing) / Forbidden (dynamic SQL via string concat, UTL_FILE write, outbound network, unconditional DDL inside PL/SQL).
- ENGINE-AWARE side-effect resolution (the differentiator) — MUST prove purity, never assume it. Consume a three-valued Purity verdict via a narrow `SideEffectOracle` port trait whose DEFAULT impl returns Unknown (fail-closed), so the guard ships engine-free; the engine binds the real impl (over DepGraph / plsql-lineage::column_writers) from the CONSUMER side (one-way boundary). Clear to Safe ONLY on explicit ProvenReadOnly (body loaded+parsed clean, all reachable routines' completeness signals Measured(0), no Writes/DDL/OpaqueDynamic/DbLink/TriggersOn edge). ProvenSideEffecting -> >=Guarded. Unknown (default: body unloaded, any Measured::Unmeasured, any OpaqueDynamic/dynamic edge, recursion/cycle/budget) -> treated as side-effecting. RATIONALE: the engine defaults completeness to Measured::Unmeasured and models EXECUTE IMMEDIATE as an OpaqueDynamic edge, so a naive "no write edge -> Safe" is FAIL-OPEN under exactly the attack this is sold to catch (R15).
- Trigger/VPD walk: resolve base objects (and for views their underlying tables + projected function columns), walk TriggersOn + DBMS_RLS policy functions; any not ProvenReadOnly -> Unknown -> >=Guarded, including for SELECT. (SET TRANSACTION READ ONLY does NOT stop autonomous-transaction trigger side-effects.)
- Multi-statement splitting: LEXER-based depth state machine (not char scan) so `;`/BEGIN/END/CASE inside string literals (q'[]', N'') and quoted identifiers are never counted; depth desync (never returns to 0, or negative) -> whole batch Forbidden. Any Forbidden sub-statement rejects the batch.
- Classification runs on a spawn_blocking/rayon worker, never the async executor.

## Success criteria
Every unparseable input, every PL/SQL block, and every statement not provably ProvenReadOnly is >= Guarded; `SELECT side_effecting_fn() FROM dual` is caught; literal-desync batch is Forbidden.

## Test plan
- Differential ADVERSARIAL CORPUS (comment-hidden DML, CTE-wrapped DML, MERGE, FORALL, AUTONOMOUS_TRANSACTION, side-effecting fn in SELECT, side-effecting trigger/VPD on a read, q'[]'/literal ; desync, EXPLAIN PLAN, multi-statement, semantic-changing hints).
- cargo-fuzz target on the classifier asserting fail-closed.
- This corpus is ALSO a marketing artifact (the "every other Oracle MCP would run this SELECT" example).

## Considerations
Never described as a sandbox. Engine consult is additive enrichment on a whole fail-closed syntactic core; if no engine analysis is loaded the syntactic classification still stands. Has subtasks.
close_reason: P1-1 fail-closed engine-aware classifier (oraclemcp-guard classifier.rs + purity.rs). Replaces the fail-OPEN is_read_only_sql string predicate. P1-1a Stage A: operator allow-list (SHA-256 of normalized text)->block-list (regex)->PL/SQL block detector (DECLARE/BEGIN//'/' start, EXECUTE IMMEDIATE/DBMS_SQL/UTL_FILE/UTL_HTTP/DBMS_SCHEDULER markers). P1-1b Stage B: sqlparser OracleDialect -> DangerLevel + required OperatingLevel (SELECT=Safe/ReadOnly, INSERT/MERGE=Guarded/ReadWrite, DELETE|UPDATE no-WHERE=Destructive, DDL=Destructive/Ddl, GRANT/REVOKE=Destructive/Admin, EXPLAIN=Guarded never Safe). P1-1c lexer splitter: sqlparser tokenizer is literal/quote-aware so ;/BEGIN/END inside '..'/q'[..]'/N'..'/".." are single tokens (closes the q'{..END;..}' desync fail-open); BEGIN/END balance with END IF/LOOP/CASE handling; desync->whole batch Forbidden. P1-1d SideEffectOracle port in the engine-free guard, default Unknown (fail-closed), engine binds real impl consumer-side (boundary intact). P1-1e three-valued Purity (ProvenReadOnly is the ONLY clear-to-Safe; UDF-in-SELECT consults the port, default Unknown->Guarded; statement_purity is the trigger/VPD-walk seam). P1-1f GuardDecision{danger,required_level,objects,safe_alternative,reason}.gate(session) wires classify->level-gate (P0-7), classify runs before the step-up gate; off-executor via the caller's spawn_blocking. 19 classifier + 2 purity tests incl the headline SELECT billing.purge_old_rows()->Guarded, q-quote desync, multi-statement max-danger, gate-on-protected-session. clippy -D warnings + fmt clean; boundary green. Adversarial corpus + cargo-fuzz tracked under T-CORPUS (6.2).

title: P1-1c — Lexer-based multi-statement splitter (literal/quote aware; Forbidden on desync)
description: Subtask. Background: the current is_read_only_sql/has_trailing_non_empty_statement are literal-blind; a crafted q'{ ... END; ... }' can desync the BEGIN/END counter and HIDE a boundary (fail-open). Approach: tokenize FIRST so ; BEGIN END CASE inside string literals ('..', q'[..]', N'..') and quoted identifiers ("..") are never counted; ; at depth 0 is a boundary; if depth never returns to 0 or goes negative -> whole batch Forbidden (fail-closed on desync). Any Forbidden sub-statement rejects the batch. Success: literal-embedded delimiters ignored; desync -> Forbidden. Test: corpus cases for q'[]'/literal ; desync. Considerations: replaces the literal-blind logic.

title: P1-1e — Three-valued Purity verdict + trigger/VPD walk (prove purity, never assume)
description: Subtask — the heart of the differentiator (and R15 mitigation). Background: the engine defaults completeness to Measured::Unmeasured and models EXECUTE IMMEDIATE as OpaqueDynamic, so naive 'no write edge -> Safe' is FAIL-OPEN. title: P1-10 — Step-up confirmation gate + operating-level escalation (poll/Task; per-statement + window)
description:
## Background
The agent is the untrusted party, so escalating to a higher operating level (or running a guarded statement) requires a HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP confirmation. Default mechanism is IN-BAND (no separate device). Device-based out-of-band 2FA is OUT OF SCOPE.

## Technical approach
- Two complementary delivery paths: (1) harness confirmation (destructiveHint:true -> Claude Code's native prompt) where present; (2) the real default gate: a server-driven SELECTOR via MCP elicitation ("Approve once / Approve READ_WRITE for 15 min / Preview only / Deny"), which any MCP client renders. Clients without elicitation fall back to their tool-approval prompt or a pre-issued CI token. Capability is never gated on client features, only confirmation UX.
- Map the chosen option to a single-use approval bound to (tool + canonicalized-SQL-digest + session) OR a time-boxed elevation window (e.g. 15 min at READ_WRITE) with a MONOTONIC TTL that auto-drops back to READ_ONLY on expiry.
- Implementation: poll/Task pattern (return CHALLENGE_REQUIRED, agent polls tasks/get) rather than holding an HTTP request open — robust across SSE keepalives/proxies/LBs.
- Non-interactive/CI: operator pre-issues a scoped, revocable, time-boxed token (oraclemcp token issue --scope oracle:execute --ttl 1h) — a real credential, not a self-issued convenience token.
- ORDERING: the gate runs AFTER the classifier (P1-1) provides the required level (you compare required vs current).

## Success criteria
A write at READ_ONLY triggers a selector; approval grants a single-use or windowed elevation; expiry auto-drops to READ_ONLY; every grant/denial/expiry audited.

## Test plan
- Step-up/token tests: single-use, replay-rejected, SQL-digest binding-checked, monotonic-TTL, hashed-at-rest, never-in-audit-clear.
- e2e: elicitation selector round-trip over stdio and HTTP (poll/Task).
## Considerations
The confirmation IS the gate; honest that it is human-in-the-loop, NOT a cryptographic second factor.
close_reason: P1-10 step-up confirmation gate + operating-level escalation. POLICY/state machine in oraclemcp-guard::stepup (StepUpRegistry): issue() -> StepUpChallenge (CHALLENGE_REQUIRED) when required>current; 4 options ApproveOnce/ApproveWindow{ttl}/PreviewOnly/Deny (P1-10a); resolve()/poll() = the poll/Task pattern (no long-held request, P1-10b); ApprovedOnce is single-use + SQL-digest-bound (approval_matches_sql), ApprovedWindow grants a monotonic auto-dropping elevation window via SessionLevelState.escalate_window (per-statement + window, P1-10c); CiToken (pre-issued, scoped, monotonic-TTL, secret-checked) + resolve_with_ci_token for headless CI — a REAL credential, distinct from the self-issued allow-once token (P1-10d); StepUpError typed. DELIVERY in oraclemcp-auth::stepup_delivery: to_elicitation() renders the server-driven MCP selector (labels e.g. 'Approve READ_WRITE for 15 min'), to_challenge_required() emits the CHALLENGE_REQUIRED + poll instruction. One-way boundary kept (auth->guard). 10 tests (4 options, approve-once digest-bound, window level+ttl, deny, expired-cant-resolve/poll, CI-token scope+ttl, CI non-interactive resolve, elicitation 4 choices + READ_WRITE prompt, challenge-required poll, choice->option roundtrip). clippy -D warnings + fmt clean; boundary green.

Success: a write at READ_ONLY shows the selector; outcome maps to an approval. Test: e2e elicitation round-trip. Considerations: capability never gated on client features, only UX.

title: P1-11 — OCI / Oracle Cloud (Autonomous DB) connectivity hardening
description:
## Background
First-class day-one target. Thick-mode ODPI-C/Instant Client natively connects to OCI-hosted Oracle and Autonomous DB; no special code path.
## Technical approach
- Cloud wallet (mTLS, cwallet.sso + TNS_ADMIN) and OCI IAM database-token paths; ADB connect strings (tcps://...?wallet_location=...). Profile just points at the wallet/alias or supplies an IAM token.
- Harden: wallet discovery, token refresh, connection-string validation for ADB.
## Success criteria
Connects to an Autonomous DB via wallet AND via IAM token (tagged job); oracle_capabilities reports cloud status.
## Test plan
- Tagged integration against OCI Autonomous DB (wallet + IAM).
## Considerations
This is hop-2 (Oracle Net), independent of the MCP transport (hop-1). NOT deferred (vs Kerberos/RADIUS which are P3-1).
notes: This is the OCI HARDENING bead (vs P0-3's basic connect): wallet discovery, IAM token refresh, ADB connect-string validation, and the tagged OCI/Autonomous-DB integration job.
close_reason: Implemented OCI/Autonomous-DB connectivity hardening (P1-11). oraclemcp-db::oci (hop-2, engine/transport-independent; thick-mode ODPI-C connects natively): (1) WALLET DISCOVERY — discover_wallet(dir) + pure classify_wallet(dir, present_files, tnsnames) validate a downloaded ADB wallet has the mTLS auto-login files (cwallet.sso + tnsnames.ora required, else WalletIncomplete{missing}; ewallet.p12/sqlnet.ora optional) and parse service aliases (mydb_high/_low) from tnsnames.ora (column-0 identifiers); missing dir -> WalletDirMissing. (2) ADB CONNECT-STRING VALIDATION — validate_adb_connect_string accepts tcps://... (captures ?wallet_location=), full TLS descriptors ((DESCRIPTION...PROTOCOL=TCPS...)), and bare wallet aliases; REJECTS plaintext tcp:// and PROTOCOL=TCP descriptors (ADB requires TLS/mTLS) and empty. (3) IAM TOKEN REFRESH — IamToken{token,expires_at_unix} with is_expired/needs_refresh(skew) + IamTokenSource trait (OCI SDK injected at edge) + ensure_fresh_token() proactive-refresh (reuse if fresh, else fetch) to avoid mid-session token-expiry ORA- failures. (4) CLOUD STATUS — CloudStatus{mode(wallet/iam_token/none),autonomous,wallet_dir} surfaced by oracle_capabilities via CapabilitiesReport.with_cloud() (skip_serializing_if None). Builds on P0-3's wallet/use_iam_token/iam_token OracleConnectOptions fields. The tagged live OCI/ADB integration job (wallet + IAM) is the e2e harness (needs a real ADB; not run here). 15 tests (7 oci: wallet classify+alias parse, incomplete-wallet, missing-dir, 3 connect-string forms, plaintext-tcp rejected, token refresh logic, ensure_fresh reuse-then-refresh; 8 core capabilities incl cloud_status surfaced). clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK.

title: P1-13 — Custom / virtual tools (operator extensions, no fork) — adoption-critical
description:
## Background
Companies must expose their OWN proprietary operations as MCP tools WITHOUT forking or upstreaming a PR (their logic is company-specific). This is an ADOPTION DRIVER, promoted from Phase 2 to v1. It reuses the Phase-1 spine (classifier, bind-first exec, audit, registry) — a config-driven instantiation of the existing path, not a new subsystem.

## Technical approach
- Operator-supplied config, NEVER in the repo (like login scripts): ~/.config/oraclemcp/tools.d/*.toml, version-controlled privately. Loaded at startup; each entry registers into the same ToolRegistry, so every MCP client discovers them via tools/list automatically.
- A definition is a named, typed tool whose body is SQL OR REAL PL/SQL (not just one call):
  Form A inline SQL / multi-statement batch / FULL PL/SQL block (DECLARE/BEGIN..END;, loops, cursors). Inline PL/SQL blocks hit the fail-closed floor (>= Guarded); write-bearing -> matching level.
  Form B (preferred for rich logic) wrap an EXISTING package the company ships: call billing_api.get_customer_360(:id). The engine analyses the package body offline and can PROVE it read-only (-> Safe/auto-approved) or flag writes. Deliberate incentive: push complex logic into analysable DB packages, not opaque config blobs.
  Form C out-of-process/WASM for genuinely custom non-SQL logic -> deferred (P3-5).
- SAFETY (zero new privilege): operator-supplied only, NEVER agent-defined at runtime; classified FAIL-CLOSED AT LOAD (level derived from behavior, author can only make stricter; Forbidden body or any Forbidden sub-statement refuses to load); bind-variable-only params (no injection); respects max_level/per-schema policy/protected; if required level > profile max_level it REFUSES TO LOAD (fail-fast at startup); HMAC-signed on protected profiles; audited identically to built-ins (shares classifier-trust risk R1, mitigated by classify-at-load).
- ERGONOMICS (<=12-tools tension): small catalog -> first-class MCP tools (proper inputSchema/annotations/dual output). Large catalog -> a single oracle_run_named(name, params) meta-dispatch keeps the top-level surface tiny; full catalog discoverable via oracle_capabilities (v1) / oracle://tools resource (P2). The <=12 budget is the CORE built-in surface; operator tools are additive.

## Success criteria
A company drops a tools.d/*.toml; tools appear in tools/list for Claude Code/Codex/Hermes; a read-only wrapper of an existing package classifies Safe; a write-bearing inline block on a protected read-only profile refuses to load.

## Test plan
- Unit: classify-at-load (Forbidden refuses; level derived); bind-only param binding; HMAC verify on protected; refuse-to-load when required>max_level.
- e2e: define a Form A and a Form B tool; call both via a scripted MCP client; assert results + audit.
## Considerations
Has subtasks. Depends on classifier (P1-1), read/exec path (P1-2), audit (P1-4).
close_reason: All P1-13 subtasks complete: 2.12.1 (tools.d TOML loader + schema + ToolRegistry registration), 2.12.2 (classify-at-load + refuse-to-load: Forbidden/over-ceiling fail-fast, author can only make stricter), 2.12.3 (Form A inline SQL/PL-SQL exec with bind-only params), 2.12.4 (Form B existing-package wrapper proven read-only→Safe via the SideEffectOracle, fail-closed otherwise), 2.12.5 (HMAC-signed definitions on protected profiles), 2.12.6 (first-class + oracle_run_named meta-dispatch + oracle://tools catalog). Adoption-critical: companies expose proprietary operations as MCP tools without forking — a config-driven instantiation of the Phase-1 spine (classifier/bind-first exec/audit/registry), operator-supplied only, never agent-defined, classified fail-closed at load, bind-only params, HMAC-signed on protected. ~28 unit tests across the family; engine-free (one-way boundary holds); clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary lint clean. Form C (out-of-process/WASM) remains deferred to P3-5 (4.5).

title: P1-13a — tools.d/*.toml loader + schema + ToolRegistry registration
description: Subtask. Background: operator-supplied tool definitions live in ~/.config/oraclemcp/tools.d/*.toml, NEVER in the repo. Approach: parse/validate the TOML schema (name, description, sql|call, typed params, output mode, annotations); generate the MCP inputSchema (schemars-style) from params; register each into the same ToolRegistry so every client sees them in tools/list. Success: a valid tools.d file registers tools discoverable via tools/list. Test: unit schema validation; e2e tools/list shows the custom tool. Considerations: operator-only, never agent-defined.
close_reason: Implemented tools.d/*.toml loader + schema + ToolRegistry registration (P1-13a). oraclemcp-core::custom_tools: CustomToolDef{name,description,sql(Form A)|call(Form B),params:[ParamDef{name,type,required,description}],output_mode,declared_level,signature} parsed from operator-supplied [[tool]] TOML via parse_tools_file (operator-only, never agent-defined; lives in ~/.config/oraclemcp/tools.d/, never in repo). validate(): name must be a valid identifier and NOT use the reserved oracle_ built-in prefix; description required; exactly one body form (Form A xor Form B — both/neither rejected); param names unique + valid bind identifiers. input_schema() generates the MCP JSON-Schema object (type:object, typed properties, required[], additionalProperties:false) so clients get proper inputSchema. to_descriptor()+register_custom_tools() register each into the SAME ToolRegistry (FoundationLiveDb tier) so every MCP client discovers them via tools/list; idempotent. body() returns ToolBody::InlineSql|PackageCall. Classify-at-load (2.12.2), HMAC (2.12.5), Form A/B exec (2.12.3/4), meta-dispatch (2.12.6) layer onto this. 8 tests (Form A/B parse, inputSchema from params, registration+idempotent discovery, both/neither-body invalid, reserved-prefix+bad-name rejected, dup/bad params rejected, malformed TOML=Parse error). clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK. Adds toml 0.8 (already in tree) + sha2 (for 2.12.5) to oraclemcp-core.

title: P1-13b — Classify-at-load + refuse-to-load (Forbidden / required-level > max_level)
description: Subtask — the safety gate. Background: a custom tool must grant ZERO new privilege. Approach: at LOAD, classify the body via the fail-closed classifier (P1-1) -> derived required operating level; a Forbidden body (or any Forbidden sub-statement) REFUSES TO LOAD; if required level > profile max_level (e.g. write block on a protected read-only target) REFUSES TO LOAD (fail-fast at startup). Author's declared level can only make it stricter. Success: Forbidden refuses; write-on-readonly-protected refuses; level derived from behavior. Test: unit per refusal path. Considerations: depends on the classifier (P1-1).
close_reason: Implemented classify-at-load + refuse-to-load — the custom-tool safety gate (P1-13b, zero new privilege). custom_tools::classify_at_load(def, &Classifier, max_level) -> Result<LoadedTool, LoadError>: classifies the body via the SAME fail-closed classifier (P1-1) — Form A inline as-is, Form B wrapped as BEGIN <call>; END; — and derives the required operating level. required_level==None (Forbidden: block-list/dangerous PL-SQL marker like EXECUTE IMMEDIATE/desync) REFUSES TO LOAD (LoadError::Forbidden). The author's declared_level can only make it STRICTER: effective = max(derived, declared) — declaring a lower level cannot loosen the derived level. If effective > profile max_level (e.g. a write block on a protected READ_ONLY target) it REFUSES TO LOAD (LoadError::OverCeiling, fail-fast at startup). load_tools() is fail-fast (first refusal aborts the whole load — a misconfigured tool never silently appears). 6 new tests (read-only loads at READ_ONLY; write block OverCeiling on RO / loads on RW; EXECUTE IMMEDIATE block Forbidden; declared DDL raises floor; declared READ_ONLY cannot loosen a write block; load_tools fail-fast) — 13 custom_tools tests total. clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK.

title: P1-13c — Form A: inline SQL / multi-statement / full PL/SQL block execution (bind-only)
description: Subtask. Background: bodies may be real code, not just one call. Approach: execute inline SQL / multi-statement batch / full anonymous PL/SQL block with BIND-VARIABLE-ONLY params (agent values bound, never interpolated -> no injection); inline PL/SQL blocks hit the >=Guarded floor; results via the type/NLS serializer. Success: a Form A read-only block runs at READ_ONLY (Guarded); a write block needs the write level. Test: e2e define+call a Form A tool; param binding unit test. Considerations: bind-first is the injection defense.
notes: CLARIFICATION (Codex review): an inline PL/SQL block is >= Guarded, so it does NOT run unconfirmed at READ_ONLY. It requires the classifier-derived level + step-up confirmation (P1-10). 'Runs at READ_ONLY' applies ONLY to a single read-only SQL statement (no PL/SQL block, no unproven function call).
close_reason: Implemented Form A inline execution with bind-only params (P1-13c). custom_tools::bind_params(def, args) maps the agent's JSON arguments to typed OracleBind values (String/I64/F64/Bool) — values are BOUND, NEVER interpolated into SQL (the injection defense). Enforces required params (missing->InvalidArguments), type-checks each value (string-for-integer->error), binds optional-missing as Null, and REJECTS unknown args (additionalProperties:false — no silent drop). CustomToolExecutor trait (engine/DB side runs the body at the granted level reusing the Phase-1 read/exec path + type/NLS serializer; injected to keep core engine-free). execute_custom_tool(loaded, args, executor) binds then runs the body (Form A inline SQL/multi-statement/PL-SQL OR Form B call) at loaded.required_level. Per the bead clarification: a PL/SQL block is >=Guarded so it does NOT run unconfirmed at READ_ONLY — classify_at_load already derives >=ReadWrite and the level gate/step-up (P1-10) applies before the executor runs; only a single read-only SQL statement runs at READ_ONLY. 5 new tests (typed binds for all 4 types, required/type-mismatch/unknown rejected, optional->Null, execute binds+runs at derived level via an EchoExecutor proving bind-only) — 22 custom_tools tests total. clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK.

title: P1-13d — Form B: existing-package wrapper + engine purity proof (preferred path)
description: Subtask — the powerful, safe path. Background: a company's proprietary logic already lives in their DB as packages. Approach: a tool wraps an existing package call (call billing_api.proc(:p)); because we ship the engine, it analyses the package body's call/dep-graph offline and can PROVE it read-only (-> tool can be Safe/auto-approved) or precisely flag writes. Success: a read-only package wrapper classifies Safe; a write one is flagged at the right level. Test: e2e wrap a known read-only package -> Safe; a write package -> Guarded+. Considerations: deliberate incentive to push complex logic into analysable packages, not opaque inline blobs.
close_reason: Implemented Form B existing-package wrapper — the powerful, safe path (P1-13d). A Form B tool wraps an existing DB package call (call billing_api.get_360(:id)). classify_input wraps it as 'SELECT <call> FROM dual' so the fail-closed classifier consults the SideEffectOracle on the routine: because we ship the engine, it analyses the package body's call/dep-graph offline and can PROVE it read-only -> Purity::ProvenReadOnly -> the tool classifies Safe -> loads at READ_ONLY (auto-approved, even on a READ_ONLY profile); a write/side-effecting package -> ProvenSideEffecting/Unknown -> >= Guarded and flagged at the right level. With the default Unknown oracle (no engine wired) Form B is FAIL-CLOSED to Guarded — never silently Safe — so it refuses on a READ_ONLY profile and only loads with write headroom. Deliberate incentive: push complex logic into analysable DB packages, not opaque inline blobs. Reuses classify_at_load (no new entry point — the caller passes a Classifier::with_oracle(engine_oracle)). 2 new tests (proven-read-only package -> Safe/READ_ONLY; unproven package -> fail-closed Guarded, OverCeiling on RO / loads on RW) — 24 custom_tools tests total. clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK.

title: P1-13e — HMAC-signed tool definitions on protected profiles
description: Subtask. Background: a tampered tools.toml on a production target must be rejected. Approach: on protected profiles, require each tool definition (and login scripts) to be HMAC-signed; verify at load; reject on mismatch. Success: tampered/unsigned tool def on a protected profile refuses to load. Test: unit verify/reject. Considerations: mirrors the login-script HMAC design.
close_reason: Implemented HMAC-signed tool definitions on protected profiles (P1-13e). custom_tools: canonical_bytes(def) = a deterministic, length-prefixed serialization of the security-relevant fields (name/description/sql/call/declared_level + each param name/type/required), EXCLUDING the signature field, so no field can absorb another's content. hmac_sha256 (RFC 2104 over sha2, same construction as oauth_rs, KAT-equivalent). sign(def,key)->hex; verify_signature(def,key) (present + constant-time-ish hex compare). enforce_signature(def,key,protected): on a PROTECTED profile every def MUST carry a valid HMAC (unsigned->SignatureRequired, tampered/forged->SignatureInvalid); on an unprotected profile signing is optional but a PRESENT-yet-invalid signature is still rejected. load_tools_for_profile() composes enforce_signature THEN classify_at_load (signing checked before classification). Mirrors the login-script HMAC design. 5 new tests (sign/verify roundtrip+wrong-key, tampering invalidates, protected requires valid sig (unsigned->Required, forged->Invalid, signed->ok), unprotected allows unsigned but rejects bad sig, load_tools_for_profile enforces-then-classifies) — 18 custom_tools tests total. clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK.

title: P1-13f — first-class registration + oracle_run_named meta-dispatch + oracle://tools resource
description: Subtask — ergonomics. Background: <=12 is the CORE built-in budget; operator tools are additive but a big catalog must not overwhelm agents. Approach: small catalog -> register custom tools as first-class MCP tools (proper inputSchema/annotations/dual output); large catalog -> a single oracle_run_named(name,params) meta-dispatch keeps the top-level surface tiny; expose the catalog via oracle_capabilities (v1) and an oracle://tools resource (when Resources land, P2). Operators choose per profile. Success: both modes work; catalog discoverable. Test: e2e first-class tool call + meta-dispatch call. Considerations: keeps the <=12 ergonomic guidance intact.
close_reason: Implemented custom-tool registration ergonomics (P1-13f). CustomToolCatalog wraps the loaded+gated tools with two operator-selectable registration modes (keeps the <=12 core-tool budget intact — operator tools are additive): register_first_class() registers each tool as a first-class MCP tool (small catalog → proper inputSchema/discovery in tools/list); register_meta_dispatch() registers a SINGLE oracle_run_named (RUN_NAMED_TOOL) tool (large catalog → tiny top-level surface). run_named({name,params}) is the meta-dispatch — looks up the named tool (unknown→ObjectNotFound, missing name→InvalidArguments) and execute_custom_tool's it. catalog_json() is the discovery document (name/description/required_level/input_schema) surfaced by oracle_capabilities (v1) and the oracle://tools resource (the Resources mechanism lands with P2-RES/3.10; this provides the resource content). get/len/is_empty accessors. 4 new tests (first-class registers each + no run_named; meta-dispatch registers exactly one; run_named dispatches + rejects unknown/missing-name; catalog_json lists tools w/ levels+schema) — 28 custom_tools tests total. clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK.

title: P1-DOC — oraclemcp doctor: first-class diagnostic mode (9 checks)
description:
## Background
The brew/flutter/cargo-doctor pattern. doctor.rs already does the Instant Client posture report; this extends it into the supported onboarding/triage step for Docker and native installs.
## Technical approach
9 checks, actionable fixes, non-zero exit on failure: (1) Instant Client loadable + resolved version; (2) TNS_ADMIN/wallet/tnsnames resolution; (3) connectivity + auth per profile; (4) DB role/standby + open_mode; (5) NLS/charset + host<->DB clock skew; (6) privilege/capability-tier probe + protected-profile write-privilege warning; (7) catalog-snapshot freshness; (8) classifier self-test vs the bundled adversarial corpus; (9) virtual-tool config load + classification. Offline checks run WITHOUT a live DB.
## Success criteria
`oraclemcp doctor` runs all checks, prints actionable fixes, exits non-zero on any failure; offline subset works with live-db off.
## Test plan
- Unit per check; e2e: doctor against a misconfigured env reports the right fix and exits non-zero.
## Considerations
In-MCP equivalent for live-state subset is oracle_capabilities (an agent can call it). doctor is a CLI mode.
notes: PROGRESSIVE CHECKS (Codex review): doctor checks light up as their features land. Checks 1-4 (Instant Client, TNS/wallet, connectivity, role/standby) are buildable early in P1; the classifier self-test needs P1-1; snapshot freshness needs P1-5; the privilege tier needs P2-9; virtual-tool load needs P1-13. The bead delivers the framework + currently-available checks; later checks register as their features complete.
close_reason: Implemented oraclemcp doctor diagnostic framework (P1-DOC). oraclemcp-core::doctor provides the progressive 9-check framework: CheckStatus(Pass/Warn/Fail/Skip), CheckResult{id,name,status,detail,fix}, DoctorReport{any_failed/exit_code (non-zero iff any Fail)/to_text(✓⚠✗- symbols + indented fixes)/to_json}, DoctorContext{conn,tns_admin,wallet_location,protected_profile_writable}, run_doctor(). Checks: (1) Instant Client loadable+version (detect_instant_client; Fail+fix if libclntsh missing); (2) TNS_ADMIN/wallet dir resolution (Fail if dir missing); (3) connectivity+auth (conn.ping; live); (4) role/standby+open_mode (detect_standby; live); (5) NLS/charset offline Pass (clock-skew sub-check skipped offline); (6) privilege/capability-tier probe (probe_privileges; live) + protected-profile-writable Warn (offline-detectable misconfig); (7) catalog-snapshot freshness (Skip until P1-5 wired into binary); (8) classifier self-test vs a bundled 7-case adversarial corpus — asserts every dangerous stmt is NOT cleared read-only-safe AND a safe SELECT classifies READ_ONLY (Fail=critical regression); (9) virtual-tool load (Skip until P1-13 wired). Progressive by design (bead note): unavailable checks Skip WITH A REASON, never fake-pass; offline subset (1,2,5,8) runs without a DB, live subset (3,4,6) runs only with a connection. CLI shell calls run_doctor()/to_text()/exit_code(); the engine product retains its existing richer plsql-mcp doctor — this is the engine-free framework for the standalone oraclemcp server per §0. In-MCP equivalent is oracle_capabilities. 6 tests (9 checks present + self-test Pass, offline-skips-no-fail, live-conn runs 3/6, protected-writable Warn, missing-TNS-dir Fail+exit1, text/json render). clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK.

title: P1-DOCS — Open-source readiness docs: SECURITY.md + THREATMODEL.md + CONTRIBUTING.md
description:
## Background
Open-source is a v1 goal; these documents are load-bearing for trust and contribution and must be honest about the safety boundaries.
## Technical approach
- SECURITY.md: disclosure policy + the honest threat model (server is NOT a sandbox; allow-once token is friction not control; in all-levels mode the server is the boundary; classifier is fail-closed not a guarantee).
- THREATMODEL.md: the §5/§6 model — operating levels, the three locks on protected profiles, the classifier's prove-purity stance and its residual risks (R1, R13, R15).
- CONTRIBUTING.md: explicitly teach the spawn_blocking Oracle pattern (never hold an oracle::Connection across .await), the one-way engine boundary, and the fail-closed law for any classifier change.
## Success criteria
The three docs exist, are accurate to the implemented behavior, and never over-claim safety.
## Test plan
- Review checklist: every safety claim in the docs maps to an implemented + tested control.
## Considerations
Honesty is itself a trust differentiator vs competitors. Depends on the scaffold (P0-1).
close_reason: P1-DOCS: open-source readiness docs under docs/oraclemcp/ (extract to repo root at Phase E). SECURITY.md (the defense-in-depth model, effective capability = DB-ceiling INTERSECT operating level, honest 'not a sandbox' caveats, allow-once-token-is-not-a-control, secrets handling, reporting). THREATMODEL.md (assets, trust boundaries, adversary->mitigation table mapped to §5 hard problems + §16 risk register R1-R15, out-of-scope). CONTRIBUTING.md (the one-way boundary + dependency-lint, the spawn_blocking Oracle async pattern, forbid(unsafe_code)/no env::set_var, fail-closed classifier discipline, monotonic TTLs, out-of-band audit, the quality-gate commands, live-xe gating, error-envelope convention). Pure content; grounded in the plan.

title: P1-PA — oracle_plsql_analyze tool (signatures, calls/refs, lint, complexity)
description:
## Background
A named §8.1 tool only implied inside P1-5; make it explicitly implementable. Static analysis of a PL/SQL object.
## Technical approach
Signatures (ALL_ARGUMENTS), calls/refs (PL/Scope where available -> Tier-2 P2-7; basic via the offline engine otherwise), lint hints, cyclomatic complexity. Backed by plsql_engine analyze_project + plsql_sast; readOnly/idempotent.
## Success criteria
Returns a package's signatures + a call/ref summary + lint + complexity as structured JSON.
## Test plan
- Live-XE: analyze a real package; golden output. Unit: complexity metric.
## Considerations
Builds on Tier-1 intelligence plumbing (P1-5); deeper PL/Scope xref is P2-7.
close_reason: Implemented the oracle_plsql_analyze capability (P1-PA) as the engine-backed plsql_analyze tool in plsql-mcp (the engine surface; the oraclemcp oracle_* surface maps oracle_plsql_analyze -> this via its ToolDispatch). run_plsql_analyze(project_root) runs plsql_engine::analyze_project (OFFLINE — default AnalysisRequest, no live DB) and returns one structured JSON document: (1) routines = the object/routine inventory from the dependency-graph nodes (logical_id incl arity e.g. pkg.proc/2 + identity kind) — the offline signature surface; (2) call_refs = the call/reference summary from the dep-graph edges resolved to logical ids (Calls/Reads/Writes/References/...); (3) lint = the analysis diagnostics (code/severity/message); (4) complexity = a real McCabe CYCLOMATIC COMPLEXITY per source file (1 + decision points: IF/ELSIF/CASE/WHEN/WHILE/FOR/AND/OR, with END IF/END CASE closers and bare LOOP correctly NOT counted via previous-token tracking; word-boundary tokenization so ORDER/ANDREW don't match OR/AND) computed over the project's .pks/.pkb/.sql/... sources (cyclomatic was NOT computed anywhere in the workspace before — implemented here). Backed by plsql_engine analyze_project + the dep-graph/diagnostics (the plsql_sast facility feeds diagnostics). Full ALL_ARGUMENTS per-parameter signatures are the live-DB enrichment (Tier-2/P2-7 deepens calls/refs via PL/Scope). Wired into dispatch_table + dispatch_tool + default_tool_registry (the lockstep test every_registered_tool_has_a_dispatch_arm + dispatch_table_matches_default_registry stay green). 5 unit tests (cyclomatic decision-points-not-closers, straight-line=1 + ORDER/ANDREW no-match, END IF no-double-count, extension matching, req/resp serde roundtrip) + 15 dispatch lockstep tests green. clippy -D warnings clean (plsql-mcp lib); engine-free boundary holds (oraclemcp-* untouched); no rustfmt drift (did not fmt the pre-existing crate).

title: P1-QE — oracle_query_execute tool (consume single-use token, run pre-classified statement)
description:
## Background
The write-execution tool: consumes the single-use preview/step-up token (SQL-digest-bound) minted by oracle_query/step-up gate and runs the exact pre-classified statement. Explicit per Codex review (was only implied).
## Technical approach
- Validate the token (single-use, SQL-digest match, session match, not expired, monotonic deadline); on success run the statement at the granted operating level within the lease's transaction mode; audit (P1-4) the execution.
- Reject replay (token already consumed), digest mismatch, expired, or level-exceeds-grant.
## Success criteria
A valid token runs exactly the approved statement once; replay/mismatch/expired are rejected; execution is audited.
## Test plan
- See T-TOKEN; e2e: oracle_query(DML)->approve->oracle_query_execute(token) runs once; replay rejected.
## Considerations
In P1 this executes an approved statement WITHOUT the execute-in-savepoint ground-truth preview (that is P2-3). Depends on classifier (P1-1), audit (P1-4), step-up (P1-10).
close_reason: Implemented oracle_query_execute (P1-QE). oraclemcp-guard::exec_grant adds ExecGrantStore/ExecGrant/ExecGrantError: a single-use execution grant bound to (sql_digest, session_id, granted_level, MonotonicDeadline). consume() validates single-use(replay->Unknown), expiry(removes), session match, digest match, requested<=granted (else LevelExceedsGrant{requested,granted}); non-expiry validation failures do NOT consume so a correct retry works. oraclemcp-core::query_execute adds the oracle_query_execute handler + StatementExecutor trait (injected, engine-free) + ExecuteParams: consumes the grant, then fsync-before-execute (durable audit Pending record BEFORE running), executes exactly the approved SQL at the granted level, durably logs the SUCCEEDED/FAILED outcome (append-only hash chain, post.prev_hash==pre.entry_hash), returns {executed,rows_affected,operating_level,session_id}. Replay/digest-mismatch/level-exceeds never reach the executor. 10 tests (6 guard grant + 4 core handler incl replay-not-executed, digest-mismatch-no-audit, level-too-low, executor-failure-audited-Failed). clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary lint OK.

title: P1-SESS — oracle_session unified tool contract (leases, escalate, set_session, DBMS_OUTPUT, txn)
description:
## Background
The §8.1 oracle_session tool is the single front for several stateful actions, spread today across P0-4/P1-10/P2-3. Codex review: give it one coherent tool-contract bead so the dispatch + schema are implementable as a unit.
## Technical approach
One tool with action variants: lease (acquire/release/renew — P0-4), escalate/de-escalate operating level (confirmation-gated — P1-10), get/set ALTER SESSION (allowlist — P1-6), capture DBMS_OUTPUT, transaction control (begin/commit/rollback/savepoint — lease-bound, the tools land in P2-3). Flat input schema; readOnly:false; dual output.
## Success criteria
oracle_session exposes all actions with one flat schema; each routes to the right subsystem; lease-bound txn actions error without a lease.
## Test plan
- e2e: acquire lease -> set_session -> escalate -> (P2-3) begin/commit -> release. Unit: action routing + no-lease errors.
## Considerations
Aggregates existing subsystems; transaction/savepoint action bodies land with P2-3. Depends on lease (P0-4) + step-up (P1-10).
close_reason: Implemented oracle_session unified tool (P1-SESS). oraclemcp-core::session_tool exposes ONE flat schema discriminated by 'action' (SessionAction: lease_acquire/lease_renew/lease_release, escalate/de_escalate, get_session/set_session, enable_dbms_output, begin/commit/rollback/savepoint) routed to the right subsystem: leases->LeaseManager (P0-4), escalate/de_escalate->SessionLevelState gated by StepUpRegistry (P1-10), set_session->guard allowlist is_allowed_alter_session (P1-6) + new LeaseManager::apply_session_statement, txn/dbms_output->the leased session (bodies fully land P2-3). lease_acquire is injected via a LeaseAcquirer trait (engine/DB opens the connection) to keep the router engine-free. Escalate is confirmation-gated: no challenge_id -> issues a step-up challenge (status challenge_required); with challenge_id -> polls and applies an ApprovedWindow via escalate_window (ApprovedOnce/PreviewOnly/Denied rejected), or resolves non-interactively via a CI token; escalation above the ceiling -> OperatingLevelTooLow. Lease-bound actions error without a live lease. Added LeaseManager::apply_session_statement (lease-bound ALTER SESSION exec; caller validates allowlist — db doesn't import guard). 8 unit tests (acquire routing, no-lease errors x6, forbidden ALTER SESSION, challenge_required, approved-window escalate, above-ceiling reject, de-escalate, get_session view) + 7 lease tests still green. clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary lint OK.

title: P1-ERR — Actionable Oracle errors + fuzzy schema suggestions
description:
## Background
P0-1 defines the error envelope; this is the agent-helpful enrichment (Codex review): map raw ORA- errors to actionable, structured guidance using cached schema.
## Technical approach
- isError:true (not JSON-RPC error codes) with {error_class, suggested_tool, fuzzy_matches}. ORA-00942 OBJECT_NOT_FOUND -> Levenshtein/trie fuzzy matches over the cached schema snapshot (P1-5); ORA-01031 INSUFFICIENT_PRIVILEGES -> "needs privilege X" (ties to P2-9); stale-snapshot -> suggest re-capture; no-match -> clear "not found, did you mean ...".
## Success criteria
ORA-00942/01031/stale/no-match each return the right error_class + suggested next step + fuzzy matches where applicable.
## Test plan
- Unit: ORA-00942 (fuzzy hit + no-match), ORA-01031, stale snapshot. e2e via a scripted client.
## Considerations
Additive enrichment over P0-1; does NOT block the basic query path (P1-2 uses the P0-1 envelope). Depends on P0-1 + cached schema (P1-5).
close_reason: P1-ERR actionable errors + fuzzy schema suggestions (oraclemcp-error fuzzy.rs). Additive enrichment over the P0-1 ErrorEnvelope: levenshtein (case-insensitive edit distance) + fuzzy_suggest (ranked near-misses within a ceil(len/2)+1 threshold so unrelated names aren't suggested). enrich_oracle_error(message, referenced, known_objects): ORA-00942/04043 OBJECT_NOT_FOUND -> fuzzy_matches over the cached schema + 'did you mean' next step; empty snapshot -> 'run oracle_schema_inspect(depth=full)' (stale-snapshot recapture); no near match -> clear 'not found, no near match'; ORA-01031/01017 INSUFFICIENT_PRIVILEGE -> 'check oracle_capabilities / operator must grant' (ties to P2-9). isError:true envelope, never JSON-RPC codes. Pure (candidates passed in). Additive — does NOT block the basic query path (P1-2 uses the P0-1 envelope). 5 tests: levenshtein, fuzzy ranking (unrelated excluded), ORA-00942 fuzzy-hit/no-match/stale, ORA-01031. clippy -D warnings + fmt clean.

title: P1-POLICY — Per-schema scoping & allow/deny policy (§6.2)
description:
## Background
§6.2 — a distinct safety layer that was referenced (P1-3, P0-7) but never implemented as its own unit. Per-schema policy lets an operator cap capability BELOW the operating level for specific schemas, and hard-protects system schemas.
## Technical approach
- TOML policy per schema: default_mode = read_only | guarded | permissive; allow_dml; deny_ddl; deny_patterns (regex); deny_all.
- Schema extracted from the parsed ObjectName (classifier, P1-1) or SYS_CONTEXT('USERENV','CURRENT_SCHEMA').
- SYSTEM / SYS / SYSAUX are deny_all BY DEFAULT and CANNOT be unlocked by an allow-once token (or any agent action).
- Composes with operating levels (§6.6): effective capability = DB-privilege ceiling INTERSECT current_level INTERSECT per-schema policy. The policy can only narrow, never widen.
- Enforced in the guard pipeline before dispatch; violations -> structured block with a safe alternative; audited.
## Success criteria
A DELETE against a deny_ddl/read_only schema is blocked even at READ_WRITE level; SYSTEM is deny-all regardless of level/token; policy can only narrow effective capability.
## Test plan
- Unit: schema extraction from ObjectName; policy intersection with level; SYSTEM deny-all not unlockable by token.
