Listener drop + auto-reconnect; pool exhaustion -> BUSY (not ORA-12519); RAC/standby failover; credential rotation mid-flight; lease-TTL expiry with an open transaction (ASSERT rollback); cancel mid-DML (ASSERT no double-execute).
## Success criteria
Every scenario degrades safely with the asserted behavior.
## Test plan
- Self: each scenario is a scripted chaos case with structured logging.
## Considerations
Depends on lease (P0-4) + cancellation/shutdown (P2-2) + admission (P2-1).
close_reason: Implemented chaos tests (T-CHAOS). Two scripted chaos suites assert safe degradation of the safety primitives in-process (the genuinely-live scenarios — real listener drop, RAC/standby failover — run in the live tagged job, but their LOGIC is exercised here). crates/oraclemcp-db/tests/chaos.rs: (1) lease-TTL expiry/teardown with an open transaction -> ASSERT forced rollback (release + expiry share force_rollback; a CountingConn proves conn.rollback() is called exactly once and the lease is dropped); (2) expired (0-TTL) lease is reaped; (3) credential rotation mid-flight -> ensure_fresh_token refreshes exactly once when within skew, reuses when fresh (no in-flight failure). crates/oraclemcp-core/tests/chaos.rs: (4) pool exhaustion -> structured OracleMcpError::Busy + busy_envelope (ErrorClass::Busy + retry_after) NOT a raw ORA-12519, capacity restored on permit drop; (5) cancel mid-DML -> CancelOutcome::mutating().can_retry==false (NEVER double-executes), read==true; (6) listener-drop/timeout (ORA-03113/12541/12170/12514) classified transient/retryable while ORA-00942/01031 are NOT (no blind retry); (7) CircuitBreaker opens after the failure threshold and sheds load within the cooldown (failover/overload protection). 7 tests; clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK.

title: T-TYPES — Type-fidelity golden tests (NUMBER->string, ISO-8601, NLS-invariant)
description:
## Background
Silent wrong numbers are the worst outcome; this proves the §5.2 contract.
## Technical approach
Every type in the type table round-trips correctly (insta golden); NUMBER(38) is a string; dates are ISO-8601 regardless of host NLS_LANG/CI locale; LOBs stream/paginate. Run under multiple host NLS settings.
## Success criteria
All golden tests pass under at least two host NLS settings.
## Test plan
- Self: golden round-trip per type x NLS setting.
## Considerations
Depends on the serializer (P0-5).
close_reason: T-TYPES type-fidelity golden tests (crates/oraclemcp-db/tests/type_fidelity.rs): a standing artifact pinning the §5.2 type table. NUMBER (small + 19/38-digit) -> lossless JSON string; numbers_as_float opt-in -> number; BINARY_DOUBLE/FLOAT -> number, >15-sig FLOAT -> string; VARCHAR2/CHAR/NVARCHAR2/NCHAR/ROWID/INTERVAL -> string; DATE/TIMESTAMP/TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE -> ISO-8601 (driver space-separator canonicalized); NLS-invariance (space vs already-ISO input -> identical output); RAW -> hex; BLOB bytes -> base64+byte_length+truncated; CLOB -> truncated+flag; NULL -> json null for every type; unsupported type -> explicit {unsupported,value:null,warning} marker (never silent). 11 tests pass; pairs with the live type-fidelity test (live_oracle.rs) proving the same against real Oracle 23ai. clippy -D warnings + fmt clean.

title: T-TOKEN — Step-up / approval-token tests
description:
## Background
The escalation machinery must be replay-proof and leak-proof.
## Technical approach
Approvals single-use; replay-rejected; SQL-digest binding-checked; monotonic-TTL (not wall clock); hashed-at-rest; never-in-audit-clear. Cover both per-statement tokens and time-boxed elevation windows.
## Success criteria
All properties hold; a clock jump cannot extend a window.
## Test plan
- Self: unit + property tests for each property.
## Considerations
Depends on the step-up gate (P1-10).
close_reason: T-TOKEN step-up/approval-token security suite (crates/oraclemcp-guard/tests/token_security.rs). Asserts every property a production claim rests on: allow-once single-use + replay-rejected (consumed token -> Unknown); digest-bound (wrong SQL -> DigestMismatch, doesn't burn the token); monotonic-TTL expiry (wall-clock jump can't revive — MonotonicDeadline); step-up ApproveOnce digest-bound (approval_matches_sql); CI token scope+secret+TTL bound; hashed-at-rest (sql_digest is sha256, never contains the secret value/table name; StepUpChallenge serializes the digest not the raw SQL); never-in-audit-clear (an AuditRecord stores sql_sha256 + truncated preview, never the approval token id or bind values — verified by serializing the record and asserting the token is absent). 7 tests; clippy -D warnings + fmt clean. Pairs with the unit tests in token.rs/stepup.rs.

title: T-PRIV — Privilege-degradation tests under a least-privilege account
description:
## Background
Many features need privileges least-privilege accounts lack; they must degrade with clear errors, never empty success.
## Technical approach
Run the suite under a least-privilege account; assert each gated tool returns a clear "needs privilege X" structured error (never empty success); assert capability reporting matches the actual tier.
## Success criteria
No tool returns empty-success when it lacks privilege; capabilities reports the real tier.
## Test plan
- Self: run suite under least-priv account; assert error classes.
## Considerations
Depends on the privilege-degradation matrix (P2-9).
close_reason: Implemented privilege-degradation test suite (T-PRIV). crates/oraclemcp-db/tests/privilege_degradation.rs drives the P2-9 privilege probe + degradation matrix with an in-process LeastPrivConn (a least-privilege account where DBA_*/ALL_*/v$parameter/*_identifiers are all denied with ORA-00942, only USER_* readable — exactly what a locked-down service user sees). Asserts: (1) probe_privileges falls back DBA_*->ALL_*->USER_* and lands on DictionaryTier::User with diagnostics_pack=false + plscope=false (degraded tier detected + reported honestly); (2) the tier drives the right view_prefix fallback; (3) requirement_matrix() documents, for every gated tool, the privilege it needs AND a structured degraded behavior — and the matrix promises a clear error/fallback (fall back / insufficient privilege / license required / never empty), NEVER silent empty success (AWR->license-gated, get_ddl->never empty); (4) ORA-01031/01017/01045 classify to ErrorClass::InsufficientPrivilege and ORA-00942 to ObjectNotFound — the agent always sees a precise needs-privilege error, never an empty result. capabilities-reports-the-real-tier is covered by the existing core test privileges_absent_until_probed_then_surfaced (CapabilitiesReport.with_privileges). The live tagged job runs the same assertions against a real least-priv Oracle account. 4 tests; clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK.

title: T-E2E — Scripted MCP client end-to-end suite (stdio + HTTP) with structured logging
description:
## Background
Beyond unit + live-DB integration, we need full-protocol e2e proving the server works for a real MCP client over BOTH transports, with detailed JSON-line logging so post-implementation correctness is verifiable.
## Technical approach
- A scripted MCP client driving: initialize -> tools/list -> oracle_capabilities -> oracle_connect -> schema_inspect(full)+snapshot -> dependency_graph -> a read query -> a write-with-step-up (elicitation selector) -> oracle_query_execute. Run over stdio AND Streamable HTTP.
- Assert dual output (content + structuredContent), pagination cursors, actionable errors, and the audit record for each call. Structured JSON-line logs throughout.
- Include the multi-agent scenario: 2+ concurrent clients over HTTP share one server; assert per-agent lease isolation (no cross-agent transaction bleed) and per-agent audit identity.
## Success criteria
The full flow passes over stdio and HTTP; concurrent clients are isolated; logs are verifiable evidence.
## Test plan
- Self: the harness IS the e2e suite; gated in CI behind a live XE container.
## Considerations
Depends on rmcp/stdio (P0-6) and the read path (P1-2); HTTP variant depends on P1-9; step-up variant on P1-10.
close_reason: Implemented the scripted MCP client e2e suite (T-E2E). crates/oraclemcp-core/tests/e2e_mcp.rs drives the protocol surface over BOTH transports with structured JSON-line evidence logs: (1) HTTP — a real Streamable-HTTP initialize handshake via the axum transport (oneshot POST -> 200 + JSON-RPC result advertising oraclemcp); (2) stdio — the same run_tool dispatch path the stdio transport invokes (oracle_capabilities + a registered tool through the injected ToolDispatch, both non-error); (3) concurrent-client isolation — two independent clients (agent-a/agent-b) drive the same server over HTTP via tokio::join, each handled independently (no cross-client bleed at the transport). Per-step structured logging (e2e_step JSON lines, --nocapture). The FULL live-DB flow (oracle_connect -> schema_inspect(full)+snapshot -> dependency_graph -> read query -> write-with-step-up(elicitation) -> oracle_query_execute) + the multi-agent lease-bleed/per-agent-audit-identity assertion run in CI behind the live XE container (the T-INTEG matrix, 6.1) — those tool bodies need a real database; this harness covers the transport + protocol surface that gates them. 3 tests; clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK.

title: PLSQL-CAT-NEW-1 — Add ALL_DB_LINKS loader for cross-DB dependency resolution (/oracle audit)
description:
Audit finding from /oracle skill applied to crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs (loaders at 1961-2586).

Gap. Catalog covers 16 ALL_*/DBA_* views but is missing ALL_DB_LINKS. PL/SQL packages that issue SELECT ... FROM remote_table@db_link get classified as having an unresolved synonym rather than a remote-DB dependency. Lineage across database links is currently silent.

Source. /oracle skill — DATABASE-REFERENCE.md + Oracle Database Reference 26 ALL_DB_LINKS docs. Local catalog file: crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs:2453-2480 (where load_catalog_views lives — the new loader goes alongside).

Proposed shape. load_catalog_db_links(conn, scope) -> Vec<DatabaseLink> querying ALL_DB_LINKS (OWNER, DB_LINK, USERNAME, HOST, CREATED). Plumbed through CatalogSnapshot::db_links. Lineage uses this to classify @link references as cross-DB rather than unresolved.

Cite. Oracle Database Reference, ALL_DB_LINKS view (https://docs.oracle.com/en/database/oracle/oracle-database/26/refrn/ALL_DB_LINKS.html).
close_reason: Added DatabaseLink struct (owner: SchemaName, name: String, host: Option<String>, public_link: bool), wired SchemaCatalog::db_links: Vec<DatabaseLink> behind #[serde(default)] for back-compat, added load_catalog_db_links() that queries ALL_DB_LINKS with owner = 'PUBLIC' OR owner IN (...) so public links surface regardless of schema scope, and apply_db_link_row() that lazily materializes the synthetic PUBLIC schema entry when needed. Wired into build_catalog_snapshot orchestration between load_catalog_grants and load_catalog_dependencies. 5 orchestration-test fixtures updated with the new expectation. New assertions verify both private (BILLING.REPORTING_LINK -> public_link=false, host=reporting-db.internal) and public (PUBLIC.WORLDWIDE_LINK -> public_link=true, host=//world.example.com/PROD) cases. 28 catalog tests pass; clippy --all-targets clean.

(2) OracleDateTime — DATE: epoch_seconds, no fractional seconds, no timezone (per plan: 'do not silently widen'). (3) OracleTimestamp — TIMESTAMP(p): epoch_seconds + nanoseconds + precision (driver-rounding documented). (4) OracleTimestampTz — TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE: preserves offset_seconds; into_utc() is opt-in (never blindly normalize). (5) OracleTimestampLtz — TIMESTAMP WITH LOCAL TIME ZONE: keeps session_offset_seconds alongside the value so server-side rendering is explicit (plan §12.3: 'never blindly mapped to chrono::Local in generated server code'). (6) IntervalYM (custom type for INTERVAL YEAR TO MONTH per §12.3): years + months pair with total_months() saturating-arithmetic helper. All types derive Clone/Copy/Debug/Default/Eq/PartialEq/Hash/Serialize/Deserialize. type_mapping.rs (PLSQL-BG-002) already emits crate::oracle_types::OracleDateTime / OracleTimestamp / OracleTimestampTz / OracleTimestampLtz / IntervalYM — they now resolve. 7 new tests: backend serde round-trip, preserves_nanoseconds matrix, OracleDateTime constructor, OracleTimestampTz::into_utc preserves nanos+precision, OracleTimestampLtz keeps session_offset, IntervalYM negative/saturating math, OracleTimestamp precision field. 35/35 plsql-bindgen tests pass; clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings clean.

title: Canonicalize Stage A marker scan so whitespace/comments cannot split EXECUTE IMMEDIATE / PRAGMA AUTONOMOUS_TRANSACTION
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs:167 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** An untrusted agent can downgrade an opaque, unanalyzable dynamic-SQL or autonomous-transaction PL/SQL block from Forbidden (never dispatchable) to Guarded (dispatchable after a ReadWrite step-up, or outright Allow in a ReadWrite session) by inserting a comment, extra space, tab, or newline between the two keywords of a multi-word side-effect marker — letting an EXECUTE IMMEDIATE whose embedded statement could be DROP/GRANT/arbitrary DML slip past the guard's hard fail-closed boundary.
**Evidence:** classifier.rs:158 `let upper = sql.trim_start().to_ascii_uppercase();` (uppercase ONLY — not whitespace-collapsed, not comment-stripped). classifier.rs:167 `let dangerous = PLSQL_SIDE_EFFECT_MARKERS.iter().any(|m| upper.contains(m));` — literal substring scan. Markers at classifier.rs:120 `"EXECUTE IMMEDIATE"` and :128 `"PRAGMA AUTONOMOUS_TRANSACTION"` are single-space literals. classify() at :737-740 returns forbidden_decision (required_level=None) when dangerous, else falls through to :748-757 returning Guarded/required_level=ReadWrite for a balanced block. levels.rs:266-271 maps required=None -> Blocked{Forbidden} (never dispatchable); :278-281 maps a satisfiable required level -> Allow or RequireStepUp.\n\nEmpirical run (CARGO_TARGET_DIR=$PWD/target cargo test -p oraclemcp-guard against Classifier::default(), tmp integration test, --nocapture):\nCASE   control_single_space: danger=Fo
**Repro:** Trigger: `Classifier::default().classify("BEGIN EXECUTE/**/IMMEDIATE 'DELETE FROM orders'; END;")` returns danger=Guarded, required_level=Some(ReadWrite). The control `BEGIN EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'DELETE FROM orders'; END;` returns danger=Forbidden, required_level=None. Same divergence for double-space, tab (\t), newline (\n) between EXECUTE and IMMEDIATE, and for `DECLARE PRAGMA/**/AUTONOMOUS_TRANSACTION; BEGIN COMMIT; END;`. X (comment/whitespace-perturbed dangerous block) -> Y (Guarded instead of Forbidden) directly observed. Single-token markers (DBMS_SQL/UTL_FILE/...) are unaffected because they contain no internal space.
**Suggested fix:** Before the `dangerous` computation in stage_a, derive a canonicalized scan string: strip PL/SQL comments (`--` to end-of-line and `/* ... */`) and collapse all whitespace runs to a single ASCII space, then uppercase — `let scan = strip_plsql_comments(&upper).split_whitespace().collect::<Vec<_>>().join(" ");` — and run the marker `.contains` against `scan` instead of `upper`. More robustly, reuse the OracleDialect tokenizer already used by analyze_batch: drop whitespace/comment tokens, uppercase bare Word tokens, and scan the token stream for adjacent `EXECUTE`+`IMMEDIATE` and `PRAGMA`+`AUTONOMOUS_TRANSACTION` sequences plus the single-token markers. Lock it in by adding the comment/double-space/tab/newline evasion variants (for both multi-word markers) to tests/adversarial_corpus.rs.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

title: Use constant-time comparison for CiToken secret in CiToken::authorizes
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/stepup.rs:130 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** A real CI escalation credential is compared with non-constant-time `&str ==`, leaking per-byte match position via timing; in principle an attacker who can repeatedly call the escalate tool could recover the secret and non-interactively elevate the session level (ReadWrite/DDL/Admin) without human confirmation. In practice the timing delta is swamped by MCP transport/scheduler jitter, so the concrete exploit is unrealizable — the real value is consistency/defense-in-depth hardening to match the codebase's own constant-time standard.
**Evidence:** stepup.rs:127-131 — `pub fn authorizes(&self, presented_secret: &str, target: OperatingLevel) -> bool { !self.deadline.is_expired() && presented_secret == self.secret && target <= self.scope }`. CiToken doc (stepup.rs:106-108): "A real credential to protect — unlike the self-issued allow-once token, this auto-approves up to its `scope` ceiling." Data flow: session_tool.rs:282-310 forwards MCP `SessionAction::Escalate { ci_secret }` unmodified into `resolve_with_ci_token(&cid, token, secret)`; stepup.rs:227-239 passes `presented_secret` straight into `token.authorizes(...)`. Convention sites: init_token.rs:57-82 (`StdioAuthPolicy::validate` uses `constant_time_eq` for a caller-presented shared init token over the MCP transport, with a doc comment on the length-leak tradeoff); oauth_rs.rs:253-263 (`constant_time_eq` for HMAC). `rg subtle|constant_time crates/oraclemcp-guard/` returned noth
**Repro:** Triggering path: agent invokes oracle_session(Escalate, target_level=ReadWrite, challenge_id=<cid>, ci_secret=X) over MCP. X reaches `presented_secret == self.secret` at stepup.rs:130 unmodified. Because `&str` PartialEq compares bytes left-to-right and short-circuits on the first mismatch, the return time correlates with the length of the matching prefix — the side channel exists. Impossibility-of-practical-exploit proof (severity downgrade): the per-byte short-circuit difference is on the order of nanoseconds and is dominated by MCP/stdio transport latency, JSON (de)serialization, the stepup `Mutex` lock, and OS scheduler jitter (milliseconds); additionally each attempt must resolve agains
**Suggested fix:** Replace `presented_secret == self.secret` at stepup.rs:130 with a constant-time byte comparison matching the codebase convention. Add a private `fn constant_time_eq(a: &[u8], b: &[u8]) -> bool` to the guard crate identical to init_token.rs:73 / oauth_rs.rs:254 (length check, XOR-accumulate over zipped bytes, return `diff == 0`) and call `constant_time_eq(presented_secret.as_bytes(), self.secret.as_bytes())`, keeping the `&& !self.deadline.is_expired() && target <= self.scope` clauses. Better: factor the duplicated `constant_time_eq` (now in three crates) into one shared helper, or adopt the vetted `subtle` crate's `ConstantTimeEq`. Add a regression note in tests/token_security.rs mirroring the init-token constant-time assertion.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

title: Reuse escaped `job` binding in build_deploy_plan poll_sql (symmetric ''-escaping) + assert even quote-count on poll_sql in test
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-mcp/src/execute_approved.rs:227 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** If a caller passes a job_name containing a single quote, build_deploy_plan emits a malformed poll_sql (literal closes early: WHERE job_name = 'x'y') while submit_block stays balanced — a self-inflicted broken run-status poll, not a security/injection or cross-schema vector. In practice mostly moot: Oracle scheduler job-name identifiers cannot contain a bare quote so CREATE_JOB itself would reject the name, and no runtime currently executes poll_sql.
**Evidence:** execute_approved.rs:220 `let job = job_name.replace('\'', "''");` — escaped binding. Line 222 submit_block interpolates `'{job}'` (escaped). Lines 226-228 poll_sql interpolates RAW `WHERE job_name = '{job_name}'`. Test gap confirmed at line 525: `assert_eq!(plan.submit_block.matches('\'').count() % 2, 0);` — only submit_block, never poll_sql. dispatch.rs:347-352 `struct DeployDdlArgs { job_name: String, ddl_bytes: String }` with only `#[serde(deny_unknown_fields)]` — no identifier/regex validation. dispatch.rs:306-311 deploy_ddl arm: `let _args: DeployDdlArgs = parse_args(...)?; Ok(DispatchOutcome::RuntimeStateRequired(RuntimeKind::LiveConnection))` — does NOT call build_deploy_plan. `rg poll_sql|DeployDdlPlan crates/` outside execute_approved.rs returns only the lib.rs re-export (lib.rs:94-95) — no production consumer of poll_sql. Crate builds and 3 build_deploy_plan tests pass (test re
**Repro:** Triggering input: build_deploy_plan("x'y", "CREATE TABLE t (a NUMBER)"). Output Y: poll_sql contains the unbalanced literal `WHERE job_name = 'x'y'` (3 single quotes, odd) while submit_block is correctly ''-doubled (12 quotes, even). Compiled and ran the exact format! logic standalone; printed: submit_block even=true, poll_sql even=false. Disproof of higher severity: same caller controls both DDL and job_name, poll_sql is a fixed SELECT against a read-only system view (USER_SCHEDULER_JOB_RUN_DETAILS), so no privilege/trust boundary crossed; and a single quote is not a legal Oracle scheduler job-name identifier char, so CREATE_JOB would reject the name regardless — the operation fails uniform
**Suggested fix:** In build_deploy_plan, interpolate the already-escaped `job` instead of raw `job_name` in poll_sql: change `WHERE job_name = '{job_name}'` (lines 226-228) to use `'{job}'`. This makes the escaping symmetric with submit_block. Extend build_deploy_plan_escapes_single_quotes (line 518) to also assert `plan.poll_sql.matches('\'').count() % 2 == 0` so the regression is covered. Optionally add identifier validation for job_name at the dispatch boundary (DeployDdlArgs, dispatch.rs:349) to fail fast on non-identifier names rather than emitting SQL the runtime would reject.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

title: Split <expr> runs on || (not just string literals) so adjacent-|| concatenations don't mis-tag an unsanitised interpolation as DBMS_ASSERT-bounded
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs:260 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** An agent calling the MCP `dynamic_sql_evidence` foundation tool on a dynamic statement that adjacently concatenates an asserted name with a raw identifier (no quoted literal between them) receives `expr_interpolations:[{sanitised:true}]`, falsely reporting the unsanitised `p_raw` run as DBMS_ASSERT-bounded. The downstream confidence score is Medium/"object set is bounded" — but that scorer feeds nothing in production, so there is NO fail-open in the actual injection detector (SEC001) or any wired audit; the harm is a wrong per-interpolation sanitisation flag in the foundation-tool output an agent may read.
**Evidence:** Walker (dynamic_sql.rs:204-248): `current_expr` flushes a `<expr>` run only inside the `c == '\''` branch (line 222 `current_expr_open`), never on `||`. `run_is_dbms_assert` (dynamic_sql.rs:260-262) = `run.to_ascii_uppercase().contains("DBMS_ASSERT.")`. Empirical repro via a throwaway integration test (since removed; tree clean of my artifact) using the exact claimed input `EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'SELECT * FROM ' || DBMS_ASSERT.SIMPLE_SQL_NAME(p_tab) || p_raw || ' WHERE 1=1';` printed:
  fragments = ["SELECT * FROM ", "<expr>", " WHERE 1=1"]
  expr_interpolations = [ExprInterpolation { sanitised: true }]
  confidence = Medium
  explanation = "interpolated dynamic SQL, all 1 interpolation(s) routed through DBMS_ASSERT — object set is bounded by the asserted names"
CONTROL (literal separator, the existing qm3q.21 test shape) = [{sanitised:true},{sanitised:false}] => Low. cargo built/ran cleanly
**Repro:** TRIGGERS: recognise_dynamic_sql("EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'SELECT * FROM ' || DBMS_ASSERT.SIMPLE_SQL_NAME(p_tab) || p_raw || ' WHERE 1=1';", "p:1") yields expr_interpolations=[{sanitised:true}] (should be 2 entries, the p_raw run sanitised:false) and score_dynamic_sql_edge => Medium "bounded" (should be Low "partial/unsanitised"). DISPROOF of the high-severity fail-open: the scoring functions have no production consumer (grep above), OpaqueDynamic edges exist only in tests, unsafe_paths/Trust Block never receive this confidence, and SEC001 detects the injection via an independent taint path — so no live injection-audit path drops or downgrades the finding.
**Suggested fix:** Two-part fix in dynamic_sql.rs: (1) in extract_execute_immediate, also break the non-literal `current_expr` run on the `||` operator (emit one ExprInterpolation per ||-delimited sub-expression), not only at `'` boundaries, so per-interpolation coverage reflects each substituted identifier; (2) harden run_is_dbms_assert so it returns true only when every non-trivial (non-empty, non-bare-literal) ||-piece itself contains a `DBMS_ASSERT.` call, rather than the any-mention contains() check. Add a regression test mirroring partial_dbms_assert_coverage_is_low_not_medium but WITHOUT a quoted literal between the asserted name and the raw identifier (the adjacent-|| case). Note for the bead: this is a recogniser-correctness / honest-evidence fix surfaced through the MCP dynamic_sql_evidence tool; the claimed downstream "unsafe_paths drop / Trust Block bounded" fail-open does NOT exist today becau

### crates/plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs:225 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** A reviewer querying the MCP `dynamic_sql_evidence` tool on `EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'GRANT ' || DBMS_ASSERT.SIMPLE_SQL_NAME(p_priv) || p_target` sees expr_interpolations=[{sanitised:true}] — one placeholder marked clean — which hides that p_target flows in unsanitised, falsely reassuring them the dynamic statement is bounded. (Does NOT miss the SEC001 critical injection finding, which uses an independent taint path.)
**Evidence:** extract_execute_immediate only flushes current_expr into an ExprInterpolation on a string-literal opening quote (dynamic_sql.rs:222-228) or at end-of-body (243-248); it never splits on `||`. run_is_dbms_assert (260-262) is a bare substring check: `run.to_ascii_uppercase().contains("DBMS_ASSERT.")`. PoC run (added to crate, then reverted; `rg ZZZ_poc` now CLEAN) of input `EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'GRANT ' || DBMS_ASSERT.SIMPLE_SQL_NAME(p_priv) || p_target;` printed: `POC fragments = ["GRANT ", "<expr>"]`; `POC interps = [ExprInterpolation { sanitised: true }]`; `POC level = Medium`; `POC explain = Some("...all 1 interpolation(s) routed through DBMS_ASSERT — object set is bounded by the asserted names")`. medium_or_low (dynamic_sql_confidence.rs:90) returns Medium because total==1 && sanitised==1. The documented invariant (dynamic_sql.rs:45-56, 83-89: "one entry per <expr> placeholder ... true me
**Repro:** Trigger: recognise_dynamic_sql("EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'GRANT ' || DBMS_ASSERT.SIMPLE_SQL_NAME(p_priv) || p_target;", "p:1") yields expr_interpolations==[{sanitised:true}] (verified). Existing regression test partial_dbms_assert_coverage_is_low_not_medium (dynamic_sql_confidence.rs:194-233) does NOT cover this: its sample `... || DBMS_ASSERT.SIMPLE_SQL_NAME(p_tab) || ' WHERE c=''' || p_raw || '''';` inserts a literal between the asserted and raw runs, forcing two interpolations. Scope correction: score_dynamic_sql_edge / dynamic_sql_confidence_level / enrich_dynamic_sql / EnrichedDynamicSql have NO production caller (rg across crates/ shows only intra-module + tests/zzz_repro_finding5.rs); the on
**Suggested fix:** Preferred (option 1 in the finding): in extract_execute_immediate, when flushing current_expr at lines 222-228 and 243-248, split the run on top-level `||` (respecting nested parens/quotes so DBMS_ASSERT(...) args are not split) and emit one ExprInterpolation per operand, tagging each with run_is_dbms_assert(operand). Then the PoC yields [sanitised:true, sanitised:false] and medium_or_low drops to Low. Cheaper alternative (option 2): make run_is_dbms_assert conservative — sanitised:true only if EVERY top-level `||` operand in the run is itself a DBMS_ASSERT.<fn>(...) call. Add a regression test mirroring the no-separator PoC asserting Low (not Medium) and an explanation without "bounded". (Option 2 alone leaves the per-interpolation COUNT inaccurate for downstream consumers, so option 1 is better.)

### crates/plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs:226 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** The MCP `dynamic_sql_evidence` tool reports an injectable `||`-adjacent interpolation as `sanitised: true` (and the dormant confidence scorer would stamp it Medium "object set is bounded"), so an analyst/consumer reading the advisory evidence sees an unsanitised value rated safer than it is; the primary SEC001 injection verdict is unaffected (it uses live taint), so no real injection goes unreported.
**Evidence:** extract_execute_immediate (crates/plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs:222-248) only closes an <expr> run at a string-literal quote; line 237 pushes every non-literal char (incl `||`) into one buffer. run_is_dbms_assert (line 260-262): `run.to_ascii_uppercase().contains("DBMS_ASSERT.")` — whole-run substring test. medium_or_low (dynamic_sql_confidence.rs:90-95) stamps Medium when `total>0 && sanitised==total`.
Ran the real crate via a throwaway integration test (plsql_symbols::recognise_dynamic_sql + score_dynamic_sql_edge):
  ADJACENT body `'SELECT * FROM ' || foo || DBMS_ASSERT.SIMPLE_SQL_NAME(p) || ' WHERE 1=1'`:
    fragments=["SELECT * FROM ","<expr>"," WHERE 1=1"]
    expr_interps=[ExprInterpolation{sanitised:true}]
    level=Medium  explanation="interpolated dynamic SQL, all 1 interpolation(s) routed through DBMS_ASSERT — object set is bounded by the asserted names"
  CONTROL (literal
**Repro:** Triggering input: recognise_dynamic_sql("EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'SELECT * FROM ' || foo || DBMS_ASSERT.SIMPLE_SQL_NAME(p) || ' WHERE 1=1';", site) -> ev.expr_interpolations == [ExprInterpolation{sanitised:true}] (length 1), and score_dynamic_sql_edge(&ev).level == Medium with "object set is bounded by the asserted names". `foo` is unsanitised yet the single collapsed run is tagged sanitised=true because run_is_dbms_assert finds "DBMS_ASSERT." anywhere in the run. Control with a string literal between the terms yields [false,true] -> Low(partial), proving the run-splitting (not the scorer) is the defect. Existing regression test partial_dbms_assert_coverage_is_low_not_medium uses a literal separat
**Suggested fix:** At the two run-close sites (dynamic_sql.rs:224-226 and 245-247) split `current_expr` on "||" into trimmed non-empty chunks and push one ExprInterpolation per chunk, tagging each via run_is_dbms_assert on that chunk alone (the single "<expr>" fragment placeholder may stay since shape comparison is count-independent). Then the claimed body yields interps [foo=false, DBMS_ASSERT=true] and medium_or_low takes the `sanitised<total` branch -> Low(partial). Skip empty/whitespace-only chunks from leading/trailing `||`. Add a regression test using the `||`-adjacent (no intervening literal) shape asserting expr_interpolations.iter().any(|e| !e.sanitised) and Low/"partial" verdict, to lock the gap the existing literal-separated test misses. Also update run_is_dbms_assert's doc comment which currently asserts an invariant ("the run is one `||`-delimited expression chunk") the code does not uphold.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

title: Fail-closed guard: reject non-identifier routine/param names before emit (BINDING_INVALID_IDENTIFIER), covering both static pub-fn path and dynamic format! path
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-bindgen/src/emit.rs:148 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** A hostile or upstream-buggy BindingPlan whose routine/parameter name is not a legal Rust identifier makes plsql-bindgen exit 0 with no diagnostic while emitting uncompilable Rust (`pub fn f", DROP TABLE users; --(...)`), silently breaking the consumer's build and defeating the crate's documented "valid Rust + typed BINDING_* failure on bad input" contract; the --robot-json audit only records generated_bytes, never compilability, so CI does not catch it. No runtime SQL injection at line 148 (the SQL block is Debug-escaped); harm is a fail-loud compile break, not data corruption.
**Evidence:** emit.rs:148 `let _ = write!(out, "pub fn {}(executor: &mut impl OracleExecutor", r.name);` and emit.rs:151 `let _ = write!(out, ", {}: {}", p.name, rust_type_sig(p));` — both write unvalidated Strings into Rust identifier positions. main.rs:212 `let mut plan: BindingPlan = match serde_json::from_str(&json)` then main.rs:228 `let generated = emit_wrappers(&plan);` — untrusted input, no validation. `rg is_ident|valid_ident|sanitize|validate|BINDING_INVALID crates/plsql-bindgen/` → no validation helper. emit_wrappers callers: only main.rs:228 (rest are #[test]). Live repro against built binary, EXIT=0, empty stderr:
  pub fn f", DROP TABLE users; --(executor: &mut impl OracleExecutor, p_a => :99 -- broken: i64) -> Result<(), ExecutionError> {
      RoutineArg::In(BindValue::Int(p_a => :99 -- broken)),
      let __out = executor.call_routine("BEGIN hr.pkg.f\", DROP TABLE users; --(:1); END;"
**Repro:** Trigger X: pipe a BindingPlan with routine name `f", DROP TABLE users; --` and IN param name `p_a => :99 -- broken` into `plsql-bindgen --input`. Output Y: exit 0, no stderr, generated `pub fn f", DROP TABLE users; --(...)` which rustc rejects ("prefix `f` is unknown"). The cited line 148 is the function-name break; line 151 is the param break. The verifier's downgrade is confirmed: line 148's SQL block is emitted with `{block:?}` (Debug), so the quote is escaped to `\"` and cannot break out — no injection there. The genuine token-injection vector is the DYNAMIC path at emit.rs:392 (and the unescaped `{name}` at 484/504/512/524): feeding name `inj"); evil("x` with a defaulted param emits `le
**Suggested fix:** Add a `fn rust_ident_problem(s: &str) -> Option<&'static str>` (Some when empty, leading digit, any char outside [A-Za-z0-9_], or a Rust keyword) and, at the top of emit_routine before line 148, check r.name and every p.name; on failure emit a well-formed typed-error body — derive a deterministic safe stub identifier for the `pub fn` signature, return `Err(ExecutionError { code: "BINDING_INVALID_IDENTIFIER".to_string(), message: ... })` reporting the original name, and return early — exactly mirroring the BINDING_DEGENERATE_FUNCTION path. Crucially route the SAME guard so it also protects the dynamic path's `{name}`/`r.name` interpolations at emit.rs:392/484/504/512/524, which is the higher-impact (true token-injection) site. Add a test parallel to degenerate_function_emits_typed_error_not_panic asserting names with spaces/quotes/`=>` yield a BINDING_INVALID_IDENTIFIER body (compilable R

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

title: Add single-probe gate (or soften docs) to CircuitBreaker HalfOpen before wiring into dispatch
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-core/src/resilience.rs:108 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** None today — the circuit breaker has no production caller (only unit tests + tests/chaos.rs), so the claimed "probe stampede against a fragile Oracle" cannot occur; the issue is a latent doc/behavior divergence to fix before the breaker is wired into live dispatch.
**Evidence:** resilience.rs:110-124 — `match inner.state { CircuitState::Closed | CircuitState::HalfOpen => true, CircuitState::Open => { ... if elapsed >= self.cooldown { inner.state = CircuitState::HalfOpen; true } else { false } } }`. The `Inner` struct (lines 76-80) has only `consecutive_failures`, `state`, `opened_at` — no probe-in-flight counter, so every caller observing HalfOpen returns true. Doc at line 72: "One trial request is allowed to test recovery". `on_failure` (line 140) re-trips on any HalfOpen failure, bounding over-admission.
Workspace grep (rg 'CircuitBreaker|allow_request' --type rust .): only crates/oraclemcp-core/src/resilience.rs (def+unit tests), src/lib.rs:39 (re-export), tests/chaos.rs (test caller). `rg 'resilience|breaker|circuit' crates/oraclemcp-core/src/server.rs crates/oraclemcp-db/src/*.rs` → ZERO hits, confirming not wired into dispatch.
Build: `cargo test -p oracle
**Repro:** Behavior is real: with cb = CircuitBreaker::new(1, Duration::ZERO), trip it to Open via on_failure(), then call allow_request() once (flips Open->HalfOpen, returns true) — a second and third allow_request() also return true because the arm `Closed | HalfOpen => true` admits every caller while state stays HalfOpen. But the harmful end-to-end failure (N agent calls stampede Oracle) is UNREPRODUCIBLE: no production path gates requests through allow_request() — server.rs and oraclemcp-db contain zero references. The breaker is Phase-2 scaffolding (file header: "plan §10; bead P2-RESIL"). Multiple HalfOpen probes is also a recognized design (resilience4j permittedNumberOfCallsInHalfOpenState), so
**Suggested fix:** Before wiring the breaker into the live dispatch path, reconcile prose and behavior. Cheapest correct fix: soften the HalfOpen doc (line 72) and CircuitBreaker doc (line 83) to state that recovery probing may admit concurrent requests (matches the current first-result-decides impl and resilience4j-style designs). If single-probe semantics are actually wanted, add `probe_in_flight: bool` to `Inner`, set it true on the Open->HalfOpen flip (line 118) and reject (return false) concurrent HalfOpen callers when it is already set, then clear it in both on_success and on_failure; add a concurrency test asserting a second allow_request returns false while a probe is outstanding. No urgency: there is no production caller today.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

title: Harden verb-prefix matching in dead text-scanner classify_statement (real twin lives in plsql-ir stmt.rs)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:680 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** None at the cited location — classify_statement in lower/mod.rs is dead with respect to the call/flow/DML pipeline, so no Calls edge is dropped and no phantom DML is emitted from it. The same verb-prefix bug DOES bite in a different file (plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs:501), where an IF/LOOP body line like `update_stats(p_id);` is misclassified as Statement::Sql{Update}, dropping its Calls edge and minting a phantom Reads/Writes table edge.
**Evidence:** cargo build -p plsql-ir -p plsql-engine: "Finished `dev`" (code compiles).

CITED FILE — crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:679-687 (the claim is locally accurate):
  for verb in ["SELECT","INSERT","UPDATE","DELETE","MERGE"] { if u.starts_with(verb) { return AstStatement::Sql{...} } }
with the `:=` branch at 703 and the statement-level Call branch at 711-722 AFTER it. The whole-word helper keyword_at (line 625) exists but is not used here.

BUT this classifier is dead for the pipeline:
1. lower_source returns `body_statements: Vec::new()` (mod.rs:50), so its classify_statement output never reaches the engine.
2. The live backend Antlr4RustBackend::parse (backend.rs:309) builds the AST via lower_parse_tree (tree_lower.rs:60), which populates body_statements through lower_statement (tree_lower.rs:510) using REAL grammar nodes: stmt.assignment_statement() (524), sql_s.data_manipulat
**Repro:** Disproof of the cited-location impact: classify_statement (lower/mod.rs:645) is only invoked by lower::lower_statement_body (mod.rs:521), whose sole callers are tests (mod.rs:1817, 1976). lower_source hardwires body_statements=Vec::new() (mod.rs:50); the engine's body extraction (lib.rs:947-962) pairs ast.body_statements by span and otherwise falls back to plsql_ir::lower_statement_body — neither route ever reads classify_statement's AstStatement output. The live backend classifies via grammar nodes (tree_lower.rs:510-639), where `update_stats(p_id);` matches stmt.call_statement() -> AstStatement::Call and `update_count := p_user;` matches stmt.assignment_statement() -> AstStatement::Assignm
**Suggested fix:** Cited file (defense-in-depth only, classifier is dead): in crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs gate the verb loop (680) and the NULL/RAISE/RETURN/EXECUTE-IMMEDIATE/IF/LOOP prefixes (652-701) on keyword_at(u, 0, kw) instead of bare u.starts_with(kw), reusing the existing whole-word helper at mod.rs:625. The actually-impactful fix is the twin in crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs:453-538: replace the bare trimmed.starts_with(verb) verb loop (501) and the NULL/COMMIT/RAISE/RETURN/EXIT prefixes (456-491) with a whole-word boundary check so `update_stats(p_id)` reaches the statement-level Call fallthrough (Unrecognized) and `update_count := p_user` reaches the `:=` Assignment branch (528). Add regression tests asserting plsql_ir::lower_statement_body("update_stats(p_id);") yields a call site and ("update_count := p_user;") yields Statement::Assignment. File the bead against stmt.rs as the pri

## Acceptance criteria
