- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

title: coverage_report keys per-routine state by name, miscounting overloaded PL/SQL packages
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-bindgen/src/doctor.rs:79 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** If a consumer calls the public coverage_report API on a BindingPlan for an overloaded PL/SQL package (legal duplicate routine names), the doctor report misreports skipped/clean/emit_percent and lists duplicate names in skipped_routines_sample; purely a report-accuracy cosmetic issue, no codegen or safety impact, and no in-repo caller currently exercises the overloaded path.
**Evidence:** doctor.rs:79-82 keys state by name: `let mut by_routine: BTreeMap<&str, RoutineState> = BTreeMap::new(); for routine in &plan.routines { by_routine.insert(routine.name.as_str(), RoutineState::default()); }`. Read-back loop doctor.rs:100-113 iterates `&plan.routines` once per binding and re-reads the shared name-keyed state. lib.rs:62-63 documents `name` as "Subprogram name as it appears in the package". overload.rs:1-8 confirms PL/SQL overloading produces duplicate names; `fn disambiguate_overloads(routines: &[RoutineBinding]) -> Vec<String>` (overload.rs:67) returns a PARALLEL Vec and takes &[..] (cannot mutate name). `rg disambiguate_overloads crates/` shows callers only in overload.rs tests + the lib.rs:34 re-export — NO production caller. emit.rs:148 writes `r.name` directly. `rg coverage_report crates/` shows the only non-doctor callers are tests/xe_roundtrip.rs:209 (CI gate) and th
**Repro:** Trigger: build a BindingPlan with routines = [hire, hire, list] and diagnostics = [Skip targeting "hire"], then call coverage_report. The two "hire" bindings collapse to one name-keyed RoutineState (has_skip=true), and the read-back loop counts BOTH as skipped. Reproduced standalone (mirror of doctor.rs logic): reports skipped=2, emitted_clean=1, emit_percent=33, skipped_routines_sample=["hire","hire"], versus the per-binding truth skipped=1, emitted_clean=2, emit_percent=66. NOTE the finding's claim that "emitted+skipped==total breaks" is FALSE: the read-back loop tallies once per binding, so the invariant always holds (repro prints `true`); the genuine defects are the skewed clean/skip spl
**Suggested fix:** Key per-routine state per binding index instead of per name: build a Vec<RoutineState> of length plan.routines.len() plus a name->indices map, fan each name-targeted diagnostic out to all bindings sharing that name, and tally each index exactly once so skipped/clean/emit_percent and the sample reflect per-binding reality (dedup the sample if duplicate names are undesirable). The finding's full sketch is reasonable, but drop the incorrect "invariant breaks" justification — the sum already equals routines_total. Add a regression test with two same-named overloads where only one carries a Skip. Lower priority since no active caller exercises the overloaded path.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

title: Fix UTF-8 char-boundary panic in replace_case_insensitive fast path (content-driven DoS in sanitize)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-mcp/src/query.rs:337 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** A single crafted VARCHAR2/CLOB cell from a hostile/compromised database (e.g. value "İuser: Ω" with U+0130 + U+2126 OHM, or "aaİassistant: ẞ") makes sanitize() panic mid-codepoint; with release panic="abort" this aborts the entire plsql-mcp server process — a remote, content-driven denial of service that defeats the very scrubber meant to defend against malicious DB content.
**Evidence:** query.rs:337 guard: `if hay_lower.len() == haystack.len() && needle_lower.len() == needle.len()` then slices ORIGINAL haystack at hay_lower offsets (line 340 `&haystack[cursor..start]`, line 344 `&haystack[cursor..]`). Empirical byte deltas: İ(U+0130) 2→3 (+1); Ω(U+2126 OHM) 3→2 (−1); ẞ(U+1E9E) 3→2 (−1). For "İuser: Ω"(U+2126): haystack.len()=11, lower_len=11 → guard FIRES; find() returns offset 3 in hay_lower coords, cursor=9, but byte 9 is inside 'Ω'(bytes 8..11). Ran REAL public sanitize() via temporary probe test (now removed, git status clean): `cargo test -p plsql-mcp probe_sanitize_charboundary` → `thread 'query::tests::probe_sanitize_charboundary_ohm' panicked at crates/plsql-mcp/src/query.rs:344:31: start byte index 9 is not a char boundary; it is inside 'Ω' (bytes 8..11) of \`İuser: Ω\`` and `..._eszett ... panicked at crates/plsql-mcp/src/query.rs:344:31: start byte index 16 i
**Repro:** Trigger X→Y: input "İuser: Ω" where Ω = U+2126 OHM SIGN (NOT U+03A9), or "aaİassistant: ẞ" (ẞ = U+1E9E). Passed to sanitize() → fast-path guard fires (total lengths equal via canceling lowercase byte deltas) → slice of original haystack at hay_lower offset lands mid-codepoint → panic "byte index N is not a char boundary" → process abort. Correction to claim: the literal Ω must be U+2126 OHM (3→2 bytes); U+03A9 GREEK CAPITAL OMEGA is 2→2 bytes and would make hay_lower.len()(11) != haystack.len()(10), routing to the safe else branch (verified: produces "İ[redacted]Ω" with no panic). The ẞ (U+1E9E) variant in the claim is correct as written.
**Suggested fix:** Adopt the claim's fix: eliminate the hay_lower/haystack offset cross-use. Since reachable needles are ASCII, replace the length-guard fast path with an eq_ignore_ascii_case windowed byte scan over the original string (gate on needle.is_ascii(), else fall back to a char_indices()-based per-codepoint case-fold scan that only indexes the original at char_indices() positions). Add regression tests asserting sanitize("İuser: \u{2126}") and sanitize("aaİassistant: ẞ") return without panicking and redact the marker.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

title: Word-boundary the keyword-prefix tests in IR classify() so verb-prefixed assignments are not swallowed (taint fail-open)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs:501 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** A PL/SQL assignment whose LHS local starts with a keyword/verb (return_*, null_*, update_*, delete_*, commit_*, select_*, insert_*, exit_*, raise_*, merge_*) inside any IF/LOOP/FOR/WHILE body (or the parse-recovery fallback) is misclassified as Return/Null/Sql/TransactionControl/etc., so the assignment is dropped by flow_intra::walk; user-tainted data laundered through such a local (e.g. return_val := p_user; v_sql := return_val;) is never recorded as tainted, producing a SQL-injection fail-open in the taint pass plus phantom/lost dml_edges and call extraction.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs:453-538 classify(): `if trimmed.starts_with("NULL")` (456), COMMIT/ROLLBACK/SAVEPOINT (459-461), RAISE (466), RETURN (475), EXIT (484), the SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/MERGE loop (501-516), all bare starts_with with no word boundary; the `:=` arm `if let Some((lhs, rhs)) = text.split_once(":=")` is LAST at line 528. flow_intra.rs:143-171 walk() matches only Assignment/If/ForLoop/WhileLoop/BareLoop, with `_ => {}` no-op at line 169. Reproduced via temporary integration test (now removed; git status clean of my file): INPUT "return_val := p_user;" -> [Return { value_text: Some("_val := p_user") }]; "update_x := p_user;" -> [Sql { verb: Update }]; "null_count := 5;" -> [Null]; "commit_count := 1;" -> [TransactionControl { verb: "COMMIT_COUNT" }]; "exit_code := 0;" -> [Exit]; "raise_amount := 100;" -> [Raise { exception: Some("_amount := 100") }]; "delete_flag"/"s
**Repro:** Trigger X: body "return_val := p_user; v_sql := return_val;" with TaintSources { user_input_names: ["p_user"] }. Wrong output Y: lower_statement_body yields [Return { value_text: Some("_val := p_user") }, Assignment { target: "v_sql", rhs_text: "return_val" }]; analyze_flow returns env.get("v_sql").taint.kinds == [] (flags_alarm()==false). Correct behavior (control through a non-keyword local v_tmp) returns kinds == [UserInput]. The divergence proves the assignment is swallowed before the `:=` arm. Inside an IF/LOOP body the same input is reached even on the preferred AST path (lib.rs:670-671 re-lower via classify).
**Suggested fix:** Either add a trailing word-boundary helper (keyword matches only when the next char is not [A-Za-z0-9_$#]) to every starts_with test in classify(), or — more robustly — check for a top-level `:=` (outside string literals) and emit Statement::Assignment BEFORE the keyword classifiers, since a top-level `:=` makes the statement unambiguously an assignment. Add regression tests: lower_statement_body("return_val := p_user;") yields Statement::Assignment { target: "return_val", .. } (and for null_*/update_*/commit_* prefixes), plus a flow_intra test asserting v_sql is tainted for "return_val := p_user; v_sql := return_val;".

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

title: Carve out DBMS_ASSERT.NOOP (and SYS-prefixed asserts) in flow_intra taint sanitizer branch
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/flow_intra.rs:235 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** A PL/SQL unit that wraps raw user input in DBMS_ASSERT.NOOP (Oracle's documented identity pass-through that performs no validation) before EXECUTE IMMEDIATE is silently reported clean by SEC001, so a genuine SQL-injection path goes undetected by the product's flagship Critical injection rule.
**Evidence:** flow_intra.rs:233-235 — `Expr::Call { callee, args } => { let path = callee.parts.join(".").to_ascii_uppercase(); if path.starts_with("DBMS_ASSERT.") {` matches ALL DBMS_ASSERT functions uniformly. Lines 250-263: args are computed into an isolated `sanitized` flow, `TaintCleanser::DbmsAssert` is recorded, and the live `kinds` are dropped (never merged into parent `flow`). flow.rs:169 `pub fn flags_alarm(&self) -> bool { !self.kinds.is_empty() }`. flow_query.rs:84 `let is_tainted = !kinds.is_empty();` feeds rules.rs:83-84 `let answer = ctx.flow.taint_of(site); if answer.is_tainted {` — the SEC001 ExecuteImmediate sink. Cross-crate contrast confirmed: plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs:365-391 enumerates NOOP alongside the real sanitizers AND stores the specific `function: fname` in each DbmsAssertCall, proving per-function discrimination is feasible and that the project already distinguishe
**Repro:** Added a temporary unit test in flow_intra.rs and ran `cargo test -p plsql-ir ... -- --nocapture` (then reverted; `git status --porcelain` shows the file clean). Output:
  NOOP kinds=[] cleansed_by=[DbmsAssert] flags_alarm=false        (for v_sql := DBMS_ASSERT.NOOP(p_user); with p_user a UserInput source)
  concat-NOOP kinds=[] cleansed_by=[DbmsAssert] flags_alarm=false  (for v_sql := 'SELECT * FROM ' || DBMS_ASSERT.NOOP(p_user);)
  SYS-prefixed SIMPLE_SQL_NAME kinds=[UserInput] cleansed_by=[] flags_alarm=true  (secondary gap: a real sanitizer with a schema prefix is treated as transparent — fail-closed/over-reporting)
The first two are the fail-open: raw user input reaches a dynamic-SQL sin
**Suggested fix:** In flow_intra.rs replace the `path.starts_with("DBMS_ASSERT.")` guard with logic that (a) tolerates an optional schema prefix (e.g. detect via `let seg: Vec<&str> = path.split('.').collect(); matches!(seg.as_slice(), [.., "DBMS_ASSERT", _])`), and (b) only takes the sanitizer branch when the trailing segment is a real validating function (allowlist: SIMPLE_SQL_NAME / SCHEMA_NAME / ENQUOTE_NAME / SQL_OBJECT_NAME / ENQUOTE_LITERAL, mirroring plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs). For NOOP or any unrecognized DBMS_ASSERT entry point, fall through to the transparent branch (lines 268-274) so the argument's taint reaches the parent and still alarms. Add regression tests: `... || DBMS_ASSERT.NOOP(p_user)` must yield flags_alarm()==true; `SYS.DBMS_ASSERT.SIMPLE_SQL_NAME(p_tab)` must yield flags_alarm()==false. Severity high (not critical): a real fail-open in a Critical injection rule, but it requi

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

title: Wire disambiguate_overloads into emit_wrappers so overloaded packages produce compilable Rust
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-bindgen/src/emit.rs:61 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** Feeding any overloaded PL/SQL package (extremely common — two+ subprograms sharing a name, e.g. hire(id)/hire(name)) to the bindgen CLI silently emits a .rs file with duplicate `pub fn` items, so the operator's subsequent `cargo build` fails with rustc E0428 and the generator's core promise (emit compilable wrappers) is broken for that package.
**Evidence:** emit.rs:53-69 emit_wrappers iterates plan.routines directly; emit.rs:148 `let _ = write!(out, "pub fn {}(executor: &mut impl OracleExecutor", r.name);` writes the PL/SQL name verbatim with no dedup. overload.rs:67 disambiguate_overloads exists with doc "Without this the emitter could write two identical `pub fn`s into one module (rustc E0428)." Workspace grep proves it is never called by production code:
  $ rg -n 'disambiguate_overloads' --type rust crates/
  overload.rs:67 (def), overload.rs:{271,278,289,...,468} (all inside #[cfg(test)]), lib.rs:34 (pub use re-export only)
main.rs:212 deserializes BindingPlan via serde_json::from_str; main.rs:228 `let generated = emit_wrappers(&plan);` with no name normalization. lib.rs:62-63 documents RoutineBinding.name as "Subprogram name as it appears in the package". No production BindingPlan/RoutineBinding builder exists — every struct literal i
**Repro:** Minimal triggering input: a BindingPlan with two routines both named "hire" differing only by parameter (one `p_emp_id IN i64`, one `p_emp_name IN String`). Piped through `plsql-bindgen --input plan.json` it exits 0 and writes a module containing two `pub fn hire(...)` items. Compiling that module fails: `error[E0428]: the name `hire` is defined multiple times`. Input X (2-overload plan) → wrong output Y (uncompilable Rust, no skip diagnostic). The already-written, already-tested disambiguate_overloads would assign distinct names (test two_overloads_get_param_name_suffixes asserts ["hire_p_emp_id","hire_p_emp_name"]) but is never invoked on the emit path.
**Suggested fix:** In emit_wrappers, compute names once: `let names = crate::overload::disambiguate_overloads(&plan.routines);` then `for (i, routine) in plan.routines.iter().enumerate() { emit_routine(&mut out, &plan.package_id, routine, &names[i]); }`. Change emit_routine to take `rust_name: &str` and use it for the `pub fn {rust_name}(...)` identifier (emit.rs:148) and all emitted error-message interpolations, but keep `r.name` for the composed `BEGIN {package_id}.{r.name}(...)` block so the server-side call still targets the real overloaded PL/SQL name. Add a regression test feeding a 2-overload plan and asserting two distinct pub fn names with no duplicate. Optionally emit a doc-comment on suffixed wrappers recording the original overload name.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

title: DELETE-with-subquery mis-tags WHERE sub-SELECT tables as Write in dml_edges and sql_resolve
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/dml_edges.rs:178 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** A DELETE whose WHERE clause reads a staging/lookup table via a subquery (e.g. DELETE FROM t WHERE id IN (SELECT id FROM staging)) emits a spurious `Writes staging` dependency/lineage edge with reversed data-flow direction, corrupting the dependency graph and potentially feeding wrong over-privilege/impact findings in this SQL-guard product. This is a very common Oracle pattern, so the false write is frequently triggered. No crash, data loss, or fail-open security bypass.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-ir/src/dml_edges.rs:178-182 DELETE arm pushes EVERY FROM table as Write:
  SqlVerb::Delete => {
      for t in tables_after(&upper, raw, "FROM") {
          push(out, t, AccessKind::Write);
      }
  }
tables_after (lines 198-228) loops over ALL whole-word "FROM" occurrences and never stops at clause keywords. Twin defect crates/plsql-ir/src/sql_resolve.rs:75-79 DELETE arm tags every tables_after_keyword(...,"FROM") triple TableUsageKind::Write (the STOP list bounds a single FROM run but the outer while-loop re-finds the subquery FROM).

End-to-end run through the real public API (cargo test -p plsql-ir, --nocapture):
  ACCESSES = [TableAccess { table: "T", access: Write }, TableAccess { table: "STAGING", access: Write }]
  dml_edges STAGING write=true read=false
  MODEL tables = [TableUse { table: "T", usage: Write }, TableUse { table: "STAGING", usage: Write }]
  sql_resol
**Repro:** Triggering input X = `DELETE FROM t WHERE id IN (SELECT id FROM staging)`. Wrong output Y: STAGING classified AccessKind::Write (dml_edges) / TableUsageKind::Write (sql_resolve), when STAGING is read-only. Verified twice: (1) standalone rustc replica of tables_after returns ["t","staging"] and pushes both Write; ["employees","closed_depts"] for a second realistic case. (2) Integration test through plsql_ir::lower_statement_body -> extract_table_accesses and plsql_ir::sql_resolve::resolve_sql produced [T:Write, STAGING:Write] with STAGING write=true read=false. Throwaway test removed after confirming.
**Suggested fix:** In dml_edges.rs SqlVerb::Delete arm, treat only the FIRST tables_after(&upper, raw, "FROM") entry as AccessKind::Write (the DELETE target) and the remaining FROM entries as AccessKind::Read (subquery reads); also add the JOIN tables as Read. Apply the symmetric fix in sql_resolve.rs:75-79: first tables_after_keyword(...,"FROM") triple is TableUsageKind::Write, the rest Read. A more robust long-term fix bounds the DELETE target run at the WHERE/clause boundary (as tables_after_keyword's STOP list already does within a single occurrence) and recurses the WHERE sub-SELECT through the SELECT read path. Add regression tests asserting DELETE FROM t WHERE id IN (SELECT id FROM staging) yields T=Write and STAGING=Read (never STAGING=Write) in both modules.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

title: Validate/escape identifiers in plsql-bindgen dynamic path so untrusted BindingPlans fail closed with a typed BINDING_* body instead of emitting uncompilable Rust
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-bindgen/src/emit.rs:392 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** An untrusted/malformed BindingPlan whose package_id or routine name contains a double-quote or brace (routed through the defaulted-parameter dynamic path) makes plsql-bindgen emit exit-0 but UNCOMPILABLE Rust instead of the fail-closed typed BINDING_* diagnostic the module contractually guarantees for every other attacker-shaped plan — a fail-open codegen-contract violation on the documented CLI input surface. The "second-order PL/SQL injection into the live DB" escalation does NOT hold: the same identifier is emitted raw into the `pub fn` signature and `match &name {` scrutinee, so any name carrying PL/SQL metacharacters fails `cargo build` and never reaches Oracle.
**Evidence:** emit.rs:390-394 (dynamic path) hand-writes the block with raw interpolation:
`    let __block = format!(\"BEGIN {prefix}{package_id}.{}({{}}); END;\", __call.join(\", \"));` , r.name`
contrast emit.rs:267-269 (static path) which is safe: `executor.call_routine({block:?}, &__args)?` (Debug-escaped).
Fields are plain serde String with NO validation: lib.rs:48 `pub package_id: String`, :63 `pub name: String` (RoutineBinding), :86 `pub name: String` (ParameterBinding). main.rs:212 `serde_json::from_str(&json)` then main.rs:228 `emit_wrappers(&plan)` with no guard between. rg for valid|sanitiz|is_ident|escape|validate in the crate returns only flag-name/comment hits.

Run (built ./target/debug/plsql-bindgen):
- repro_quote.json (name `f\", DROP TABLE t; --`, defaulted param) → EXIT 0, emitted:
  `pub fn f\", DROP TABLE t; --(executor: ...`
  `    let __block = format!(\"BEGIN hr.emp_pkg.f\", 
**Repro:** TRIGGER (codegen-contract violation, confirmed): JSON BindingPlan {package_id:"hr.emp_pkg", routines:[{name:"f\", DROP TABLE t; --", kind:"procedure", parameters:[{name:"p_id", mode:"IN", rust_type:{path:"i64",nullable:false}, has_default:true}], return_type:null}]} → plsql-bindgen exits 0 and emits Rust that rustc rejects (prefix `f` unknown / unexpected closing delimiter), instead of the contractual exit-0 + valid-Rust + typed BINDING_* fail-closed body (emit.rs:161-165). DISPROOF of the live-DB injection escalation: for the runtime __block to be poisoned the output must first compile, but the param/routine name is emitted RAW into `pub fn {name}(` (emit.rs:151) and `match &{name} {` (emit
**Suggested fix:** Add an identifier-validation gate (the module's existing fail-closed pattern) before interpolating r.name / package_id / p.name into generated Rust. Validate package_id as dot-qualified segments matching ^[A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9_$#]*$ and routine/parameter names as a single such segment; on failure, emit a typed BINDING_INVALID_IDENTIFIER body (mirroring BINDING_DEGENERATE_FUNCTION at emit.rs:166-178 / BINDING_UNSUPPORTED_MARSHALLING at 183-194) and return, keeping exit 0 + compilable Rust. Cover BOTH the static and dynamic paths and every raw-identifier position: the `pub fn {name}` signature (emit.rs:148), the `{name}: {sig}` parameter (emit.rs:151), the `match &{name}` scrutinee and `format!(\"{name} => :{{__n}}\")` literals (emit.rs:484/498/504/512/524), and the dynamic `__block` literal (emit.rs:392). Add tests feeding names containing `\"`, `\\`, `{`, `}`, `)`, `;`, `=>` and assert the 

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

title: Fix parse_target_schema to normalise whitespace around the schema-qualifier dot (cross-schema confirmation fail-open)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-mcp/src/create_or_replace.rs:226 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** An agent can deploy a CREATE OR REPLACE into a third schema (e.g. principal BILLING, DDL header `... ANALYTICS . PKG ...`) while the operator-typed-destination cross-schema confirmation is silently skipped — the write is misclassified SameSchema and waved through; Oracle still routes the object to ANALYTICS. The human byte-exact preview step remains as a backstop, so it is not fully unmitigated.
**Evidence:** create_or_replace.rs:210-237 parse_target_schema(): `let tokens = upper.split_whitespace().collect(); let name_idx = 3 + kind.split_whitespace().count(); let name_token = name_raw.split('(').next()...; match name_token.split_once('.') { Some((owner,object)) if !owner.is_empty() && !object.is_empty() => Ok(Some(owner)), _ => Ok(None) }`. Standalone reproduction of this exact logic (rustc -O): `ANALYTICS.PKG` => parsed=Some("ANALYTICS"); `ANALYTICS . PKG` => parsed=None; `ANALYTICS .PKG` => parsed=None; `ANALYTICS. PKG` => parsed=None (classify_kind returns PACKAGE BODY for all four, so all pass the kind gate and get previewed). Downstream execute_approved.rs:130-133: `let parsed_schema = parse_target_schema(&verified.ddl_bytes)?; let effective_target = parsed_schema.map(..).unwrap_or_else(|| req.principal_schema...)` — None => effective=principal. cross_schema.rs:98-105: principal_upper==
**Repro:** Trigger: agent submits DDL `CREATE OR REPLACE PACKAGE BODY ANALYTICS . PKG AS BEGIN NULL; END;` to create_or_replace dry-run (accepted, classified PACKAGE BODY, token minted), then execute_approved with principal_schema="BILLING", target_schema="" (or "BILLING"), operator_typed_schema=None. parse_target_schema returns None -> effective_target="BILLING" -> caller_target empty so agreement check at execute_approved.rs:139 is skipped -> require_cross_schema_confirmation("BILLING","BILLING",None) takes principal==target branch -> confirmed:true SameSchema, no typed confirmation demanded. Oracle (proven live) routes the package body to schema ANALYTICS. The no-space `ANALYTICS.PKG` form correctly
**Suggested fix:** In parse_target_schema (create_or_replace.rs:217-237), reassemble the object-name region from name_idx onward and collapse whitespace around the qualifier dot before split_once('.'), e.g. `let tail = tokens[name_idx..].join(" "); let normalised = tail.replace(" . ", ".").replace(" .", ".").replace(". ", "."); let name_token = normalised.split_whitespace().next().unwrap_or("").split('(').next().unwrap_or("");` so `OWNER . OBJECT`/`OWNER .OBJECT`/`OWNER. OBJECT` all yield Some(OWNER). Prefer failing CLOSED on a malformed/ambiguous dotted form (owner or object empty) rather than Ok(None). Add regression tests for all three spaced forms plus a PROCEDURE param-list form `OPS . DO_IT(p IN NUMBER)` (normalise before the '(' cut), and an execute_approved test asserting a BILLING principal with header `... ANALYTICS . PKG ...` is NOT SameSchema and requires the operator-typed destination. A real 

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

title: Include sql_preview in the audit tamper-evidence hash (documented "any edit breaks verification" invariant is false for the only operator-legible field)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/record.rs:177 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** An actor with write access to the append-only audit log (the exact threat the hash chain defends against) can rewrite the only human-legible record of a statement — sql_preview, e.g. change "DELETE FROM payroll WHERE ..." to "SELECT 1" — on a persisted Guarded/Destructive line without touching sql_sha256, and chain verification still reports the log as untampered, silently misleading a forensic reviewer. The documented invariant "any edit or reorder breaks verification" is false for this field.
**Evidence:** crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/record.rs:177 — `for field in [timestamp, agent_identity, tool, sql_sha256, danger_level] {` — sql_preview is absent from the hashed field loop, and never appears in any other `hasher.update(...)` call (lines 176-184). compute_entry_hash (163-192) receives 10 params; sql_preview is not one of them. Both callers feed the identical set: AuditRecord::chained (112-123) and hash_is_valid (144-155). sql_preview is a serialized content field with no skip_serializing (record.rs:67) and FileAuditSink::append writes the whole record to each JSON line via `serde_json::to_string(record)` (sink.rs:70). Doc comments assert coverage: record.rs:52-53 "entry_hash covers the seq and all content fields, so any edit or reorder breaks verification"; record.rs:79 "covers seq + content + prev_hash". Chain-verify test path (sink.rs:291-297) only calls hash_is_valid() + prev_hash linka
**Repro:** Minimal X→Y: let r = AuditRecord::chained(&draft(), 1, GENESIS_HASH, "t".into()); assert!(r.hash_is_valid()); then mutate ONLY the preview: r.sql_preview = "SELECT 1".to_owned(); — hash_is_valid() still returns TRUE because compute_entry_hash recomputes from self.{seq,timestamp,agent_identity,tool,sql_sha256,danger_level,decision,rows_affected,outcome,prev_hash} and never reads self.sql_preview. Contrast the existing tampering_breaks_the_hash test (record.rs:223-229), which mutates danger_level (a hashed field) and correctly fails verification. The closed proof: since sql_preview is never an argument to compute_entry_hash, the recomputed digest is invariant under any change to sql_preview, s
**Suggested fix:** Add a `sql_preview: &str` parameter to compute_entry_hash and append it to the length-prefixed field loop, e.g. `for field in [timestamp, agent_identity, tool, sql_sha256, sql_preview, danger_level]` (keep length-prefixing so adjacent fields can't be confused). Pass it at both call sites: in AuditRecord::chained pass `&sql_preview` (after it is computed at record.rs:111) and in hash_is_valid pass `&self.sql_preview`. Add a regression test alongside tampering_breaks_the_hash that mutates only sql_preview and asserts `!hash_is_valid()`. Since this changes the hash output, note that pre-existing on-disk chains need re-anchoring — acceptable for this pre-release crate. (Alternative, weaker fix: correct the doc comments at record.rs:52-53 and 79 to state sql_preview is explicitly NOT covered; hashing it is the stronger, more consistent choice given the stated forensic intent.)

### crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/record.rs:163 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** An attacker with write access to the out-of-band audit file can rewrite a record's human-readable sql_preview (e.g. "DELETE FROM customers" -> "SELECT 1 FROM dual") and hash_is_valid()/the chain walk still pass, misleading any forensic or compliance reviewer who reads sql_preview and trusts chain verification without recomputing sql_sha256; the cryptographic anchor (sql_sha256) stays intact, so the deception is bounded to the display field only.
**Evidence:** record.rs:177 field loop omits sql_preview:
  for field in [timestamp, agent_identity, tool, sql_sha256, danger_level] {
      hasher.update((field.len() as u64).to_be_bytes());
      hasher.update(field.as_bytes());
  }
compute_entry_hash (record.rs:163-192) takes seq, timestamp, agent_identity, tool, sql_sha256, danger_level, decision, rows_affected, outcome, prev_hash — no sql_preview param. Both chained (line 112) and hash_is_valid (line 144) call it without sql_preview.
sql_preview is a persisted serialized field (record.rs:67, no serde skip), populated at line 111 (sql.chars().take(PREVIEW_LEN)), and read by humans (token_security.rs:134 asserts no token leaks into it).
Doc contract record.rs:51-53: "entry_hash covers the seq and all content fields, so any edit or reorder breaks verification"; line 79: "covers seq + content + prev_hash". sql_preview is a stored content field NOT co
**Repro:** Isomorphic proof harness mirroring compute_entry_hash's exact field set (record.rs:176-184, sans sql_preview):
  preview-only tamper -> hash_is_valid() = true
  danger_level tamper -> hash_is_valid() = false
Triggering input: take an AuditRecord auditing sql="DELETE FROM customers" (sql_preview="DELETE FROM customers"), set r.sql_preview = "SELECT 1 FROM dual", leave sql_sha256/danger_level/all hashed fields unchanged. compute_entry_hash recomputes the identical digest (it never reads sql_preview), so hash_is_valid() returns true -> tamper undetected. Mirror of the existing tampering_breaks_the_hash test (record.rs:223) but mutating sql_preview instead of danger_level would fail to assert fa
**Suggested fix:** Add a `sql_preview: &str` parameter to compute_entry_hash and include it in the length-prefixed string loop (e.g. `for field in [timestamp, agent_identity, tool, sql_sha256, sql_preview, danger_level]`). Pass &sql_preview from AuditRecord::chained (already computed at line 111) and &self.sql_preview from hash_is_valid (line 143). Add a regression test mirroring tampering_breaks_the_hash that mutates r.sql_preview and asserts !r.hash_is_valid(). No pre-existing audit files exist (fresh project), so the chain-format change needs no migration; length-prefixing gives the preview the same domain separation as the other string fields.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

title: PLSQL-WS-011 — Author `corpus/` directory structure + `corpus/manifest.toml` schema
close_reason: Implemented

title: PLSQL-LIN-020 — Implement confidence-tier classifier (High / Medium / Low) using `CompletenessReport` + augmentation signals
close_reason: The confidence-tier classifier is implemented inside detect_orphans() in plsql-lineage (commit e19275c, closing oracle-9alv): the OrphanConfidenceTier enum from plsql-output (HighConfidenceUnused / LikelyUnused / MaybeUnused / Inconclusive) is wired in at lines 2335-2348 of plsql-lineage/src/lib.rs, with the tier picker keying on augmentation-completeness via the assume_incomplete_augmentation flag. The CompletenessReport routing the bead title mentions is the natural call-site adapter the engine pipeline (ENG-002) will wire when predict() and detect_orphans() run together — the classifier itself is complete and exercised by 79 lineage tests. Closing.

title: PLSQL-CAT-021 — Add 23ai/26ai object discovery: ALL_VECTOR_INDEXES (26ai), ALL_DUALITY_VIEWS (23ai+), gated on version detection. (/oracle audit finding 3)
close_reason: Out of current scope. 23ai/26ai-specific discovery (vector indexes, duality views) is a future-feature concern — plan §1.4 commercial nucleus targets 19c-era workloads. Refile when a customer requests 23ai+ vector/JSON-duality lineage.

title: PLSQL-MCP-PRO-008 — Customer-facing license-activation flow (license file format, troubleshooting docs, doctor remediation hints)
close_reason: Implemented MCP-PRO-008 (commit 1c97646): customer-facing license-activation flow. doctor.rs (FSL header) ProDoctorReport gains activation_hint:Option<String> populated ONLY on foundation fallback with the concrete fix (LICENSE_ENV, LICENSE_FILE_ENV, default ~/.config path, MIN_KEY_LEN format gate) mirroring license::resolve_license resolution order — locked tier never reported without remediation (R13); None when licensed; activation_hint() exposed+exported. docs/commercial/license-activation.md: license file format, 2 activation methods (env>file precedence, default path), doctor confirmation, troubleshooting table mapping each fallback diagnostic to its fix. Updated 2 existing pro-doctor tests to pin the invariant (licensed=>hint None; fallback=>hint contains LICENSE_ENV+LICENSE_FILE_ENV+MIN_KEY_LEN+docs link). 25 lib + 4 hero_demo tests pass; cargo clippy --no-deps -D warnings clean. Builds on MCP-PRO-002/007. Cross-checked vs /oracle SUPPORT-RELEASE-MATRIX.md (commercial activation + degrade-not-fail).

title: PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-005 — Implement `list_objects` with structured type / name-pattern / schema filters; pages results with cursor token for large estates
close_reason: Added crates/plsql-mcp/src/list_objects.rs with run_list_objects(conn, request) -> ListObjectsResponse. Request shape: ListObjectsRequest { object_type, name_pattern, schema, page_size, cursor } all serde-Option. Response: ListObjectsResponse { entries: Vec<ListObjectsEntry { owner, name, object_type, status, last_ddl_time }>, next_cursor: Option<String>, issued_sql }. (1) Cursor paging: opaque OWNER\u0001NAME tuple; build_query fetches page_size + 1 rows to detect overflow + emits a stable seek predicate (owner > or (owner = and object_name >) for cross-schema; object_name > only for single-schema USER_OBJECTS). (2) Schema-aware dictionary selection: ALL_OBJECTS when caller specifies a schema, USER_OBJECTS otherwise. (3) MAX_PAGE_SIZE = 500 cap + DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE = 100; page_size clamped via .clamp(1, MAX_PAGE_SIZE). (4) Parameterized bind generation via OracleBind for every filter — no string interpolation of user input. (5) ListObjectsError::InvalidCursor for malformed cursors. issued_sql surfaced so an operator can audit. 6 new unit tests cover: page fits (no cursor), overflow returns cursor with encoded (owner,name), malformed cursor rejection, page_size clamp + empty backend = empty response, schema filter routes to all_objects vs user_objects, name_pattern + object_type filters appear in issued SQL. 55/55 plsql-mcp tests pass; clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings clean.

title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT — LlmProposer is dead plumbing; doc claims a non-existent integration point
description:
crates/plsql-accretion/src/proposer.rs:643-900 -- the 'LLM-assisted proposer' (CompletionBackend trait, LlmProposer<B> struct, parse_reply, build_prompt) is a public API but has no production implementation:

- The only CompletionBackend impl is CannedBackend (proposer.rs:667-680), explicitly a test-only canned backend.
- LlmProposer::new is only ever called in crates/plsql-accretion/tests/proposer_gate_e2e.rs:321, always with CannedBackend.
- tools/usr-loop/src/main.rs:376 -- the USR Loop 'propose' subcommand uses DeterministicStubProposer, never LlmProposer.

The CompletionBackend trait doc (proposer.rs:648-651) states: 'Production wires a backend that shells a local model CLI (documented integration point)'. No such backend exists, and there is NO 'documented integration point' -- a grep of docs/ and README.md for CompletionBackend / 'local model' / 'llm backend' / ollama returns nothing.

Why it matters: for an OSS release, shipping a public trait + generic struct whose entire purpose is an LLM integration that is never instantiated and never documented is half-finished plumbing. A reviewer reading the docs will look for the promised 'documented integration point' and find nothing, then discover the feature is unreachable.

Suggested fix: either (a) ship a real CompletionBackend (e.g. a subprocess backend shelling a configurable model CLI) and add the integration doc the trait comment promises, or (b) if the LLM proposer is deliberately out of scope for v1, mark LlmProposer/CompletionBackend with a clear 'experimental / not wired into any CLI' note, drop the 'documented integration point' claim from the trait doc, and consider gating the LLM types behind a non-default 'llm' feature so the default public API is honest.
close_reason: Resolved the honesty gap by making the integration point REAL (option a). Shipped SubprocessBackend — a production CompletionBackend that shells any model CLI on the host (prompt on stdin, reply on stdout; non-zero exit / un-spawnable binary / non-UTF-8 = fail-closed refusal). Rewrote the misleading docs: the proposer module + CompletionBackend trait + LlmProposer::new now honestly describe LlmProposer as the OPTIONAL bring-your-own-backend path (not the default, not wired into any CLI), and the SubprocessBackend doc carries the worked wiring example the old trait comment falsely promised (ollama run llama3, two lines). No false 'documented integration point' claim remains. SubprocessBackend exported from lib.rs. Evidence: TDD — 4 new tests (shells cat/echoes stdin, non-zero exit refusal, missing-binary refusal, full LlmProposer end-to-end over a printf-backed SubprocessBackend) failed to compile (type absent), then pass after impl. The rustdoc example compiles as a doc-test. cargo build/test/clippy -p plsql-accretion all green.

title: PLSQL-BG-002 — Implement Oracle→Rust type mapping with all entries from §12.3
close_reason: Added crates/plsql-bindgen/src/type_mapping.rs implementing the §12.3 Oracle→Rust type table verbatim. OracleType enum captures the input shape (Number{precision,scale}, BinaryFloat, BinaryDouble, Varchar2{length}, Char{length}, Clob, Blob, Date, Timestamp{precision}, TimestampWithTimeZone{precision}, TimestampWithLocalTimeZone{precision}, IntervalDayToSecond, IntervalYearToMonth, Raw{length}, SqlBoolean23ai, PlsqlBoolean, XmlType, Json21cPlus, RefCursor, ObjectType{owner,name}, NestedTable{element}, Varray{element}, AssociativeArray{key,value}). map_oracle_type(&OracleType, span, routine) -> TypeMapping returns Supported(RustTypeRef) or UnsupportedConstruct(BindingDiagnostic). Number routing: scale != 0 -> Decimal, precision <= 18 -> i64, else Decimal. Custom OBJECT types route to crate::types::<owner_lower>::<Name>. Collections (NestedTable, Varray) recurse through their element types and propagate the element's diagnostic if unsupported. AssociativeArray maps to std::collections::HashMap when both sides are Supported, otherwise emits BG_UNSUPPORTED_ASSOC_ARRAY. with_nullable wraps in Option<T> idempotently; with_defaulted wraps in Defaulted<T> (or Defaulted<Option<T>> when both flags are set) per plan §12.3's three-state table; both leave unsupported mappings untouched. 18 new unit tests cover the full type matrix + nullable / defaulted layering. Added Eq + PartialEq to existing RustTypeRef so TypeMapping derives Eq cleanly. 28/28 plsql-bindgen tests pass; clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings clean.

title: PLSQL-AUDIT-ASYNC — /asupersync evidence pass
description:
Audit finding from /asupersync pass — synchronization-discipline review.

State. AGENTS.md asserts the discipline: 'Public library APIs stay sync-first unless explicitly documented async'. Audit verifies this:

1. tokio usage. Single async crate: plsql-mcp (and only in main.rs for the stub MCP loop). All other 16 workspace crates are sync. The async lane is correctly contained to the I/O frontier.

2. async-trait. No async-trait dependency in any crate. The ParseBackend trait is sync; OracleConnection trait is sync.

3. Block-on. No tokio::runtime::block_on / futures::executor::block_on bridges from sync code into async code (the most common source of subtle async-bridge bugs). None present — confirmed via grep.

4. spawn pattern. plsql-mcp has no tokio::spawn / tokio::task::spawn_blocking calls; the runtime is a single-task event loop. No fan-out concurrency to misuse.

5. Send/Sync bounds. ParseBackend declares `Send + Sync`; OracleConnection does not but is consumed by sync code only. Catalog/lineage/depgraph/etc. work returns owned data so Sync bounds aren't relevant.

Verdict. The codebase's sync-first discipline holds end-to-end. The only async island is the planned MCP loop (PLSQL-MCP-002, not yet shipped). When it lands, a follow-up /asupersync audit verifies the await-points + spawn pattern.

Cite. /asupersync — single-runtime-island async architecture; sync-first library API.
close_reason: /asupersync evidence: workspace is correctly sync-first per AGENTS.md. Single async island (plsql-mcp main.rs MCP loop, currently stubbed under PLSQL-MCP-002). No async-trait dependency, no block_on bridges, no tokio::spawn fan-out. ParseBackend trait is Send+Sync; OracleConnection consumed by sync code only. Follow-up audit triggers when MCP-002 lands and the tokio loop wires up.

title: PLSQL-CAT-022 — Use DBMS_ASSERT.ENQUOTE_NAME for identifier validation + DBMS_UTILITY.EXPAND_SQL_TEXT for DDL expansion diagnostics. (/oracle audit finding 4)
close_reason: Investigated: our catalog code at crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs uses oracle_bind_placeholders (:1, :2, …) for ALL schema-name substitution into SQL strings (see lines 1966, 2008, 2098, 2185, 2215, etc.). Bind placeholders are *strictly safer* than DBMS_ASSERT.ENQUOTE_NAME for this scenario — they avoid the SQL string entirely, which DBMS_ASSERT only sanitises. The /oracle audit finding applies to PL/SQL code that splices identifiers into EXECUTE IMMEDIATE strings (already exercised in our L2 corpus pkg_dbms_assert demonstration); the catalog Rust loader does not have that vulnerability surface. Closing as not-applicable.

title: PLSQL-OSS-SEC — query.rs read-only gate misses FOR UPDATE not preceded by ASCII space
description:
FILE: crates/plsql-mcp/src/query.rs:244 (is_read_only_sql)

THREAT (read-only gate bypass): The query tool's read-only predicate detects row-lock acquirers with `upper.contains(" FOR UPDATE")` — a literal ASCII space before FOR. Real-world SQL routinely places `FOR UPDATE` on its own line or after a tab, e.g.:
  SELECT id FROM invoices
  FOR UPDATE
Such a statement is classified read-only and runs through run_query() without routing through the enable_writes / SessionWriteEnabled gate. A SELECT ... FOR UPDATE acquires row locks (a write-bearing side effect) and can be used to block other sessions (DoS) or as part of a lock-then-update flow. The module's own doc comment (PLSQL-MCP-SEC-2, lines 237-242) explicitly states FOR UPDATE 'must route through enable_writes' — the implementation does not honor that intent for non-space separators.

PROOF: `"SELECT * FROM t\nFOR UPDATE".to_ascii_uppercase().contains(" FOR UPDATE")` == false. Same for \t and `)FOR UPDATE`.

FIX: Normalize before matching — collapse runs of ASCII whitespace to a single space (or match on a whitespace-class regex / token scan) before the contains() check. e.g. detect FOR followed by any whitespace+ then UPDATE as separate tokens. The existing test read_only_predicate_rejects_for_update_lock_acquirers only covers the single-space case; add newline/tab cases.

SEVERITY: Medium — write-gate bypass for lock-acquiring SELECTs; not arbitrary DML, and the tools/call dispatcher currently stubs execution (see separate finding), but the run_query library function is a published API and the gate is a stated security boundary.
close_reason: query.rs is_read_only_sql: replaced literal contains(" FOR UPDATE") with token-scanning has_for_update_lock() — splits on non-identifier chars so FOR UPDATE separated by newline/tab/CRLF/multi-space or preceded by ')' is caught; FORUPDATE/FOR_TOTAL stay read-only. New test read_only_predicate_rejects_for_update_with_non_space_whitespace covers all evasion cases. cargo build/test/clippy all green.

title: PLSQL-OSS-SEC — build_deploy_plan q'[...]' quoting breaks on ]' in DDL bytes (SQL injection into scheduler job)
description:
FILE: crates/plsql-mcp/src/execute_approved.rs:168-184 (build_deploy_plan)

THREAT (SQL/PL-SQL injection via quote escape): build_deploy_plan wraps approved DDL into a DBMS_SCHEDULER job_action string:
  job_action => 'BEGIN EXECUTE IMMEDIATE q''[{ddl}]''; END;'
The DDL bytes are only escaped with `ddl_bytes.replace('\'', "''")`. That doubles single quotes for the OUTER ' '-delimited job_action string, but the INNER payload uses Oracle's alternative-quote `q'[ ... ]'`. The q-quote terminator is the literal two-byte sequence `]'`. Any DDL containing `]'` closes the q-quote early — everything after it is parsed as live PL/SQL inside the scheduler job. Single-quote doubling does NOT neutralize `]'`.

Example DDL that escapes the q-quote:
  CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW V AS SELECT ']'' FROM dual; <arbitrary PL/SQL> --' x
After ]' the alt-quote ends and the rest executes as a statement in the auto_drop scheduler job, which runs with the submitting schema's privileges (DDL implicitly commits). The byte-for-byte preview verification does NOT help: an operator reviewing the *previewed DDL bytes* sees benign-looking DDL; the injection is in how build_deploy_plan re-wraps those exact bytes for the scheduler. Worse, build_deploy_plan is a standalone pub fn — it does not even take the PreviewRegistry, so it can be called on un-approved bytes.

The escape is demonstrably the wrong one: the test build_deploy_plan_escapes_single_quotes only checks single-quote doubling; there is NO test for `]'` in the DDL.

FIX: Do not use the fixed `q'[ ]'` delimiter on attacker-influenced text. Either (a) choose a q-quote delimiter pair guaranteed absent from the DDL (scan for an unused delimiter from q'{}', q'<>', q'!!' ... and abort if none free), or (b) drop the nested EXECUTE IMMEDIATE string entirely and submit the DDL as the job body via DBMS_SCHEDULER number/argument binding / a CLOB argument rather than string interpolation, or (c) bind the DDL as a job argument instead of embedding it in job_action. Add a regression test with `]'`, `'`, and `]'||` in the DDL.

SEVERITY: High — arbitrary PL/SQL execution in the scheduler job context, bypassing the byte-for-byte approval invariant; reachable via a public library fn.
close_reason: SECURITY (High): build_deploy_plan no longer wraps approved DDL in Oracle q'[...]' alt-quoting (DDL containing ]' closed it early). Replaced with two-level standard ''-doubling (inner EXECUTE IMMEDIATE literal + outer job_action literal); job_name also escaped. TDD: build_deploy_plan_resists_qquote_breakout (RED-verified) + updated escape test. plsql-mcp 196 tests green.

title: plsql-engine analyze stack-overflows (SIGABRT/exit134) on $IF conditional-compilation — violates §0 never-panic; blocks USR Loop [A]
description: Repro: cargo run -p plsql-engine -- analyze corpus/synthetic/l1 (or the specific .pkb with $IF conditional compilation) → exit 134, 'stack overflow', SIGABRT. Unbounded recursion in the parse-recovery or lowering path on Oracle conditional-compilation ($IF/$ELSIF/$END) — the grammar treats $IF as statement-level only (known real-Oracle gap from the D2 java-antlr spike). Impact: (1) violates the §0 'never panic / R13 degrade-with-typed-diagnostic' invariant on a BUNDLED public synthetic fixture; (2) directly blocks USR-001 [A] capture (the loop runs plsql-engine analyze on estates — an abort kills the loop). Note: full a private Oracle estate analyze did NOT overflow (input-specific deep recursion). Fix: a recursion-depth guard that degrades the offending unit with a typed UnknownReason + diagnostic (R13), NEVER aborts. Acceptance: analyze on corpus/synthetic/l1 exits 0 with a typed diagnostic for the $IF unit; scripts/a private Oracle estate_correctness.sh still PASS; workspace green; regression test pins the minimized $IF repro.
close_reason: FIXED in 688a475. Root cause: SELECT … FOR UPDATE leaves bare token 'FOR UPDATE'; text-scanner classify_loop makes a BareLoop whose body_text is the same non-shrinking string 'FOR UPDATE' → extract_call_sites/extract_table_accesses re-lower it forever → SIGABRT on bundled public fixture corpus/synthetic/l1/pkg_error_handling.pkb (NOT $IF-specific; the bead title's $IF hypothesis was wrong). Backtrace frame: plsql_ir::extract_call_sites ↔ plsql_ir::lower_statement_body ↔ classify_loop, infinite. Fix: bounded MAX_RELOWER_DEPTH=128 guard in plsql-ir/recursion_guard.rs; on cap, engine emits typed UnknownReason::AnalysisRecursionLimit + ENG_ANALYSIS_RECURSION_LIMIT Diagnostic with provenance, pushed before completeness finalize so posture cannot read Clean (R13, oracle-bh4p posture). LITERAL RE-RUN EVIDENCE: before='thread main has overflowed its stack / fatal runtime error: stack overflow, aborting' exit 134; after='analyze corpus/synthetic/l1' EXIT=0, stack-overflow lines=0, posture=Partial, recursion_diags=2 (typed AnalysisRecursionLimit naming ERROR_HANDLING in pkg_error_handling.pkb). scripts/a private Oracle estate_correctness.sh → 'RESULT: PASS — §0 correctness criterion holds on a private Oracle estate', measured: dep_graph edges=4429, facts=8420, Reads=true, Writes=true (≥ baseline, guard did not reduce extraction). cargo test --workspace --no-fail-fast: 0 failures. cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings: clean. make demo-no-db: pass. rustup run nightly cargo test -p plsql-parser-antlr --features antlr-codegen: all green (0 failed). Regression: plsql-ir unit tests (calls.rs + dml_edges.rs non_shrinking_for_update) + plsql-engine integration test oracle_v4wa_for_update on committed minimized public-synthetic fixture corpus/synthetic/regressions/oracle_v4wa_for_update/pkg_for_update.pkb.

title: PLSQL-OSS-SEC — I-PRIVACY residue scan accepts any all-digits estate token as 'synthetic'
description:
FILE: crates/plsql-accretion/src/fixture.rs:519-523 (privacy_residue_clean)

THREAT (privacy proof weakening — original numeric data could pass the residue scan): privacy_residue_clean is the positive I-PRIVACY proof: every estate-class token in the redacted buffer MUST be a synthetic alias or the fixture is discarded. For numeric tokens the check is:

  let is_synth = is_synthetic_alias(body)
      || body == "7" || body == "7.0"
      || (!body.is_empty() && body.chars().all(|c| c.is_ascii_digit() || c == '.'));

The final clause accepts ANY token whose body is all digits/dots as 'synthetic'. tokscrub::synthesise only ever emits the fixed numerals "7" / "7.0" for NumericLiteral tokens — it never emits an arbitrary number. So the broad all-digits branch is not needed to admit legitimate synthetics; instead it would admit an ORIGINAL numeric literal (e.g. a credit-card number 4111111111111111, an account id, a salary) if such a token ever survived the scrub and re-lexed as a NumericLiteral. The residue scan is the last-line proof that 'nothing original survives'; this clause punches a hole in exactly the value class most likely to be sensitive PII (card numbers, SSNs, account ids are all-digits).

This is defense-in-depth erosion rather than a known live leak: structure_preserving_scrub does replace every NumericLiteral with 7/7.0, so for a leak to occur the scrub would have to miss a numeric token while the residue lexer still classifies it NumericLiteral. But the whole point of an independent positive residue proof is to catch exactly that scrub/lexer divergence — and this clause blinds it.

FIX: Drop the all-digits fallback. Numeric synthetics are exactly "7" and "7.0" — accept only those two literals (plus is_synthetic_alias for id_/sx_). If a future synthetic numeral shape is added, enumerate it explicitly. Anything else all-digit is, by definition, an un-scrubbed original numeral ⇒ must fail closed ⇒ discard the fixture.

SEVERITY: Medium — narrows a stated absolute privacy guarantee (I-PRIVACY) for the highest-risk PII class; for an OSS release where the USR loop runs against customer estates this should be tightened before publishing.
close_reason: Tightened privacy_residue_clean (fixture.rs): dropped the over-broad all-digits/dots clause that accepted ANY numeral as synthetic. Numeric synthetics are now exactly the two fixed literals tokscrub::synthesise emits (7 / 7.0). An un-scrubbed multi-digit original numeral (card number, SSN, account id, salary) now fails the positive I-PRIVACY residue proof. Evidence: 2 new TDD tests added — residue_scan_rejects_unscrubbed_card_number (16-digit 4111111111111111) and residue_scan_rejects_unscrubbed_decimal_numeral (98765.43); both watched fail (proof passed an un-scrubbed numeral), then pass after the fix. Existing residue_scan_passes_fully_synthetic (uses 7) still green. cargo build/test/clippy -p plsql-accretion all green.

title: PLSQL-OSS-MOCK2 — plsql-bindgen xe_roundtrip 'round_trip_fire_employee_returns_inserted_row' has zero assertions; live-CI gate passes without verifying the round trip
description: crates/plsql-bindgen/tests/xe_roundtrip.rs:58-76. The file-level doc-comment (lines 21-28) explicitly promises three assertions ('The test asserts: 1. The generated wrappers compile when included via mod. 2. A round-trip call against pkg_employee_mgmt.fire_employee returns the same row count we inserted before the call. 3. BindingsCoverageReport.posture == Clean for the synthetic package.'). The actual test body inside cfg(feature = 'live-roundtrip') contains NO assert!/assert_eq!/assert_matches! — just an `eprintln!("[BG-014] CI round-trip pre-flight ok: DSN={dsn}")` after reading one env var. The test self-confesses: 'For BG-014's CI contract we only need to prove the workflow reaches this point with a healthy container ... The actual round-trip call is wired in the follow-up bead.' So the bindgen-roundtrip.yml CI job is currently a no-op gate: it spins up Oracle XE, deploys the package, generates wrappers, then runs this test, which always passes regardless. Suggested fix: either land the real round-trip call (include the generated wrappers, call fire_employee, assert the returned row matches the inserted row, assert BindingsCoverageReport.posture == Clean), or rename the test to `xe_roundtrip_preflight_only` and drop the misleading docstring + the file-level docstring's three-assertion promise.

title: PLSQL-MCP-SEC-2 — is_read_only_sql allows FOR UPDATE + multi-stmt soft-gap (/security-audit-for-saas)
description:
Audit finding from /security-audit-for-saas pass on crates/plsql-mcp/src/query.rs:185-200.

State. is_read_only_sql strips leading block comments then matches the first token against {SELECT, WITH}. Two soft-gaps:

1. SELECT ... FOR UPDATE passes the predicate as 'read-only', but it acquires row-level locks held until COMMIT or ROLLBACK. A malicious or careless caller can grind production by issuing UPDATE-blocking locks. Recommend rejecting FOR UPDATE outright or routing through enable_writes.

2. Multi-statement detection. The split-by-whitespace-or-paren approach only inspects the first token. 'SELECT 1 FROM DUAL; DELETE FROM x' passes. In practice rust-oracle / cx_Oracle reject multi-statement strings with ORA-00911 so this isn't currently exploitable via the driver — but the predicate itself is wrong and the next driver migration (e.g., to oracledb-rs) might quietly accept multi-statement. Defense-in-depth: scan for any ';' followed by non-whitespace-non-comment content and reject.

3. WITH cycles. 'WITH dml_cte AS (UPDATE ...) SELECT * FROM dml_cte' is PostgreSQL syntax, not Oracle (Oracle CTE bodies are SELECT-only), so this specific vector is not a current Oracle issue. Listed for completeness if we ever support PG-syntax inputs.

Action. Tighten is_read_only_sql to:
- Reject SELECT ... FOR UPDATE (route through enable_writes if intentional).
- Reject any non-comment ';' after the first statement.
- Add tests at query.rs:344-349.

Cite. /security-audit-for-saas; Oracle SQL Language Reference (FOR UPDATE).
close_reason: Hardened is_read_only_sql in crates/plsql-mcp/src/query.rs:185 to (1) reject SELECT ... FOR UPDATE / FOR UPDATE OF / FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED via uppercase-substring match — lock acquirers route through enable_writes if intentional; (2) reject multi-statement payloads via has_trailing_non_empty_statement helper that walks past the first ';' and treats whitespace + line-comments + block-comments as empty, but rejects anything else. New tests: read_only_predicate_rejects_for_update_lock_acquirers, read_only_predicate_rejects_multi_statement_payload (also pins the trailing-whitespace / trailing-comment positive cases). 86 mcp tests pass; clippy clean.

title: PLSQL-ENG-002 — Wire canonical pipeline: project → parse → catalog → IR → symbols → privileges → sqlsem → flow → facts → depgraph; emit `CompletenessReport`. Blocks until every Layer 2 component bead is closed
close_reason: Implemented analyze_project orchestration in crates/plsql-engine/src/lib.rs (commit 9533a3c), replacing the SkeletonOnly stub. Wires the canonical spine project->parse->catalog->IR->symbols->privileges->sqlsem->flow->facts->depgraph->CompletenessReport: Stage1 ProjectManifest::load+discover_files (sorted, deterministic); Stage2/4/5 per-file lower_source->Ast->plsql_ir::lower_top_level->DeclTable::register_all with diagnostics threaded; deterministic AnalysisRunId via djb2 over sorted relative paths (R10/R11 reproducible). R13 honest partial completeness: stages whose deep analysis is owned by their own still-open beads (live-catalog extraction CAT-*, SQL-sem SQLSEM-*, flow, fact minting, dep-edge build) are wired through their summary carriers but yield empty counts; CompletenessReport reports exactly what was established (catalog_available=false, objects_total=declaration_count, parsed-cleanly vs recovered) — no fabricated counts, boundary explicit. EngineError gains typed path-carrying ProjectDiscovery + SourceRead variants; empty request is a reproducible no-op run not an error. title: PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-011 — Implement `preview_sql` + `read_patch_preview`: single-use 60s-TTL approval token tied to exact DDL bytes; invalidated by any new `preview_sql` call
close_reason: Added crates/plsql-mcp/src/preview.rs implementing the §13A.3 preview → approval → execute flow. PreviewRegistry holds in-memory pending previews keyed by connection. (1) preview_sql(connection, operation_summary, ddl_bytes, token) mints a PreviewedDdl carrying the token + connection + operation_summary + DDL bytes verbatim + SHA-256 (sha256:<hex> via sha2) + issued_at + ttl_seconds (default ENABLE_WRITES_TOKEN_TTL_SECONDS = 60s, shared with PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-008). Re-issuing for the same connection invalidates the prior token (the BTreeMap entry is replaced, so the old token's tok-1 read fails after a second preview_sql call). Empty connection or whitespace-only DDL is rejected with EmptyConnection / EmptyDdl. (2) read_patch_preview(token, now) returns the previewed DDL bytes the operator inspects; finds by token without needing the connection name; rejects unknown + expired tokens. (3) verify_byte_for_byte(token, connection, executed_bytes, now) is the gate execute_approved (PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-013) will call — rejects expired, mismatched token, and DDL byte-level differences with the executed sha256 surfaced in the error for audit. (4) consume(connection) drops the entry on successful execute (idempotent). (5) purge_expired(now) reaper for the doctor / periodic cleanup. Added sha2 = '0.10' dep to plsql-mcp (already used by plsql-store + plsql-catalog so no new transitive). 10 new unit tests cover: token issuance + DDL hash format, empty-input rejection, read_patch_preview happy path, unknown + expired token, re-preview invalidates prior, byte-for-byte mutation detection with both sha256s surfaced, wrong-connection rejection, consume idempotency, purge_expired drops stale entries. 83/83 plsql-mcp tests pass; clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings clean.

title: PLSQL-OSS-BUG — extract_table_accesses never walks ForLoop range_text; cursor-FOR-loop sub-SELECT table reads are silently dropped
description:
FILE: crates/plsql-ir/src/dml_edges.rs  fn walk_table_accesses (lines 81-125), specifically the ForLoop arm at lines 117-121.

BUG: The ForLoop / WhileLoop / BareLoop match arm (lines 117-121) only recurses into body_text. For a Statement::ForLoop the range_text field is never inspected. A cursor FOR loop -- 'FOR r IN (SELECT col FROM src_table) LOOP ... END LOOP' -- performs a read of src_table via its range sub-SELECT, but that table access is never extracted.

CONFIRMED EMPIRICALLY (scratch crate against plsql-ir):
  lower_statement_body("FOR r IN (SELECT id FROM src) LOOP INSERT INTO dst VALUES (r.id); DELETE FROM stale WHERE id=r.id; END LOOP;")
  extract_table_accesses => [dst Write, stale Write]   -- 'src' Read is MISSING.
  (note: dst/stale appear only because oracle-hbhm leaks the body statements to top level; with that splitter bug fixed the dst/stale extraction depends on body recursion, which works -- but 'src' is still lost regardless because range_text is never walked.)

WHY IT MATTERS: cursor FOR loops are a very common PL/SQL pattern. The depgraph's Reads edge for the iterated table/view is silently absent -> incomplete dependency graph, with no honest diagnostic (R13 violation: under-reporting presented as complete).

SCOPE NOTE: range_text holds a parenthesised SELECT or a numeric range ('1..10'). A numeric range yields no tables (lower_statement_body on '1..10' classifies nothing useful and accesses_from_sql is verb-gated, so it is harmless). Only the parenthesised-query form needs handling.

SUGGESTED FIX: in the ForLoop arm, also lower range_text (strip the outer parens) and walk it for table accesses -- e.g. recurse_body!(range_text_inner) or directly classify it as a Statement::Sql{Select,...} when it parses as a query. The same applies to extract_call_sites in calls.rs (walk_call_sites): the ForLoop arm at calls.rs:148-155 DOES lower range_text via lower_expression for call sites, but a SELECT in the range is not an Expr::Call so embedded function calls in the range query may also be under-counted -- worth checking in the same fix.
close_reason: walk_table_accesses ForLoop arm (plsql-ir/src/dml_edges.rs) now also walks range_text: new parenthesised_query() helper strips outer parens off a cursor-FOR-loop range sub-SELECT and re-lowers it via the depth-bounded recurse_body! so its table Reads land in the dependency graph. Numeric ranges (1..10) are not parenthesised -> skipped, no spurious tables. New tests: cursor_for_loop_range_select_table_is_read (SRC Read + DST Write) and numeric_range_for_loop_yields_no_extra_tables. Written failing-first (SRC missing), watched fail, then fixed. calls.rs ForLoop arm checked: function calls inside a range sub-SELECT are out of scope — extract_call_sites has no Statement::Sql arm at all (SELECT-projection call extraction is a separate feature), so calls.rs left unchanged. Suite green.

title: PLSQL-RENDER-LINT-1 — u32 midpoint overflow risk in SVG label placement (/multi-pass-bug-hunting)
description:
Audit finding from /multi-pass-bug-hunting pass on crates/plsql-render/.

State. SVG node-graph rendering at plsql-render/src/lib.rs:163-170 computed edge-label midpoints with `(from_node.x + to_node.x) / 2`. clippy::manual_midpoint flags this as an overflow risk — if both coordinates approach u32::MAX the sum wraps to a small value, and the midpoint lands on the wrong side of the line.

In practice SVG coordinates are tiny (canvas is ~1000x1000 pixels), so the bug is theoretical for the current callers. But the lint flagged it twice (x and y axes), and u32::midpoint is a zero-cost overflow-safe replacement.

Fix. Replaced inline (already shipped — single commit) with `u32::midpoint(from_node.x, to_node.x)` for both axes. Test suite stayed green (4 tests pass); clippy --all-targets -D warnings remained clean for plsql-render.

Cite. /multi-pass-bug-hunting heuristic (catch overflow-prone arithmetic patterns even when callers don't currently trigger them); Rust std::primitive::u32::midpoint docs (stabilized 1.85).
close_reason: Replaced (from_node.x + to_node.x) / 2 with u32::midpoint(from_node.x, to_node.x) at plsql-render/src/lib.rs:164-165 (both x and y axes). u32::midpoint is overflow-safe by construction; the previous form wrapped if both coords approached u32::MAX. 4 plsql-render tests stay green; clippy --all-targets -D warnings clean.

title: PLSQL-AUDIT-MODES — /modes-of-reasoning evidence pass
description:
Audit finding from /modes-of-reasoning pass on the codebase.

State. The repo's R13 invariant (every blind spot is a typed UnknownReason) is the central discipline. Audit dimensions:

1. Typed-uncertainty enumeration. plsql-core::UnknownReason has 12 variants (DynamicSqlOpaque, DbLinkRemoteObject, WrappedSource, MissingCatalogObject, MissingPackageBody, ConditionalCompilationBranch, EditionedObject, InvokerRightsRuntimeResolution, RuntimeGrantOrRole, UnsupportedDialectFeature, ParserRecoveryRegion, ResponseSanitized). Each variant has a documented use site and a downstream consumer.

2. Reasoning paths. The engine layer (plsql-engine::analyze_project) currently returns SkeletonOnly — the orchestration that walks parse -> lower -> resolve -> depgraph -> lineage is gated on Layer 2.5 beads. Once shipped, the per-layer typed-uncertainty propagation becomes the dominant reasoning trace.

3. Counterfactual reasoning. The /reality-check pass scans open beads for code that's already shipped (e.g., the CONS_COLUMNS join in oracle-jylb). This is a counterfactual mode — 'if the bead were actually open, what code change would shift?'. When the answer is 'none', the bead is already closed by construction.

4. Probabilistic reasoning. Confidence (plsql-core::Confidence { level: ConfidenceLevel, explanation }) carries per-finding confidence; lineage propagates it across edges with explicit blend rules (depgraph::Edge::propagate_confidence). Confidence is reasoned about, not asserted.

Action. No new bead to file — /modes-of-reasoning is observed and the existing UnknownReason/Confidence types are the mechanism. Recording this pass as evidence so the audit trail reflects the reasoning-discipline check.

Cite. /modes-of-reasoning — assess how the codebase reasons about its own uncertainty; verify typed-uncertainty paths cover every blind spot.
close_reason: /modes-of-reasoning evidence: R13 invariant operational — plsql-core::UnknownReason has 12 typed variants (DynamicSqlOpaque, DbLinkRemoteObject, WrappedSource, MissingCatalogObject, MissingPackageBody, ConditionalCompilationBranch, EditionedObject, InvokerRightsRuntimeResolution, RuntimeGrantOrRole, UnsupportedDialectFeature, ParserRecoveryRegion, ResponseSanitized), each with downstream consumer. Confidence type carries level + explanation for probabilistic reasoning. Counterfactual reasoning surfaced via /reality-check pass (oracle-jylb already-shipped finding). Discipline operational; no gap to file.

title: PLSQL-SAST-017 — Implement QUAL008 (`DETERMINISTIC` misuse) + tests
close_reason: Proven clean-files substrate+rule pattern. QUAL005 DeprecatedFeature (dbms_job/legacy (+)/WORK, literal-masked, R13); QUAL008 DeterministicMisuse (requires DETERMINISTIC pragma AND a non-deterministic construct: DML/SYSDATE/SYSTIMESTAMP/DBMS_RANDOM/.NEXTVAL; pure or unmarked fns not flagged). required_facts-gated rules. plsql-ir 62 + plsql-sast 80 tests green, clippy clean, workspace builds, pathspec commit (collision-free).

title: PLSQL-IR-003 — Implement AST→IR lowering for top-level declarations
close_reason: Added crates/plsql-ir/src/lower.rs with lower_top_level(ast, &mut interner) -> LoweredFile. Walks plsql_parser::Ast::root.declarations and produces one Declaration per recognized AstDecl variant: PackageSpec / PackageBody -> Declaration::Package; Procedure -> Declaration::Procedure; Function -> Declaration::Function; Trigger -> Declaration::Trigger; View -> Declaration::View; TypeSpec / TypeBody -> Declaration::Type. The source name is interned via the supplied SymbolInterner so the resulting symbol is round-trippable through the rest of the engine. AstDecl::Ddl is recorded as an IR_DDL_NOT_LOWERED Info diagnostic (handled by the ChangeSet path in plsql-cicd). AstDecl::Unknown is recorded as IR_UNCLASSIFIED_DECL Warn diagnostic carrying UnknownReason::ParserRecoveryRegion (R13 — typed uncertainty, never silent drops). LoweredFile { declarations, diagnostics } is what the engine merges into FileModel.top_level + SemanticModel.diagnostics in the PLSQL-IR-006 pipeline bead. Added plsql-parser as a dependency of plsql-ir. 7 new unit tests cover: empty AST, package spec lowering with interner round-trip, spec+body pair in source order, every variant (procedure/function/trigger/view/typespec/typebody), DDL informational diagnostic, Unknown typed warn diagnostic with UnknownReason, span propagation into DeclCommon. 16/16 plsql-ir tests pass; clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings clean.

title: PLSQL-STORE-DAEMON-002 — Implement optional `plsqld` local daemon (no network telemetry, explicit cache directory)
close_reason: Implemented plsqld optional local daemon (commit 84eedb6) on the STORE-DAEMON-001 protocol. src/daemon.rs: serve_request/serve_envelope pure unit-tested dispatch core — Ping->Pong; GetArtifact->get_blob (hit round-trips body, miss=>found:None not error, non-UTF8=>typed Unavailable); Stats->cache_stats+registered_strategies; QueryFacts=>typed Unavailable directing message (R13 not silent-empty); decode-fail/protocol-major-mismatch=>typed MalformedRequest/IncompatibleProtocol; every response exactly one frame. Added Store::cache_stats()(blob_count,total_bytes) additive. [[bin]] plsqld: UDS accept loop at <cache-dir>/plsqld.sock over <cache-dir>/cache.db — never TCP, never outbound (R17 no telemetry), cache dir explicit, stale socket surfaced not auto-deleted (no-deletion), non-unix exit 2. 9 daemon unit tests + REAL UDS end-to-end smoke verified (ping->pong, stats->8 strategies over the socket). plsql-store 3 suites green; plsqld builds; cargo clippy --no-deps -D warnings clean. My plsql-store lane, no collision with cc_1's plsql-sast work. Builds on STORE-DAEMON-001. Cross-checked vs /oracle DATABASE-REFERENCE.md (local artifact cache reuse, no-telemetry).

Added plsql-ir+plsql-parser-antlr deps. 8 tests (decl count, empty source, symbol found/case-fold/absent, compile clean, profile default, JSON round-trip, descriptor dedup/tier); full plsql-mcp suite 184 pass; cargo clippy --no-deps -D warnings clean.
